Pick up Chance and Community Chest cards for unexpected prizes and penalties, but watch out for those tax spaces! The Main Line is historic in its own way, but there is no better way to get a real representation of the area than by playing Main Line MONOPOLY.

Get ready to rhyme with this ultimate game for Dr. Seuss fans! MONOPOLY: Dr. Seuss lets generations of fans relive their favorite childhood books as they buy, sell, and trade unforgettable titles from the author’s collection, such as The Cat in the Hat, Green Eggs and Ham, and The Lorax! Set up Libraries and Book Stores to secure your valuable works as you travel the board with tokens of iconic story objects. Stack up Dr. Seuss character-faced currency and be the last player with cash to win!

—description from the publisher

Help the Na'vi defend Pandora against the RDA! In this Monopoly: Avatar Edition board game, players travel around the gameboard and connect to as many locations as they can to save Pandora from the greedy humans. With each connection players make, their bond to Eywa grows stronger. Draw Na'vi and RDA SecOps cards, collect woodsprites, and protect the land from the evil RDA Scorpion. The player with the most woodsprites when all locations have been connected wins—and restores the land! This beautiful Avatar game for ages 8 and up is a fun board game for Family Game Night, and it makes a great gift for fans of Avatar and the Monopoly board game!

—description from the publisher

Champurrado is a promotional card for the base game Coffee Rush, distributed in Mexico.

This promotional card adds a new drink inspired by a traditional Mexican beverage. The promo card is treated as a standard order and follows the normal rules of the game.

Components:

1 card (43×67 mm):

• 1 MEXICO Country Order card (Champurrado);

Ingredients = Caramel, Chocolate, Milk, Steam)

• 1 Title/Rules card

Zumoffa, the Lord of Night, has lured critters of the wild into a great maze to dine on them before escaping through the Midnight gate. Cursed, Zumoffa can't eat his victims while they sleep, but they can't fall asleep if they are hungry, need a bedtime story read to them, or need to snuggle.

As a Midnight Guardian, you alone stand between the innocent critters of Shady Valley and Zumoffa. Meet the needs of all of the critters by Midnight to win the game. You have 7 rounds and a maze to navigate through. Take a wrong turn, and it will slow you down.

Along the way, you'll find many wonderful items to help you in this mad dash to rescue the critters. You will also find special locations such as a magical fountain, as well as allies and enemies, too. Play Solo, Co-op, or the variant Game Modes: Free-For-All, or Teams.

Midnight Showdown Battle Game
This game comes free with 7 To Midnight as an "epilogue" to the story. It can be played as part of 7 To Midnight or as a stand-alone game that explores the story further, enabling you to battle Zumoffa, attempt to seal his gate so he can't escape, and rescue his captive.

Do you have what it takes to defeat the Lord of Night?

7 To Midnight and Midnight Showdown are being released in both Print and Play (PnP) and Print editions.

—description from the publisher

By the early 1990s, the global technology industry was changing fast; computing moved beyond machines into software, services, and global networks. Indian technocrats and entrepreneurs recognized that while hardware was limited, talent was not. Software could travel where machines could not, and disciplined execution could compete with scale. Working within constraints, they pushed for fewer controls and wider global access. With institutions like NASSCOM giving the industry structure and voice, Indian IT firms earned trust step by step and began to lead the IT industry in the world.

In Tycoon: High-Tech 1991, players take on the role of engineers leading emerging technology companies based in key Indian cities, expanding their reach into global markets across APAC, EMEA, and North America. Players compete by bidding for major international projects, managing and training a growing workforce, and scaling their companies across the four pillars of high technology: Systems, Applications, Data, and Networks. The game ends with the Tech Expo, and players compete to win the most prestigious awards, to become - The Tycoon.

—description from the designer

Don Quixote sets out in pursuit of noble ideals, charging ahead with passion and conviction.
Yet not every victory brings glory, and blind ambition can easily lead to folly.
In this game, winning a trick is often a risk rather than a reward. Try to avoid taking tricks whenever you can. If you cannot, take exactly what is required — no more, no less. And it is wiser to claim a trick that holds points.
Careful judgment, not reckless triumph, is the key to success.

—description from the publisher

Welcome to the colorful world of Mumi, Kuki and Tidi!

These three friends love playing with numbers. The game “muti kuti” is their biggest fun. If you would like to join them, here are the rules:

     add up the numbers
     add up the characters
     multiply numbers and characters of the same color on the revealed cards.


That’s all! Have fun!

—description from the back of the box

In a kingdom where magic and intrigue intertwine, use strategy to fulfill your destiny!

Immerse yourself in the enchanting world of Terres de Destinées, a card game combining bidding, strategy, and collecting. In this kingdom where magic and intrigue intertwine, each player must forge their own path and fulfill their destiny. It's up to you to anticipate, combine your cards with cunning, and make the most of your powers to outsmart your opponents.

Accessible and quick to learn, Lands of Destiny is ideal for both casual players and board game enthusiasts looking for replayability and tactical depth.

Why choose Lands of Destiny?

     A clever card game with simple and accessible rules.
     Dynamic auctions for constant interaction between players.
     Infinite card combinations guaranteeing high replayability.
     A family-friendly and competitive gaming experience, ideal for your game nights.


How to play?

     Check the game objectives and establish your strategy.
     Participate in auctions to obtain essential cards.
     Activate your cards' powers and turn the game around to your advantage.
     Be the first to achieve an objective to win the game!


If you enjoy fantasy card games, interactive bidding, and strategic collecting in the style of Fantasy Realms, then Lands of Destiny will quickly find a place in your game collection. Perfect for playing with family or friends, it combines accessibility and subtlety in a compact format.

—description from the publisher

Dans un royaume où magie et complots s’entrelacent, usez de stratégie pour accomplir votre destinée !

Plongez dans l’univers envoûtant de Terres de Destinées, un jeu de cartes mêlant enchères, stratégie et collection. Dans ce royaume où la magie et les complots s’entrelacent, chaque joueur doit tracer son propre chemin et accomplir sa destinée. À vous d’anticiper, de combiner vos cartes avec ruse et de tirer le meilleur parti des pouvoirs pour surpasser vos adversaires.

Accessible et rapide à prendre en main, Terres de Destinées s’adresse aussi bien aux joueurs occasionnels qu’aux passionnés de jeux de société en quête de rejouabilité et de profondeur tactique.

Pourquoi choisir Terres de Destinées ?

     Un jeu de cartes malin aux règles simples et accessibles.
     Des enchères dynamiques pour une interaction constante entre les joueurs.
     Des combinaisons de cartes infinies garantissant une forte rejouabilité.
     Une expérience de jeu familiale et compétitive, idéale pour vos soirées ludiques.


Comment jouer ?

     Consultez les objectifs de la partie et établissez votre stratégie.
     Participez aux enchères pour obtenir les cartes essentielles.
     Activez les pouvoirs de vos cartes et renversez la partie à votre avantage.
     Soyez le premier à réaliser un objectif pour remporter la victoire !


Si vous aimez les jeux de cartes fantastiques, les enchères interactives et la collection stratégique à la manière de Fantasy Realms, alors Terres de Destinées trouvera vite sa place dans votre ludothèque. Parfait pour jouer en famille ou entre amis, il combine accessibilité et subtilité dans un format compact.

—description from the publisher (French)

In this deck-building card game, you must recruit 3 Linkship Pilots and escape from Tag World. Each turn, you'll use Birdians to recruit new crew members who will add more credibility points that you need to recruit Linkship Pilots.

Each character has their own Group who they're close to and abilities that they'll use when they're deployed. Build the best crew who will help you escape Tag World before you alert the Bird-in-Trainings who are holding you captive!

Story:

Tag World is a World run by birds known as Bird-in-Trainings (and Higher-Up Birds who rule this World). Bird-in-Trainings raise their own group of kids called Wings, in a Shelter. While these kids may have enjoyed their time in Tag World initially, they've grown tired of being in the birds' care and are looking for a way to escape Tag World and return home.

A boy named Cipo and his friend Kolo infiltrate the group and spread stories of sneaking out and using a Linkship to drive them home. There are 12 Wings who know how to pilot a Linkship and can bring a group home. It takes 3 Linkship Pilots to pilot it, so you must use credibility points to recruit the Linkship Pilots in your Shelter.

Gain enough credibility to sway the Linkship Pilots to join you. To do that, you'll use Birdians to recruit other Wings from the Open Shelter and bring them to your Linkship Area so they can join your Linkship crew.

During the escape from Tag World, the Wings must watch out and be careful not to alert the Bird-in-Trainings. Once they're alerted 3 times, the birds find them and take them back to the Shelter. It's up to you to recruit the best crew who will help you escape from Tag World on a Linkship.

—description from the designer

Flavors is a colorful and highly interactive strategy game about the epic clash of anthropomorphic food factions. Base game has 4 unique factions.

Imagine fighting for the Sheriff Burger, leading armies of crispy nuggets and fearless fries into battle - while across the battlefield stand the Flan Queen and the Fondant King, commanding their sweet legions of cookies and confections. In Flavors, every clash is a deliciously absurd showdown where culinary fantasy meets sharp tactical play.

Flavors combines area control, deckbuilding, and multi-use cards into a fast-paced yet deeply strategic experience. Each turn, players play action cards from their hand to perform actions on the board: moving armies, gathering resources, battling opponents, and expanding their influence. Resources are a core part of the game and are used to create powerful heroes. For example, by combining rice, fish, and greenery, you can prepare the deadly Ninja Roll. Each hero brings unique abilities and tactical options, allowing players to shape their strategy as the game evolves.

Flavors delivers a game that is fun, chaotic, and competitive - all brought to life by vibrant illustrations from the outstanding artist Edu Valls.

—description from the publisher

Hamlet is an asymmetric card game for 2–4 players, with a playtime of 40–60 minutes, inspired by the inner conflict of Shakespeare’s tragic hero. During the game, players embody the emotions that tear Hamlet’s mind apart: Fear, Despair, Doubt, Envy, Pride, and Anger.

The game is played over two acts, each representing a key stage of the tragedy. In every act, players use character cards to compete for dominance across four different scenes. Winning a scene grants Influence points, strengthening that emotion’s hold over Hamlet’s thoughts. The more influence an emotion accumulates, the closer it comes to fully consuming Hamlet’s mind - and winning the game.

One role stands apart: Harmony. The player who takes on this special emotion also plays character cards, but instead of competing for dominance, their goal is to balance the conflicting emotions. By carefully steering the game toward balance, Harmony seeks to end the tragedy in a draw, denying all other players a clear victory.

—description from the publisher

Roll dice, move tokens, buy properties (players/stands), collect rent, build up sets for more rent, and aim to bankrupt opponents by landing on their developed spots, but with player tokens representing Villa legends/stars and properties being club areas like Villa Park stands. Key actions involve buying unowned properties or putting them to auction if you pass, getting money for passing "GO," and using Chance/Community Chest (or similar) cards for special effects.

Spin and Win with Spinazzi!
Spinazzi—the game everybody can play and anyone can win. Spinazzi is easy to play, a fun game that’s ideal for office parties, families or anytime friends get together. Whether you have a party of two or twenty two, everyone will want to play Spinazzi.

How to get started

Place the six marbles in the center dish of the Spinazzi board.

To begin, place the top between the thumb and forefinger positioning it in the center of the game dish. Giving the top a sharp spin, the marbles will bounce off the top, collecting in the numbered pockets. Although some marbles may remain in the center dish, a player’s turn is complete when the top stops spinning.

If the top or any of the marbles flip out of the Spinazzi game dish, the player loses a turn and receives no points.

The red marble doubles the point value of any pocket. If all six marbles score, the player receives one free turn and a chance to earn more points.

After each play, add the total number of points and proceed to the next player.

The first player to reach a total of 1,000 points wins the game. For a shorter version of Spinazzi, play a 500 point game.

Odd or Even Wager—place your bets!

For a change-of-pace, try your luck at this version of Spinazzi. Play action is similar to Spinazzi, except that each player indicates odd or even before playing. To win, the sum total of points must be odd or even as specified by that player. For added excitement, assign a monetary value to each point or place a wager before each play.

Rock & Roll Spinazzi!

Test your reflexes with this variation on Spinazzi. Follow the play action as described above. While the top is spinning, pick up the game board and gently rock it. Easy does it, the top or marbles may jump from the game board resulting in a lost turn. However, with a little practice a player can use this action as a way to increase points and to win.

Spinazzi in the Red!

Here’s Spinazzi with a twist, only this time keep your eye on the red marble. After each play, double the value for the red marble and deduct those points from your total. The first player to reach 500 wins.

You start with 7 cards in your hand, and there are face-up cards on the table. You begin your turn by deciding whether you want any of the table cards. If you do, you may discard the same number of cards from your hand and take those cards from the table, allowing you to create a strong starting hand.

You can then use your cards to perform various actions (for example: stealing cards, grabbing money from another player’s piggy bank, trying to slow down an opponent, or drawing more cards for yourself), or to acquire customers from the market, which adds money to your piggy bank.
You go through these steps every turn.

If you have paid close attention to who is currently the richest player, you can adapt your turn accordingly. If you know that you are the richest, you may try to end the game as quickly as possible. If you are not, you can adjust your turn to try to make another player poorer.

The game ends as soon as one of the four customer colors is depleted. At that point, the player who has collected the most money is declared the winner.

—description from the designer

Je hebt 7 kaarten in je hand en er liggen kaarten open op tafel. Je begint je beurt door te kijken of je liever kaarten van de tafel hebt en dan kun je er evenveel uit je hand wegdoen en die van de tafel pakken om zo een fijne starthand te hebben. Je kunt de kaarten vervolgens inzetten voor diverse acties(bijvoorbeeld: kaarten stelen, een graai doen in de spaarpot met geld van een ander, proberen een ander te vertragen of jezelf meer kaarten te bezorgen) of om klanten van de markt te halen waardoor er geld in je spaarvarken komt.
Deze stappen doorloop je elke beurt.
 Heb je goed onthouden wie de rijkste op dat moment is, dan kun je daarop je beurt aanpassen. Weet je dat jezelf de rijkste bent, dan kun je proberen het spel zo snel mogelijk te beëindigen. Ben je dat niet, dan kun je je beurt aanpassen om te proberen een ander armer te maken.

Het spel eindigt zodra er 1 kleur van de 4 kleuren klanten op is. Dan wordt er gekeken wie het meeste geld heeft verzameld en die is daarmee de winnaar.

—description from the designer (Dutch)

The Stone of Madness Dicefall is a cooperative tactical stealth board game for one to five players. Players jointly control five prisoners confined within an isolated monastery ruled by the Inquisition. Each prisoner has distinct abilities, restrictions, and mental conditions.

The game is played in structured rounds where players assign actions to the prisoners, move them through the monastery, and interact with rooms, objects, and objectives. Actions are resolved using dice. Dice represent uncertainty under pressure and are tightly linked to the character performing the action and the current situation.

Stealth is the core of the experience. Many actions create noise, which increases tension and causes Inquisition patrols to react and reposition. Patrol movement and behavior respond to player decisions and accumulated alerts, turning the monastery into an active threat rather than a static board. Avoiding detection requires coordination, timing, and restraint.

As the game progresses, prisoners are affected by madness and phobias represented through cards. These effects restrict available actions, impose compulsory behaviors, or disrupt carefully planned strategies. Mental deterioration is a central pressure and must be managed collectively, as ignoring it can quickly spiral into failure.

The monastery is built using modular tiles and narrative elements, ensuring that objectives, layouts, and challenges vary between sessions.

Players win the game by successfully completing the mission objectives defined by that scenario. Players lose if the monastery reaches a critical alert state or if the prisoners are no longer able to continue the escape due to accumulated consequences, containment, or mental collapse.

Victory depends on coordination, careful planning, and managing both physical exposure and psychological pressure throughout the mission.
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

description: Catchphrase style game. Identify as many familiar phrases as possible using the illustrations on the card. The first player to shout out the right answer wins the point!

Each turn, roll and combine dice to race up the mountain. Get to the top to score, but don’t get knocked off! If you roll the goat symbol, activate new abilities — powers that rotate every mission. As your story progresses, you’ll unlock even more powerful abilities and face tougher challenges. The mountain itself might even transform beneath your feet.  

Mountain Goats: Legacy combines  dice rolling/combining and events into a longer  version of Mountain Goats.  But, after the first session, more and more hazards and gameplay tweaks show up. The dice now have a goat symbol — when rolled, you’ll get to use one of the unlocked player powers. These can change with every session! There’s also a new system called “Courage” that greatly affects your ability to mitigate luck and refine your dice rolls.

This game can be played as either a single session or a legacy game.  As a legacy game, it is fully replayable and resettable. You will not be asked to destroy or modify components. The first session can also be combined with the original Mountain Goats game.

—description from the publisher
family: Organizations: Game Designers of North Carolina
Versions & Editions: Legacy Versions of Non-Legacy Games

description:
Take on the role of Ivy, the courageous new owner of a magical inn. Build your bewitching B&B to best suit your eccentric guests while cultivating the ivy covering your walls.

Each turn, carefully place each card in a grid. Assign guests, noting their preferences (things like "diagonally adjacent", "same row", "surrounded" etc.) to score them at the end of the game.

In Story Mode, earn rewards for meeting point goals and completing unique challenges.

—description from the publisher

Clash of the Elementals is a competitive card game built on a 54‑card deck. The deck contains twelve classes across four elements (Earth, Water, Air, Fire), two Wild Cards, and four Rules Cards. Players build four armies of five cards each, with the fifth card hidden until the endgame.

On each turn, players draw one card and play one card into any incomplete army. Armies are compared head‑to‑head using defined formations (e.g., Pentacle, Phalanx, Battle Line), ranked by strength and resolved with tie‑breaker rules based on class hierarchy, element order, and Wild Card effects.

The objective is to win the majority of 4 army battles. If the overall result is tied, players resolve with single‑card duels using unplayed cards until one achieves three victories. Wild Cards (Wizard and Sorceress) can assume any class and element, providing flexible but decisive power.

Winning requires assembling stronger formations than your opponent and managing card placement to maximize hierarchy and elemental advantages.

—description from the designer

It's like the name says. With the Ultimate Pre-Game Board Game, you get the ultimate pre-game. Need we say more? It's full of mini-games, challenges, and even QR codes, so each time you play is a brand new (but still ultimate) pre-game. Come on. Would you expect any less from us?

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Return to Dark Tower: Creeping Darkness Corruption Deck
description:
30 corruption mini cards to add to your game play.

Was included in several pledge levels when backing Return to Dark Tower: Expeditions as well as a separate add-on during the pledge manager.

The coziest card game this side of Santa’s Workshop is BACK just in time for the holidays… with MORE Christmas Movie Magic: our brand-new expansion pack filled with festive fun!

Includes:

     55 All-New Cards - packed with even more holiday tropes, cozy clichés, and movie-night mayhem.
     Two New Suits: Grey Bells & Yellow Stars - add strategy, variety, and fresh laughs every round.
     New 4 & 6 Point Cards - bigger plays, bigger comebacks, more fun for everyone.
     Now Up to 8 Players - perfect for festive family gatherings and parties.
     Fits in Your Original Box! The cards slide right into your original game box.


—description from the publisher

Honor Amidst The Stars
Vanguard is a micro-expansion inspired by Honor's End, blending knightly flair with Moonraker's sci-fi universe. It introduces the card-tucking mechanic: a fresh layer of strategy that lets you build and stack your deck in entirely new ways.

Knightly Crew
Recruit five new Crew members inspired by noble houses of a forgotten age. Each one allows you to tuck cards beneath them, creating powerful layered effects and long-term combos worthy of a seasoned captain.

Forged in Starlight
Upgrade your vessel with five new Ship Parts built for precision and planning. These experimental modules expand your ability to tuck and manage cards, rewarding foresight and tactical depth.

Trials of Valor
Face five new Contracts that can be tucked beneath existing ones as modifiers. They make missions riskier but also more rewarding for those bold enough to take them on.

—description from the publisher

Prepare to embark on the grandest Five Parsecs adventure yet as you make planetfall on a world filled with wonders, terrors, and difficult challenges.

Planetfall is a solo and co-op adventure game where you take the role of a crew of colonists, scientists, and explorers from the mighty Unity of Humankind, building a colony on an alien world packed full of long-overgrown crashed starships and dangerous ancient ruins, while contending with mysterious and hostile lifeforms.

You will have to direct your colony’s expansion and research as well as commanding your characters and troops in tactical miniatures combat. Over time, your colonists will gain experience and improve their combat abilities, while the R&D department adapts alien tech to upgrade your armories... and perhaps even your very bodies.

STAND-ALONE, COMPATIBLE RULES
The game is playable with any miniatures you would like to use and requires only a small figure count per battle. Rules are compatible with characters brought over from Five Parsecs from Home, Tactics, and Bug Hunt, allowing you to chain together the grandest space adventure you have ever experienced.

FEATURES:

Easy combat rules, allowing a single player to handle action quickly.
Packed with random tables including artifacts, colony events, crises, and more.
14 mission types ranging from exploration to pitched battles to delves among alien ruins.
Tactical enemies including pirates, renegades, outlaws, alien raiders, K’Erin landing parties, Converted recon teams... even hostile fellow colonists.
In-depth research system with 13 scientific theories, 40 buildings, and 8 augmentations to unlock.
As you explore, you will map out the world around your colony, and create your own unique encounter tables, filled with bizarre and evolving table-generated lifeforms.
Assailants specific to the Kensington Reach setting: Introducing the psionic, saurian Slyn and ancient robot guardians, the Sleepers.
Multiple epic endings possible for your colony.

Psy Game  is a strategic card game (200 cards)centered on the practice and profession of psychiatry and psychotherapy. Players collaboratively manage patient care by assembling multidisciplinary therapeutic teams, recruiting the best clinicians, accumulating care points, and navigating crises and treatment obstacles. The objective is to guide patients toward recovery while balancing limited resources and professional dynamics. Designed both as an educational tool and a social game, Psy Game blends real-world psychiatric themes with engaging strategy, making it ideal for training sessions, group discussion, or casual play.

Psy Game turns the complex world of mental health care into a fast, clever, and surprisingly accessible card game. You do not need any medical background—just basic arithmetic and a taste for tactical play. Each round challenges you to manage patients, apply treatments, and outplay others by making smart decisions under constraints, much like real clinical settings. It is easy to learn, quick to play, and quietly addictive. You will come for the theme out of curiosity and stay for the gameplay.

—description from the designer
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
Last Run is a fast, portable press-your-luck dice game for 2 or more players, wrapped in a snow sports theme. Built for skiers, snowboarders, and anyone who enjoys quick games with big moments, it feels right at home in a ski lodge, a condo, a cabin, or at the kitchen table.

On your turn you roll custom dice to line up scoring combos and earn points. At any time you can bank your score, or press your luck and roll again for a bigger payoff. Push too far and you wipe out, losing what you were building, so every turn becomes a small gamble in timing and nerve.

The rules teach in a few minutes and turns move quickly, keeping everyone involved. Games play in about 15 to 20 minutes, with big swings, comebacks, and table talk that feels like après, even when you are nowhere near the mountain.

Last Run comes in a compact box with six custom POW dice, a specialty Last Run die, microfiber rules and scoring cloths, extra score sheets, a sticker sheet, and a packable travel pouch. It is a giftable, travel ready game that brings mountain energy anywhere you play.

The Goalkeeper Lost in France:

A story and scenario for
Through the Mud and the Blood & CoCking Up the Mud and the Blood and other World War 1 skirmish wargame rules

boardgame: Hide, Stone, and Bone: A Paleolithic Skirmish Wargame
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
This large Advanced Tobruk System(ATS) module presents the Gulf War's battle of 73 Easting (26 February 1991) in which the US Second Armored Cavalry Regiment attacked defensive positions manned by elements of Iraq's Tawakalna Division.

Critical Hit's ATS, an outgrowth of Avalon Hill's venerable Tobruk (1974), is a high-detail game of modern tactical combat. Units are squads and individual vehicles; hexes are 50 yards across. 73 Easting is oriented toward the 2014 edition of the ATS rules, available separately.

The winning player will combine a detailed awareness of the strengths and weaknesses of the units, as presented on the games counters and on separate cards with tactical acumen. Successful tactics generally match those that work in the real world, but real-world knowledge is not necessary: players can discover modern tactics simply by playing the game.

73 Easting is not a city or a town. It is a line on a map of a country almost totally devoid of landmarks. This major confrontation began a little earlier at 69 Easting, four kilometers to the west. Each “Easting” marker is one kilometer from one line to another. Thus at this point they were about three miles west of Easting 73. The American unit to bear the brunt of the battle was the 2nd Armored Cavalry Regiment and its three squadrons of tanks, APCs and infantry. For two days, the regiment served as the tip of the spear for the 7th Corps.

Features 10 maps to mate with 8 scenarios plus all the combat counters you need to play -- plus Special Rules card and color folio PLUS a sheet of ATS Special Markers (not the BOT ones recently added to the catalog although the publisher suggests those) -- a total of 626 color, die cut counters.

The key is the action - as in ATS CLASSIC action. This is not EVO, it is not overhead Personnel -- it is the presentation ATSers have been enjoying since 2002 with the enhanced 2025 3/4" and weapons PLUS the new AFV Data Cards in large format, with campaign-specific data and the tables for each, you need.

Players determine who the dealer is by Rock-Paper-Scissors or by rolling dice. The dealer shuffles and deals five cards face-down to each player. The remaining cards form a face-down deck in the middle of the table. Players can only look at their own cards.

The dealer first draws a card from the deck, and adds it to their hand. He/She plays a card of their choice face-up on the table.

Players take their turns playing their cards in the center of the table in a clockwise direction. The next player must play a card that is the next number in sequence after the previous player's. For example, If 2 was played previously, then the current player must play a 3.  It the previous card was a 4, then a 5 needs to be played, and so on.

If the player has no cards to play or wishes to pass, he/she draws a card from the deck. When a player plays a 5, the next player needs to play a 1. If the player cannot play a 1, he/she must take all the played cards and the next player takes his/her turn. If the next player also has no 1 card, he/she draws a card from the deck, and the next player takes his/her turn. The same process is repeated until a card with the number 1 is played.
The player who empties their hand by playing all of his/her cards the fastest is the winner of the match.

Scoring
The winner of the match earns a winning token and 5 points. All other players receive negative points for the number of cards they have in their hand. Players who pass after the deck runs out of cards are out of the game and receive a minus point for the number of cards they have in their hand.

How to play a card (Important Rules)
There are 2 ways to play cards, either (a) or (b): 
(a) A player can play a sequence of consecutive cards on their turn. For example, if the previous player has played a 1, the current player can play a sequence of 2, 3, or 2, 3, 4, 5, etc. However, 5 and 1 are not consecutive, so you cannot play 2, 3, 4, 5, 1, and so on. The card always stops at 5.

(b)Or a player can play multiple cards of the same number on their turn. For example, playing only 3, 3, 3, or only 5, 5, 5, 5, 5.


     However, when playing a sequence of consecutive cards, cards of the same number cannot be played more than once. For example when playing a sequence of cards, the sequence of 2,3,4 or 1,2,3,4 are permitted.  Whereas 2, 2, 3, 4, or 1, 2, 3, 3, 4, 4, 4 are not allowed.


Winner
Once each player has become a dealer once in a round, the game ends. For a 2 player game, 2 rounds are played, and for a 3 player game, 3 rounds are played, then the final score is calculated based on points gained and points lost. The winner is the player who earns the highest score.

Additional rules
You may add the multiplier card to the deck. The multiplier card can be any number of the player's choosing. At the end of match if a player is holding the multiplier card in their hand, they will get a 10 point deduction.

A trivia game where players go around a 3×3 board, answering questions. Answering correctly gives you steps on the board and when you've gone around the board three times, you can enter the middle space to answer the final question.

The questions are multiple-choice questions with four possible answers. If you choose from two choices, you get 1 point. If you choose from four choices, you get 2 points and if you answer freely without hearing the possible answers, you get 3 points.

The questions are in two parts (for example: "In which sea... / is the Great Barrier Reef?"). After you hear the first part, you can ask for another player to help you with the question. If someone helps you and you get the answer right, you both get points.

Theme

Polaroid The Game is a storytelling and memory game built around iconic Polaroid-style photo cards. Players work with a deck of colourful photo cards and a timer shaped like a Polaroid camera, drawing inspiration from the idea that a picture can spark imaginative connections and unexpected narratives.

Gameplay

Each round begins with players taking turns placing photo cards from their hand onto a central pile, one by one, while explaining how their chosen image connects to the previous one. This builds a chain of photos tied together by improvised storytelling. The goal is not only to be creative with the narrative but also to focus on the sequence of images as players go, because once the timer buzzes, the game enters the next phase. When time is up, you enter the Recall Phase: one player (the Recaller) must try to remember and recite the exact order of cards placed in the story without looking. If they successfully recall the sequence, they score points; if they falter, other players may seize the chance to answer and earn points themselves. This mix of imagination and memory keeps everyone engaged from start to finish.

How to Win

Winning Polaroid: The Game comes down to scoring the most points over a series of rounds. Points are gained by successfully linking stories in ways that are memorable and by correctly recalling the sequence of photo cards during the Recall Phase. At the end of the agreed number of rounds, the player with the highest score is the winner.

Lov'in Game is a board game played on a heart-shaped track pierced by an arrow. The objective is to form a couple and lead it to the star at the tip of the arrow.

Players start as single and move their tokens by rolling a die. Single players may move in either direction to meet others. When two players agree, they can form a couple, or a single player may challenge a member of an existing couple by rolling the die; matching results create a new couple, sending the remaining player back to the start.

Once a couple is formed, the two players share a single Couple Token made of two half-hearts. Each half-heart can improve or deteriorate depending on the spaces reached. If the heart turns completely black, the couple breaks up.

Special spaces may strengthen or weaken relationships, trigger instant attraction, or allow strategic movement. When a couple’s heart is fully red and it lands exactly on the Two Rings space, it moves to the star and wins the game.

—description from the designer

The floating city of Ho Ina is drowning in the toxic fumes of MG-16132099, commonly known as Muema Gas—an industrial vapor compound leaking from aging reactor cores. Muema Gas thickens the air, corrodes machinery, and disrupts all communication systems. Audio transmission is completely useless; only a few intermittent mechanical signals can penetrate the poisonous haze.

You and your partner take on the roles of two intelligence agents, each holding half of the clues. Only by combining these fragments can you form the access codes needed to seal the reactor before Muema Gas reaches critical levels and turns Ho Ina into a permanent cloud of poison suspended in the sky.

Unable to speak, you can only send limited signals through the Muema Gas. You must deduce, coordinate, and uncover the two codes together. A single small error is enough for the Muema Gas pressure gauge to hit its limit—and end everything.

—description from the publisher

The table is set, now it’s time to clear it before it’s too late!

In All You Can Eat Buffet, each player has a deck of Mood cards, which they play to grab food from the table. 
During a round, these cards are revealed simultaneously and then resolved in player order and according to their instructions.

But beware: if two players play the same Mood card, they must clink glasses and take a drink token. 
Too many drink tokens might go to your head, and you may no longer be able to decide which card to play!

After 10 rounds, the game ends and points are tallied from the different food cards each player has collected.

Foods all score differently: meat gives you straightforward points, fruits reward you for collecting the most types, garlic will cost you points unless paired with parsley, and so on…
 The player with the most points wins!

—description from the publisher

Localis is a board game that challenges players to place iconic French cities, landmarks, and monuments onto a gridded map of France according to their exact geographical location. Each card features a difficulty level, which determine the number of points awarded when the card is correctly placed.

High Five Games presents a night of competitive fun. Contains 30 games to test skill, speed and balance.
Party game using props such as balloons, cups, ping pong balls etc

boardgame: Ridley's Games Room: 90s Pop Music Quiz
description:
How well do you remember the 90s? With a host of music questions spanning the decade you'll have fun casting your mind back to artists like the Spice Girls, Nirvana and huge hits such as MMMBop!
Includes 140 question cards.

boardgame: Ridley's Games Room: 2000s Pop Music Quiz
description: How well do you remember the noughties? With a host of music questions spanning the decade you'll have fun remembering huge hits from artists such as The Black Eyed Peas, Avril Lavigne and Nelly Furtado. Release your inner DJ with 40 cards featuring random music trivia and next line challenges about popular songs, albums, and artists from the new Millennium.

Conectados ("Connected") is a super fun board game, perfect for couples who love a challenge. Show how much you know your partner in a game full of intriguing and revealing questions.
Designed for 4 to 8 players, "Connected" is the perfect choice to liven up gatherings and parties. Bring together several couples and turn your night into an experience of joy and laughter.

Each round is a new chance to bet on your mutual knowledge. Write your answers, place your bets, and see who is truly in sync!

The game's questions are carefully crafted to promote fun and positive moments. Get ready for funny disagreements and surprising discoveries about your partner.

Perfect for a quick night out or a game marathon. "Connected" is easy to learn, quick to play, and impossible to forget!

The fight between the Archon-Prince of the Federated Commonwealth and the grieving Captain-General of the Free Worlds League has already blazed for weeks. On Oliver, the fierce cadets of the Third NAIS Cadre knew it was only a matter of time before the enraged troops of the Free Worlds League Military turned their eyes towards a former League world that had been battled over for centuries. The Federated Commonwealth forces on Oliver waited and prepared for the day. That day has come, and the fight begins in earnest.

Turning Points: Oliver uses Chaos Campaign rules to give players the option of fighting individual battles or to follow a campaign arc. Included are a detailed map of the world of Oliver, a run down of the forces involved, and scenario tracks recreating the battles.

—description from the publisher

This expansion brings new challenges that await you in a special endgame scenario unlocked after completing the story campaign. Face two new bosses - the Elven Wraiths and the Demon! Encounter two additional monsters - the Erynia and the Freshwater Siren.

The dream of the Federated Suns is found in the heart of every citizen, even amongst its adopted children. Amongst its elite regiments are the Kestrel Grenadiers, its ranks filled with not only those born in the Federated Suns, but former Capellan citizens that have found their own way to the Federation. These Capellan immigrants and Federation citizens fight for this dream even as their homelands come into conflict in war after war. But their dream of freedom for all Capellans comes to an end with the fall of the Sarna March. They must prove themselves in a Federated Suns that increasingly sees every Capellan as a potential traitor. A challenge faced by generations of Jade Warriors.

Spotlight On: Jade Warriors includes a Unit History, Personalities, Personnel Rosters, Mission Tracks, and data for use with all scales of BattleTech play.

—description from the publisher

Introducing Mint Kingdom Conquest — a brand-new 46-card expansion for Mint Kingdom (sold separately).

Step into a micro world of medieval conquest with Mint Kingdom Conquest - A compact mint tin solo game where you take on the role of a Lord - managing resources, building an army, and expanding your realm!

The game begins with the building of your kingdom. Grow your population and assign them roles — farmers to cultivate food, blacksmiths to forge metal, and peasants to generate gold through taxes. Each turn brings new events that could either aid your progress, like grand Tournaments, or challenge it, such as devastating famines. Once your resources are secured, raise an army of knights, crossbowmen, men-at-arms archers, pikemen, peasants and mercenaries to lay siege to the castles of three neighboring Lords. Conquer all three territories to claim ultimate victory!

What's New in Conquest?


     Adds 2 new Nobles and their Maps, bringing the total to 5, increasing replayability and offering a wider range of difficulty.
     Adds 2 new basic unit types to the game: Crossbowmen and Men-at-Arms for more variety.
     Introduces Mercenaries: Elite soldiers for hire who can have a drastic impact in battle.
     Introduces Prize Cards: Rewards earned after defeating a Noble that grant immediate or ongoing effects throughout the game.
     Introduces Settlement Cards: Begin your rise to the throne from humble beginnings as a hamlet or from a wealthy city rich in resources. Settlement Cards let you adjust the game’s difficulty by increasing or decreasing starting subjects and resources.
     Adds 4 new Event Cards for more variety.
     Replaces the original Battle Cards with 12 new ones, adding greater diversity and reducing downtime from shuffling.
     A few minor rule changes to enhance gameplay.


—description from the designer

Shrapnel: The Official BattleTech Magazine is your guide for securing your legend for future generations amid fiery, unforgiving BattleMech combat in the 31st century and beyond!

Dispossessed during a bitter campaign, a stranded mercenary, forced to literally stare death in the face, wrestles with his own will to survive. The owner of a Zeus that has been in his family for generations is in danger of losing this priceless legacy to bureaucratic red tape, and must decide how far he is willing to go to keep it. And the eccentric mercenary commander Alonzo Barrett, a descendant of the ancient Musketeers, seeks to engineer a fateful showdown with the legendary Black Widow herself, but will a legend fall for his gambit?

In this issue, you’ll travel to the heart of Mongolia on Christmas evening, 3151, where a Mongolian Cavalier who fought the Clans must join forces with her conquerors in pursuit of a common goal. You’ll follow a close-knit group of young friends in the Magistracy who discovered an abandoned Star League ’Mech and learn that jumping into a ’Mech cockpit isn’t as simple as they imagined. And finally you’ll visit a system abandoned during the fires of the Fourth Succession War, a planet not quite as uninhabited as previously assumed…

Protect your birthright and fight for future generations by exploring the melee-centric ’Mechs common in the Rasalhague Dominion, learning some common pitfalls to avoid in ’Mech engineering, and diving into the chivalric societies and MechWarrior cabals of the Federated Suns aristocracy. Then wage a new war with intel from unit and planet digests, technical readouts, playable scenarios, and more!

—description from the publisher

Welcome to Saving Angels, a solitaire World War II print and play wargame. Set in the Philippines during World War II, you are in command of a group of American Paratroopers (11th Airborne Division) and guerrilla forces on a daring raid on the Los Baños prison camp to rescue the Japanese held captives.

You must lead your squads, after hopefully surviving the danger and threat of their airborne and amphibious landings, across to the camp, and engage the enemy. You must secure prisoners and escape before the enemy reinforcements arrive.

The game comes complete with two maps. In the first map you will command your forces from their landings to the camp, whilst engaging any enemy forces. Once you have arrived in the camp, you will then continue play on the second map. In the second map, you will search the camp - gathering together the prisoners whilst dealing with the enemy before making your escape back across the first map.

The game can also be played cooperatively with 2-4 players, with each playing various Allied Units between them. This game utilises a lot of the core mechanics and gameplay from Devil of a Job and Operation Biting.

Welcome to Devil of a Job, a solitaire World War II print and play wargame. In this game you are in command of a group of British Airborne on a daring raid in enemy-occupied France on D-Day to capture and hold two key strategic bridges. As part of Operation Tonga these were key objectives that had to be captured intact, to help allow Allied troops to break out from the landing beaches.

"I found offering my thanks to these chaps a devil of a job" Major John Howard (from his diary).

boardgame: Until the Bitter End: Tanks – Battle of the Bulge
description: Welcome to Until the Bitter End - Tanks - Battle of the Bulge,  a World War II wargame. This is the fifth game in the Until the Bitter End series and the third game that focuses, solely, on tank (and other armoured fighting vehicles) battles. This is a print and play game for solitaire and two-players. This is a standalone game in my series of WW2 print and play games. You don't need to have played or own the first games - all of the digital PDF files are included (you simply print it out, provide yourself with a regular six sided dice and a pencil). This game also features new elements in the series including a full Campaign Game with rules for both two-players and solitaire campaigns.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups
family: History: World War II (Battle of the Bulge)

description:
RUUNS is an abstract strategy game for 2–3 players played on a modular board that changes with every game. Players take on one of three colors and draw triangular rune tiles blindly from a bag, placing them onto a shared board that develops over the course of play. Each placement reshapes the available space and influences future decisions, ensuring that no two games unfold the same way.

On each turn, a player draws a rune and must place it on the board according to specific placement rules, whether the piece matches their own color or an opponent’s. Runes must connect to existing pieces of the same color through defined contact points, allowing players to build scoring clusters while also disrupting their opponents’ formations. In two-player games, the unused color acts as a neutral blocking element, adding additional tactical considerations.

Players can win in one of two ways: by completing a continuous path connecting their two starting positions for an immediate victory, or by scoring the most points when no further legal moves remain. Points are earned by forming connected clusters of runes, with larger groups and certain configurations yielding higher scores. The game ends when the board is full or no legal placements remain, and the player with the highest total score wins.

—description from the designer

boardgame: Betwixt Two Tricks and Work, Life, Balance
description:
This game is packaged as a single deck of cards, with the rules for two games: Betwixt Two Tricks and Work, Life Balance.

The deck of cards is a somewhat standard deck of 52 cards, with A-K in four suits. However, each corner of a card shows only the suit or the rank, and not both.

Betwixt Two Tricks is a trick-taking for 3 or 4 players where each person will play 2 cards to the tricks.  The first person to play to a trick plays a card in front of the person on their left, setting the rank of that player's card. That player then plays a card in front of themselves, setting the suit of their card, and a card in front of the person to their left, setting that player's rank.  The goal is to balance the tricks won by the players on your left and right.

Work, Life, Balance is a trick-taking game for 2 players where players also play 2 cards to each trick, one for the rank and one for the suit. During setup, a random rank and suit are determined to be worth points for the hand. Each hand consists of 4 tricks, and by rule, at the end of the hand, each player must possess 2 tricks, so there is a redistribution process. The person with the most points after 2 hands wins.

boardgame: 七部族興亡記 (Nana Buzoku Kōbōki)
description:
In 七部族興亡記, roughly translated as "The Rise and Fall of the Seven Tribes", the players are competing to win the most tricks.

While cards have both a suit and a rank, the trick resolution is unique. Each person who played the strongest suit will count as having won the trick, and can take the card they played as a point.

Most suits have special powers depending on how many are in play, and some are permitted to be played face down.

In 雇われギルド, roughly translated as "Guild for Hire", the players compete in a loosely defined trick-taking game.

First, the players set the cards used in the game out in a market, arranging them by their cost, from 0 to 6. Then, the players are given coins, and draft the hand of cards they will use for the trick-taking portion in turn order, paying the corresponding cost of the cards they choose.

Once each person has 5 cards, the trick-taking begins. The lead player chooses a card from their hand and plays it face up on the table. Then, the other players each choose a card from their hand and play them face down. Once everyone is ready, the cards are revealed, any effects are resolved, and the winner of the trick collects the cards and leads to the next trick.

Players earn points for the cards they collect and the person with the most points after a number of rounds wins.

In けいとりて, roughly translated as Knitting Trick-Taking, players' cards are tiles with different lengths and no suit. The game includes a piece of yarn wrapped around a spool that is knotted throughout as the yarn is switched to a different piece of a different color.

When a player plays one of their cards, the yarn is unspooled enough to place their card at the end of the previous player's, and a clip is placed on the yarn. While the game is "must follow", a players card is permitted to be the suit of any of the colors of yarn that it is adjacent to.

More points are awarded for winning tricks with lower ranked cards, and the person with the most points with the game ends wins.
publishers: るりるりゲームズ (ruri ruri games)

description:
In アメノチハレルヒ, roughly translated as "Clear Skies After the Rain", the players are recording certain lines of dialogue. The game alternates between "lesson" tricks and "recording" tricks, with the lesson tricks being must follow and the recording tricks being may follow.

At the start of the game, each player is given a prompt card showing the lines of dialogue that they must record in order to win the game, with each line corresponding to a specific card they need to win a trick - though the correspondence to which card is not included on the prompt cards. The players are encouraged to say their lines as they play their cards.

The rules to the game presume it will take multiple hands, and in future hands, players retain any properly collected lines of dialogue they've won and will be dealt fewer cards based upon how many they have.

Whenever a player has collected each of their lines of dialogue, the game ends, with that player saying their lines and then winning the game.
publishers: るりるりゲームズ (ruri ruri games)

boardgame: 四色絵画の収集家たち (Yon Shoku Kaiga No Shūshūkatachi)
description:
In 四色絵画の収集家たち, roughly translated as "Collectors of Four Color Paintings", the players are competing in a trick-taking game. Each card features two illustrations in the style of cards from the game Illusion, and on the back, show the percent of each of 4 colors in those drawings.

Each player will play a card to each trick, then reveal the percent of the lead color on the back, with the highest percent winning the trick. Players also then draft new cards into their hand beginning with the player who had the least of the lead color on their card.

As players lead to tricks, they use a colored chip to declare the lead suit, and their points at the end of the game will be based upon the values of their cards which correspond to the last chip they haven't used.
publishers: あーきっと.ゲームズ (Archit Games)

description:
Game Overview

Unblocking is a social, condition-based knowledge game where players score points by selecting answers that satisfy a condition, rather than answering traditional questions.

Unlike trivia or quiz games, Unblocking does not ask “What is the correct answer?”
Instead, it asks:

“Choose something that fulfills this condition.”

The tension of the game happens before the answer is given, as players interpret the condition, assess risk, and decide whether their choice will be accepted by the group.

There is no player elimination.
Mistakes do not remove players from the game—they become part of the social experience.

---

Objective of the Game

Players compete to score the most points by successfully meeting the conditions presented during the game.

The game can end in two official ways:

Primary mode: after 8–10 full rounds

Secondary mode (optional): when a player reaches 100 points

The player with the highest score wins.

---

Core Concept: Conditions Instead of Questions

Each turn is driven by a condition, not a question.

Examples of conditions:

“A country located in Africa”

“An herbivorous animal”

“A chemical element that is alkaline”

“Something that floats”

“A character from mythology”

Players must interpret the condition, choose an answer, and accept the risk of being validated or rejected.

---

Game Setup

1. Each player receives:

1 mini abacus set to zero points

2. Shuffle the VSD Cards and place them face down.

3. Select a starting player to act as the first arbiter.

4. Decide the number of rounds or point limit.

The role of arbiter rotates every turn.

Turn Structure

On a player’s turn:


     The current arbiter reveals the top VSD Card, which defines the condition and turn modifiers.
     The active player announces a response that they believe satisfies the condition.
     The group validates the response:
         Using the Answer Validation Sheet
         Or by group consensus, with the arbiter having final authority
    
     The VSD Card effect is applied:
         Points are awarded if the answer is valid
         A secondary effect may apply if the answer is invalid
    
     The player updates their score using the mini abacus.
     The arbiter role passes to the next player.


---

VSD Cards (Value & Social Dynamics)

VSD Cards define what happens during a turn.

The deck is structured approximately as follows:

~50% point-value cards (+1 to +5 points)

A small percentage of Pachanga-style cards

The remainder introduce light turn modifiers or social dynamics

Point Values

Valid answers score points as shown on the card.

+ 5 point cards are intentionally rare.

Pachanga-Style Cards

Some VSD Cards include light social micro-activities that trigger only if the player’s answer is invalid.

These activities:

Are brief and non-punitive

Do not eliminate players

Exist to maintain social energy and group engagement

Examples include:

a short verbal challenge

a simple group action

a playful instruction

Errors remain part of the fun, not a punishment.

---

Answer Validation & Arbiter Role

Each turn has an arbiter, responsible for:

Reading the VSD Card

Consulting the Answer Validation Sheet

Making the final decision if disagreement arises

The arbiter role rotates every turn to ensure fairness and shared authority.

Multiple answers may be valid for a single condition.

---

Scoring System

All players track points using their mini abacus

Scores are always visible to all players

Strategy emerges from relative positioning, not hidden information

---

End of the Game

At the end of the final round, or when a player reaches the target score:

All players compare scores

The player with the highest total wins

There are no tiebreakers by elimination; ties may result in shared victory if desired.

---

Replayability & Expansion

The Classic Edition includes 60 VSD Cards, providing high replayability through:

condition variety

interpretation differences

social dynamics

Additional VSD Card sets can be added without modifying the core rules, allowing the system to scale while preserving its structure.

​Coludoku (color+sudoku) 色独/콜루도쿠 is a strategic card game for 1-6 players. There are 1-4 macarons with different colors in one square dessert card. Players rotate or flip 2×2 dessert cards on the table, attempting to arrange the 4 cards so that every row and column has macarons of different colors. Player gets the highest score after six rounds win the game.

—description from the designer

Combining anagram solving with word making, this is a game to test your knowledge and power of quick thinking. Ingenuity is played against the clock and provides fun for all who like word games. Just when you think you have a word ready to score an opponent steals a letter !!!

The game can be played by 2 or more players individually or in teams.

boardgame: 葬送のフリーレン 人狼 (Frieren: Beyond Journey's End Werewolf)
description:
■ Cover illustration by Abe Tsukasa!
Frieren & Aura's cards use the cover illustration!

■ Find the demons who are deceiving humanity with words and banish them with Zoltraak!

■ Use magic to identify treasure chests to spot the mimics, and you might have a chance to survive...

■ Contents:
29 role cards, 3 role list cards, 2 treasure chest cards, instruction manual

■ Roles:
Continental Magic Association Receptionist (host)
Wizard
Archmage
Warrior
Priest
Hero
Laundry Wizard
Childhood Friend
Martial Arts Priest
Demon
Lord
Founder

—description from the publisher (translated)

In this TCG you compete with your opponent to see who completes their five Stories first, using Characters and Events from the multiple works of the manga artists CLAMP.

To complete your Stories, you need to arrange your Characters as the difference scenarios each Story card describes, but be careful and well supplied with Events, as your opponent may interfere with their own to try to stop you.

Once a player has completed all five of their Stories (and thus flipped them over, revealing their full illustration) the game ends and that player is the winner.

Christmas time is coming and as ALWAYS Santa Claus takes all the credits for your hard work. It’s time for the elves to organize themselves for better working conditions. Let’s unionize!

Christmas Union is a roll and write game where you take the role of one of Santa Claus helpers. You and your colleagues are tired of doing all the hard work but getting none of the credits for making kids happy this time of the year. Can you organize your fellow workers and demand for your rights?

How to play:
The game sheet is divided into 5 areas: the Santa Claus Factory (left), the Elves Assembly (middle) and the Strike manifesto (right). On top you have the Demands tracks and on the bottom left the Oh my Deer! track.

These areas are mini-games that you play every round, following these steps:

     Roll 2d6 (two common dice)
     Choose one to use in the Factory
     The other one will be used in the Assembly
     Add the sum of the two dice to prepare a Strike.


The dice rolled will be used by ALL players, but each one is free to make their own choices on each area use each die.

Game End:
When one player completes any of the 3 areas, the game ends and points are scored. You score points during the game and for the position in each of the demands. The player with the most points wins the game. In a case of a tie, the player with less marks in the “Oh my Deer” track wins.

—description from the designer

In Wo ist Mimmi?, there are 50 cards, including sheep cards, shepherd cards and other cards. The cards lie on the table, face down. 
On their turn, the player turns a card of their choice. If one or more sheep are shown on the card, the card remains face up and the player picks another card. They continue turning up cards until they turn up an "other" card or a shepherd card.

In the event of a shepherd card, the player can collect the open sheep card (if 3 or more sheep are shown). 
Otherwise, they turn back the cards, once they turn up another card.

The game ends once Mimmi, the pink sheep, is collected (with the help of a shepherd card).

The player with the most sheep (=points) wins.

In Solstice, 1-4 of your friends make offerings to Celtic Gods to try to win the most favor by the end of the game. In this bidding, bluffing, and deduction game, you’ll use what you know, as well as what you know your opponents know, to choose how many of your precious coins you should bid on propositions.

The game ends in one final auction phase where players have a last chance to snag that critical addition to their victory point tableau.

—description from the publisher

Enso is a game inspired by the “Enso”(円相) motif depicted in calligraphy or painting within Zen culture.

Unlike most games, the possible moves for a piece depend on its adjacent state in this game: a piece adjacent to an enemy must make a capture; a piece adjacent only to allies can move to an enemy's adjacent space or an unoccupied space without making a capture; and a piece with no adjacent pieces cannot move.

The objective is to surround one of your pieces with “empty” – that is, to place it in a position where all eight directions surround vacant spaces.

100% AI Generated Card Visuals!
Shadows & Syndicates is a 1v1 duel card game. There are no booster packs for players (Players have access to all cards), no deck-building, no errata, card bans, set rotations, or meta—and no mana flood/loss or power creep. Collectors get a standalone, fair collector's product featuring boosters that function completely independently of the gameplay.

The gameplay experience is similar to Chess: the rules are explained quickly so you can start playing immediately, while the true depth and mastery emerge through experience.

—description from the publisher (translated)

100% AI Generated Card Visuals!
Shadows & Syndicates ist ein 1-gegen-1-Duell-Kartenspiel. Es gibt keine Booster für Spieler (Spieler haben Zugriff auf alle Karten), keinen Deckbau, keine Errata, Kartenbans, Setrotation oder Meta – und kein Mana-Flood/Loss oder Powercreep. Sammler erhalten ein eigenständiges, faires Sammlerprodukt mit Boostern, das komplett spielunabhängig funktioniert.

Vom spielerischen Anspruch her ist es wie Schach: Die Regeln sind schnell erklärt, sodass man sofort loslegen kann, doch die wahre Spieltiefe und Meisterschaft entwickeln sich über die Erfahrung.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Зомби в доме: Заражение – Эвакуация (Zadventure: Evacuation)
description:
Зомби в доме: Заражение – Эвакуация ("Zadventure: Evacuation") expansion completely changes the game's objective, turning it into a perilous rescue operation. You must return to the house to find and evacuate five family members. But your car can't fit everyone – you'll need a helicopter for the final rescue. Find the flare gun to call it in and become the pilot!

The helicopter is your key to salvation, a true flying fortress that can extract you and the family from the very heart of danger. And every rescued person becomes your loyal ally, as each family member possesses unique abilities that will aid you in this difficult task.

However, be prepared to face six new monsters: from the Wraith, who can pass through walls, to the Swamp Horror, which can only be defeated with a grenade launcher. Get ready for a tense game where tactics and teamwork will decide everything!

—description from the publisher (translated)

boardgame: Обольщение: Промокарты джентри (Obsession: Gentry Promo Cards)
description:
Promo cards are given as a gift for pre-ordering the Russian edition from 4GAMES.

Pack of five guests (gentry) cards that can be added to the base game Obsession:

     Юрий, граф Варпов ("Yuri, Earl of the Warps")
     Леди Алёна Миронова ("Lady Alyona Mironova")
     Денис Егоров, эсквайр ("Denis Egorov, esq.")
     Юрий и Александра Загродские ("Yuri and Alexandra Zagrodsky")
     Елена, виконтесса Шкуткова ("Elena, Viscountess Shkutkova")


The cards feature images of Russian board game bloggers.

Would it be easy to guess what was invented earlier — the computer or instant coffee? Or perhaps… the first pair of jeans? Dive into the witty party game “Time Machine” and set off on an adventure-filled journey through time.

The goal of the race is to outsmart your opponents by traveling to a year as close as possible to the finish event set by the Time Navigator.

To win, you’ll need both knowledge and daring. Time machine fuel is limited, and on every turn you face a tough decision: rest and replenish your cards, or launch into yet another risky journey through time?

During the game, one of the players becomes the Time Navigator. Each turn, the Navigator announces a randomly drawn historical event but does not reveal its date. The other players race to choose the chronologically closest event from the cards in their hands. Players may also skip the race and replenish their time machine fuel — their supply of date cards. Once everyone has made their choice, the Navigator predicts which player’s event will win the race through time.

A victory point is awarded to the player whose chosen event has the smallest time gap between its date and the finish event. The Time Navigator also earns a point if their prediction is correct.

This abstract game is playable on a square-grid surface of any finite size.

In their turn a player may place and remove a total of friendly and enemy pieces (respectively) that is equal to or less than “X”, an integer > 1 and agreed upon at the start of the game.

The idea of “control” over a space relying only on occupation of its flanks and rear (which permits capture in it, no matter how strong it is) is inspired by games like Ki (Corey Clark, 2010) and Control (Takuro Kawasaki, 2024) though those games forbid placement in an enemy controlled space.

Placements and removals in the game must be balanced, especially early on, and there is a crucial difference in placement between pieces that are already on the grid versus those that are coming from the pieces not yet placed.

A player loses the game through attrition (losing more than half of their starting pieces).

—description from the publisher

boardgame: 群雄戦国時代 (Sengoku Jidai period)
description:
Multi player wargame for 3-6 players themed around Japan's Sengoku period. Each player controls an alliance of warlords and strives to become the ruler of medieval Japan.
Gunyu Sengoku Jidai means the Warring States Period for Warlords.

In set-up, each player forms an alliance of one major and three minor warlords. If there are fewer than six players, non-player alliances are also formed. This ensures that battles will occur across Japan at the same density regardless of the number of players.

Each turn, the order of action for each alliance is determined by chit pull. Non-player alliances are temporarily controlled by the most recently active player. A chit for Kyoto, the capital of medieval Japan, grants an extra turn to the ruler of Kyoto.

During their turn, players spend action points based on the number of territories and their leaders' political abilities to mobilize troops, engage in battles, move troops and leaders, recruit leaders, and acquire and play strategy cards.

A player who controls 10 areas at the end of their turn immediately wins.

The Stacking Trilogy is a game anthology featuring three of Dieter Stein's early signature stacking games. It includes the following three titles:

     Abande - Add your piece or move them onto adjacent enemy stack while keeping all stacks connected throughout the game.
     Attangle - Trap your opponent's pieces from two different directions and beneath your stacks.
     Accasta Pari - Capture opponents through continuous split-move chains while aiming to occupy enemy territory.


The board features double-sided printing: one side for Abande and Attangle with a central point, and the other for Accasta Pari with the “castle” areas.

A Greek board game released in 1973 featuring the well-known comic book character Lucky Luke. The characters featured in the board game are Lucky Luke, the Daltons, and John Doe.

It is played by 2-6 players, who choose their character in the game after rolling the dice. Whoever rolls the highest number becomes Lucky Luke, whoever rolls the second highest number becomes John Doe, and then those with the lowest numbers become the Daltons. To start the game, each player places their pawn on the board at the point marked with their character's name. Lucky Luke plays first, followed by the Daltons and finally John Doe.

Players can only move forward, except when they land on the small circle spaces on the board, where they can move backward. During the game, Lucky Luke can take the Daltons out of the game only when he lands on the same square as them, and they will end up at the sheriff's headquarters in Dodge City.  John Doe takes the Daltons out of the game in the same way, with the difference that the Daltons end up at the sheriff's headquarters in Canyon City. The Daltons are headed for their hideout. No fights between Lucky Luke and John Doe are allowed during the game.

When the game is over and everyone has taken their places, we count which of Lucky Luke and John Doe has taken out more Daltons.  Then we count how many Daltons have made it to their hideout. The winner will be determined based on these counts.

A board game manufactured and released in Greece during the 1970s, themed after the famous television series "The Muppet Show".

It is played by 2 to 3 players.At the start of the game, players place the cut-out cardboard pieces on the board in the slots provided. Then each player chooses a Muppet and places it at the starting point.

The winner is the first player to reach the finish line (circle) with the exact number.  Along the way, when a player lands on a square with writing on it, they follow the instructions. If a player lands on a square occupied by another player, they must spin the wheel again.

Лесные истории ("Forest Tales") is an educational adventure game that will introduce you to the mysteries of the forest. Your task is to find food for your animal in the woods while avoiding the dangers lurking everywhere.

The game lasts several rounds (days) until one of the players collects all the food types shown on their animal card.
Each player chooses one of the forest animals and receives that animal's terrain cards.
All players simultaneously decide which part of the forest their animal will go to for food and place the corresponding card face down in front of them.
The terrain cards are then revealed, everyone learns each other's plans, and sends their animal figures to the chosen area. Special rules apply for animals that end up in the same location: larger animals scare smaller ones, faster animals outpace slower ones. Additionally, animals can be frightened by the hunting dog roaming the forest. Not every forest dweller will get to eat that day!
Players whose animals get to eat draw food cards. However, they can only keep the food that their animal actually eats.

The winner is the player who first fully feeds their animal by collecting all the food pictured on their animal card.

—description from the publisher (translated)

In Merville Battery, you take control of the heroic attackers of the 9th Parachute Battalion, 6th Airborne Division in the moments just before the D-Day beach landings on 6 June, 1944, in a desperate assault against the Merville Battery. Your mission is vital. Fail and the British forces landing at Sword Beach will suffer a devastating artillery barrage as they come ashore from the heavy guns of the German 1st Battery, endangering the entire D-Day operation.

The goal of Merville Battery is to assault the battery compound by passing over mines and barbed wire, withstanding small and heavy arms fire, then disabling the artillery ensconced in four casemates. Your forces must then withdraw safely before the guns of HMS Arethusa open fire on the compound.

The game board depicts the Merville Battery compound, with 9 PARA making their assault from the east. The men of 9 PARA must pass through a minefield and charge into the camp to reach the casemates and disable the guns within. The mission does not end when the guns are disabled. The paratroopers must also aid wounded comrades and help them escape.

The actions of the defending German 1st Battery, 1716th Artillery Regiment, 716th (Static) Infantry Division are represented by a deck of cards while you make each critical decision on behalf of the British paratroopers.

—description from the designer

The award-winning party game Telestrations will get your family and friends laughing as they draw what they see, then guess what they saw for hours of hilarious miscommunication. All players simultaneously sketch, pass, and guess while revealing laugh-out-loud and unpredictable outcomes!

Adult version of the game.
family: Versions & Editions: Adult Versions of Family-Friendly Games

description:
Together, revisit the little moments of life to see your partner with the eyes of the heart.

In Totem, players reveal each others' strengths and qualities by forming totems, combinations of an animal card (representing a strength) and a quality card. Every player contributes one animal card and one quality card to each other player's totem.

Players can earn more points if the cards that they played were considered to be especially relevant and accurate, either by the active player or by the other players. The favourite animal and quality cards for that round stay in front of the active player and become their totem. The game ends when everyone has a totem.

Win together when you have created a complete Totem for your partner (or for the couple of friends if you are playing with several people).

In Totem, players reveal each others' strengths and qualities by forming totems, combinations of an animal card (representing a strength) and a quality card. Every player contributes one animal card and one quality card to each other player's totem.

Players can earn more points if the cards that they played were considered to be especially relevant and accurate, either by the active player or by the other players. The favourite animal and quality cards for that round stay in front of the active player and become their totem. The game ends when everyone has a totem.

Totem Junior Features 60 large, colorful cards, simplified rules, and vocabulary tailored for younger players.
family: Versions & Editions: Junior Versions of Grown-Up Games

description:
Every player has 12 hidden cards (3x4). Two are turned face up. On your turn, you can take the top card from the discard or draw pile. You can exchange one card (hidden or open) from your display. The round ends when one player has only open cards. (equal turns). All cards will be revealed. Add the numbers of the card for scoring. The game ends after one player has 100 or more points. Whoever has the lowest number wins.

Special rule: Whenever one column of 3 cards all have the same value, they will be discarded and no longer scored.

Cards are ranked from -2 up to 12.

Available on exclusivity on DisneyLand Paris shop.

In this new expansion of Water Wars, the galaxy will suffer an invasion of three new races, who come in search of our water reserves.
These new races incorporate new abilities that will allow you to explore new ways of playing, such as placing fire tokens on planets, responding to an attack with acid blood, or obtaining additional water from planets.
In addition, four new planets are added to this expansion, and these planets have special bays where fire tokens can be placed.
A few elite cards are also included.
This new expansion uses the rules from the Water Wars rulebook. The game setup and endgame remain the same as in the base game. Players choose their armies and battle for 3 rounds, with the player who has accumulated the most water winning the game.

—description from the publisher
