family: Game: Fighting Wings Aircraft Data Card (ADCs) downloadable expansions

description:
A simple race game to encourage Bible reading. Players select a random verse,count the nouns in that verse,then move one space per noun.

—user summary

The Best Generation is a strategic Islamic card game that promotes knowledge, spirituality and joy through play. Learn, strategise and have fun competing against other players in the race to be the first to accumulate 35 points.

Each player gets to play 3 actions during their turn. Here are some actions you may take:


     Play an Action Card from your hand.
     Draw a Question Card
     Build your Sahaba Deck
     Rearrange your Sahaba Cards


The first person to accumulate 35 points wins.

Even though there is a trivia element to this game, you may win the game even without answering any question cards. However, when you answer the questions correctly, you may draw an additional card, which gives you an advantage.

Ultimately, this is a strategy game, and you may win based on how you play your cards.

—description from the publisher

Race to Jannah is a lighthearted card game where players compete to reach Jannah by collecting hasanats, and making sure others don't reach 5 hansanats before you do.

The goal of the game is to be the first to have 5 hasanats in your deck.

On your turn, you:

- draw a card from the deck 
- play an action / upgrade / downgrade / hasanat card

This game is quick to play, requires no Islamic knowledge and is a fast and simple game to learn and play.

-description from publisher

boardgame: Betrayal at House on the Hill and Baldur's Gate Promo Cards: Flash Freeze & Death Curse
description:
Pack of two promotional event cards; one for Betrayal at House on the Hill and the other for Betrayal at Baldur's Gate:
- Flash Freeze
- Death Curse

DIC3: Hip-Hop is a roll & write jam for one to as many players as you can fit around the table. Run it solo or squad up with your crew - everyone plays on their own sheet, then you compare scores and see who really brought the heat.

Inspired by the addictive flow of match-3 mobile games, DIC3: Hip-Hop is all about dropping symbols, chaining combos, and clearing the grid. The more match-3s you land, the smoother your run - and the faster those symbols disappear.

—description from the publisher

Forte Arena is a tactical gladiator arena game where players compete for supremacy between rival gladiator schools. Over four intense rounds (“shows”), players place gladiators into the arena, resolve simultaneous directional attacks, claim rewards, and manage the risks of exposure in the central combat zones.

Each gladiator card features asymmetric strength values in four directions, creating a constantly shifting tactical puzzle. Gladiators attack immediately upon entering the arena, striking all adjacent opponents at once. Successful attacks earn denarii, while overwhelming victories may wound enemies—claiming trophies that can be converted into extra turns or endgame points.

The arena itself is a double-edged sword: central spaces grant valuable rewards, but leave gladiators dangerously exposed. Timing, positioning, and orientation are everything. Tactical action cards and optional wild beast encounters add further layers of depth for experienced players.

After four shows, the gladiator school with the most denarii is crowned champion.

—description from the designer

Push Your Hen is a rustic game of skill.

Hatch your eggs, push your Mama Hen toward the river, and skillfully manage the risks to advance without losing everything.

Each turn, a player rolls an Egg token around the henhouse: depending on its trajectory, it lands in a specific area of ​​the coop and reveals a Chick.

The player then chooses whether to stop and use the chicks to advance their Hen, or to continue hatching more eggs to try and collect even more. The unpredictable fall of the eggs creates a physical and visual dimension that makes each turn unique.

Vroom is a cooperative racing game.
Evaluate your speed, move your car without board nor reference points and try to cross the finish line in the correct order.

Draw a Speed card and move your car on your made up circuit. With values between 1 and 7, the cards won't make you move with consistency and you'll need to evaluate the distance your car will move; with no board and tiles to guide you. Only your car movement will give a clue about the card you drew.

At the end of the fifth round, everyone must be ordered in correlation with the sum of the 5 Speed cards they drew.

Events and other optional modules will shake up your strategies a little once you get the hang of it: Jump stunts, landslides, weather and more…

Svaraðu rangt is a thrilling and fun family game where you have to answer four lively questions that everyone should be able to handle.

You must answer every question wrong in under 15 seconds. But to shake things up, sometimes you have to answer correctly too—which makes it wonderfully confusing and hilarious. This is a game for everyone, creating great moments and bursts of laughter when, for example, you end up answering wrongly whether all Icelandic women are left-handed in summer. Or can you answer wrongly whether cows are found in the East of Iceland? Is “house” a three-letter word? Is German spoken in Germany? The game that makes everyone laugh—and get a bit on edge—trying to answer simple questions the wrong way.

—description from the publisher (translated

Svaraðu Rangt er hörkuspennandi og skemmtilegt fjölskylduspil þar sem þið þurfið að svara fjórum hressum spurningum sem allir eiga að ráða við.

Svara þarf öllum spurningum rangt á innan við 15 sekúndum. En til að hrista upp í þessu þarf stundum að svara rétt líka og það gerir einstaklega ruglingslegt og skemmtilegt. Þetta er leikur fyrir alla og býr til skemmtilegar stundir og hlátursköst þegar td svarar því rangt hvort að allar íslenskar konur örvhentar á sumrin? Eða getur þú svarað því rangt hvort kýr finnist á Austurlandi? Er Hús þriggja stafa orð? Er töluð þýska í Þýskalandi? Spilið sem fær alla til að hlæja og fara á taugum við að svara einföldum spurningum rangt.

—description from the publisher

Take the principles of a standard straight: A 2 3 4 5

To Jack & Back uses all of the cards in order twice - one going up, and the other going down - to create two pathways, with two jacks positioned as each end as bookends.

With this order, players take it in turns to place a card from their hand into a discard pile in the middle, going up or down in value from the previous card. Play thus moves from left to right and then right to left, bouncing back and forth between the two jacks. Suits are irrelevant. First to dispose of all of their cards wins!

Simple, right?! Well, there's a number of catches. The first is that players can only select a card that's up to three in sequential value ahead of the previously played card. Don't have one that fits the bill? Well pick up another one and add that to your hand!

The second catch is that when you get to the jacks at each end, one player will have to pick up two additional cards. Yay, more cards to hold!

Then there's miss a go sevens, change direction aces, act-as-anything jokers, and choose to play again multiples that further confuse matters!

Play in five minutes or play all evening. Works for 2 players or 3+. Easy to learn, simple to play, loads of fun!

—description from the designer

boardgame: Field Commander: Robert E. Lee – Expansion #4 Second Manassas Battle Pack
description:
An optional battle pack which includes five battles:

Cedar Mountain
Brawner's Farm
Stony Ridge
Groveton
Chinn Ridge

Cedar Mountain: The first battle in the campaign that would culminate in Second Manassas, this game focuses on the actions of “Stonewall” Jackson who rallied the 27th Virginia to prevent the Union capitalizing on its earlier retreat. Scale: Regiment.

Brawner’s Farm: This game covers the early stages of the Confederate attack on the Union column marching along Warrenton Turnpike at the opening of the Battle of Second Manassas. Scale: Regiment.

Stony Ridge: This battle covers the charge made by Grover’s First Brigade against Thomas’s Confederate Brigade defending Stony Ridge in mid-afternoon of the second day of fighting during the Battle of Second Manassas. Scale: Regiment.

Groveton: This game covers the clash between brigades of Union Brigadier General Hatch’s First Division and Confederate Brigadier General Hood’s Division along the Warrenton Turnpike close to Groveton late on the second day of the Battle of Second Manassas. Scale: Regiment.

Chinn Ridge: The early stages of the Confederate attack on Chinn Ridge during the Battle of Second Manassas. Scale: Regiment.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Field Commander: Robert E. Lee – Expansion #3 Antietam Battle Pack
description:
An optional battle pack which includes five battles:

Turner's Gap
Fox's Gap
The Cornfield
Bloody Lane
Brave 9th

Turner’s Gap: The attack made by the 2nd Wisconsin Regiment (part of the Iron Brigade) against the 6th Georgia Regiment in the assault on Turner’s Gap. Scale: Company.

Fox’s Gap: This game covers the early attack made by elements of the 1st and 2nd Brigades of the Union Kanawha Division against Garland’s Confederate Brigade. Scale: Regiment.

The Cornfield: This battle covers the initial clash between Confederate companies of the 18th Georgia Regiment and Union companies of the 2nd United States Sharpshooters and the 2nd Wisconsin Regiments in the Cornfield north of Sharpsburg, thus initiating the Battle of Antietam. Scale: Company.

Bloody Lane: This game covers the final attack on the Confederate flank north of Sharpsburg made by elements of the 61st New York Regiment and elements of the 64th New York Regiment. Specifically, it recreates the moment when the 6th Alabama attempted to pivot to meet the Union soldiers. Scale: Company.

Brave 9th: This battle covers the charge made by the 9th New York Infantry against the 15th South Carolina in the moments before A.P. Hill launched his own counter-attack on the extreme left of the Union flank. The soldiers of the 9th made the furthest progress, with some entering the streets of Sharpsburg itself. Scale: Company.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Field Commander: Robert E. Lee – Expansion #2 Chancellorsville Battle Pack
description:
An optional battle pack which includes five battles:

Catherine Furnace
Rebell Yell
Hazel Grove
Marye's Heights
Salem Church

Catherine Furnace: The 23rd Georgian Infantry Regiment delays attempts to breakthrough to the hidden road network that Hooker now suspects is being used by the Confederates to maneuver into a flanking attack. Scale: Company.

Rebel Yell: The 75th Ohio make a brave but futile stand against the overwhelming numbers of Jackson’s soldiers who, after completing the famous hidden march to bring them onto the flank of XI Corps, scream their Rebel Yell as they attack. Scale: Company.

Hazel Grove: After both “Stonewall” Jackson and A.P. Hill were both wounded it fell to the cavalry commander, JEB Stuart to organize and execute the attack at Hazel Grove. After hours of desperate fighting the Confederates were able to drive back the Federal forces, thus beginning a more general withdrawal. Scale: Brigade.

Marye’s Heights: The first Union attempt to break through to Chancellorsville was made against Confederate troops holding a stone wall atop Marye’s Heights, a short distance outside Fredericksburg. Though heavily outnumbered, Early and his soldiers were able to delay a Federal advance for several hours. Scale: Regiment.

Salem Church: While Lee was enjoying great success on the western part of the battlefield, the Confederate position to the east was precarious, with little more than a token force being left in the vicinity of Salem Church to prevent Union reinforcements arriving from Fredericksburg. This battle recreates the Federal assault on the Rebel firing line. Scale: Brigade.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Field Commander: Robert E. Lee – Expansion #1 Gettysburg Battle Pack
description:
An optional battle pack which includes five battles:

First Shots
Little Round Top
Culp's Hill
East Cavalry Field
Pickett's Charge

First Shots: Shortly after 7:30am on July 1, 1863, the first shots were fired at the commencement of the Battle of Gettysburg. This game covers the advance of Archer’s and Davis’ Brigades along the Chambersburg Pike into the 8th Illinois Cavalry. Scale: Company.

Little Round Top: At the extreme left of the Union Left Flank, this position was vital. One of the most famous incidents of the American Civil War is portrayed in this game: the desperate downhill charge made by the 20th Maine to finally drive back the Confederate advance. Scale: Company.

Culp’s Hill: After drawing away units to deal with Longstreet’s main attack, the Union defense of Culp’s Hill was weakened. This game focuses on the Confederate attack against a single brigade protected by hastily constructed defensive works. Can the Federal defenders hold on until reinforcements arrive? Scale: Regiment.

East Cavalry Field: Stuart vs Custer! This battle recreates Custer leading the 7th Michigan in a counter-attack. Scale: Company.

Pickett’s Charge: This battle covers the furthest advance made by the Confederate General Lewis Armistead on July 3 during Pickett’s Charge at a place that came to be known as “the angle”, referring to a bend in the wall Union soldiers sheltered behind. For a moment the Union line was broken, but Armistead was unable to turn the captured artillery back on the Federal forces and reinforcements quickly arrived, driving the Rebels back. Scale: Regiment.

—description from the publisher

Moonshineville is a tactical board game set in a fictional city during the Prohibition era of the 1920s. Players take on the roles of rival gang leaders, each running an illegal liquor operation while trying to stay one step ahead of both the police and their competitors.

At its core, Moonshineville is about movement, timing, and controlled risk. Players produce illicit liquor, load it onto their trucks, and navigate the city’s road network to complete delivery contracts. Every route choice matters: the police are always on the move, and a single misstep can wipe out an entire shipment.

The game is driven by a multiuse card system where every card offers a meaningful choice. Each card can be used in one of two ways: either played hidden into the action stack for planned effects, or as a reaction from your hand to trigger actions and counter-actions at unexpected moments.

These hand-actions give Moonshineville a strong reactive layer. Players can interrupt opponents, manipulate the police, sabotage deliveries, or steal the initiative at precisely the right moment. The most impactful actions come with real consequences, often increasing police pressure or permanently sacrificing valuable resources. Timing is everything.

The police are not a passive obstacle, but a shared and dynamic threat. Their movement is directly influenced by player actions, meaning that every aggressive play risks exposing not just yourself, but potentially everyone else at the table.

Moonshineville rewards players who can read the board, read their opponents, and know when to stay quiet—and when to strike. Victory rarely comes from a flawless plan, but from smart decisions made under pressure, and from using the right action at exactly the wrong moment for someone else.

—description from the publisher

description:
Hangzhou is one of the most beautiful cities in mainland China, praised by poets  as "There is Paradise in Heaven; Suzhou and Hangzhou on Earth" for over a thousand years. Pay your respects to the charming locations around the city, and plan your next vacation with City Explorer: Hangzhou!

A number of Location Cards will be laid in a grid face-down on the table. Players will take turns secretly inspecting the cards, pushing a column to change the order, or taking a row as their travel plans.

When all players have taken a row of Location Cards and depart for travel, the game ends and players will compare travel plans to see who is the best city explorer of Hangzhou!

An abstract game (lightly themed as a 'King of the Mountain' style game in the Roman Coliseum), OM is played on a circle divided into 20 sections. Each player starts with 10 pieces. Rolling 2 dice (a Red die & a White die), the Red die forces each player to move a piece in the direction indicated, while the White die allows the player to move ANY piece in EITHER direction. If you can get 2 pieces on a section with 1 opponent, the opponent's piece is removed from the board. When you reduce your opponent to 2 pieces on the board you win!

—description from the designer
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
Drink or Die is a bluffing and set-collection card game for 2–5 players where rival alchemists brew mysterious potions and dare each other to drink them.
Try it now: https://tabletoplab25-hash.github.io/DRINK-OR-DIE/

The deck contains 90 cards across five elements: Fire 🔥, Ice 🧊, Light ✨, Shadow 💀, and Nature 🌿. Each card has a value of 1, 2, or 3. Elements form two opposing pairs — Fire vs Ice and Light vs Shadow — while Nature is a neutral wildcard that corrects imbalance but comes at a cost.
On your turn, one player creates a potion by placing two cards from their hand into a shared cauldron: one face-down (hidden) and one face-up (visible). Then, in clockwise order, each player either adds a card to the cauldron (face-up or face-down, respecting a balance rule) or drinks the potion, claiming all the cards for their personal collection. A player who has already drunk this round still participates by adding cards, influencing what the remaining players will be forced to swallow. If the cauldron ever reaches six cards, the next eligible player must drink — no choice.
After drinking, that player brews the next potion for the others. The round ends when everyone has drunk once, and hands are refilled to six cards. The game ends when the draw pile runs out.
Scoring rewards both volume and balance: your score equals the sum of all your card values, minus your Nature total, minus three points for every point of remaining elemental imbalance. Nature reduces your imbalance but subtracts from your raw score — and overshooting with too much Nature is just as punishing as having too little. The player with the most points wins.
With hidden information driving every decision, Drink or Die creates tense moments of social deduction at the table. Should you drink now while the potion looks safe, or wait for more cards and risk someone poisoning it? Can you trust the player who says "it's just a Nature 1" face-down? And once you've drunk, can you weaponize your remaining cards to sabotage an opponent's next potion?

Welcome to the high-speed sliding game where the challenge is to match patterns against the clock! Players compete to be the first to replicate one of the 60 template cards to win each round. As each new card is flipped, the race begins anew, testing your speed and precision. Prefer a more relaxed experience? Enjoy solo play to practice and improve your skills without the pressure of competition. This game offers both thrilling races and calm practice sessions, making it versatile for different play styles and skill levels.

—description from the publisher

Take your feathered feuds to new heights with Birds of Play! Discover three new expansions for Perch that can be mixed-and-matched. Chase the sun and moon, nibble on breadcrumbs, and splash into new territory with ponds.

The fight for the homestead just got fiercer!

In Seeds the Day: Bird feeders are spread across the homestead. Each bird that reaches the tile first will score a snack and 3 victory points scored immediately! While securing an early lead on the scoretrack is valuable, you'll want to keep an eye on how this might affect turn-order.

Points in the Sky: This expansion rewards long-term planning and strategic positioning of your flock! As the sun and moon tiles revolve around the homestead, they'll change the rewards at the locations they shine upon.

Pondemonium: Splash into Ponds with new scoring opportunities and creatures to control! Use breadcrumbs to attract birds from adjoining locations to new Pond tiles. Then, during scoring, outflank the other flocks by counting the birds on the Pond AND neighboring locations. Control the Frog or Perch pond to gain access to their asymmetric abilities and manipulate the birds on their pond.

This set also comes with 20 new hidden objective cards that highlight the unique mechanics of each expansion.

—description from the publisher

In De Veiling van Hashiban, players are goblin merchants competing in a secret auction house beneath the city of Hashiban. Each player begins with three hidden merchants, each interested in specific categories of fantastical objects – books, plants, weapons, curiosities and more. Over the course of the game, players acquire objects at auction in the hope of matching these secret demands while keeping their true interests hidden from the table.

On a turn, the active player first gains income, then chooses one of several actions. They can reveal a new object from the deck and hold an open auction, or they can offer a lot of cards from their hand and set the starting price themselves. In both cases, players openly bid with banknote cards, raising or passing until only one bidder remains. Players may negotiate, make side deals, or even combine their money to push up the price, as long as everyone involved agrees to the terms. Special actions include a lottery-style “Bonoloto” that allows players to exchange small bills for a chance at bigger ones and powerful weapons that can temporarily knock rivals out of an ongoing auction.

Whenever a player collects a set of objects that matches one of their hidden merchants, they may reveal that merchant by discarding the matching cards. Revealed merchants, as well as special share certificates tied to the public merchant on the table, provide ongoing income whenever certain categories of objects come up for sale. The first player to reveal all three of their merchants wins the game, having outbid, outbluffed and outmanoeuvred the other goblin traders at the Hashiban auction.

—description from the publisher

Ants is a trick-taking game for 2 players.  Players rearrange the hand they are dealt into cards they will follow with and cards they will lead with. They demarcate this with a "Queen" card. The Queen card is the only card that could be used to lead or follow.

The game lasts for a single hand, with the first player to win a trick that includes the Queen winning the game.

Mechanic GO!! is a trick-taking game for 3 to 5 players where the players are assembling a giant robot from the cards played to tricks.

At the start of each hand, the players secretly select one of the cards from their hand to set aside face down as their favorite color. Cards of this color in the final robot will be worth extra points for them.

The trick play is standard "must follow", with no trump suit. The winner of the trick chooses any one of the cards played and places it onto the scaffold of the giant robot which matches the value of the card. The player collects the other cards from the trick in a face down pile in front of them.

The game takes place in rounds of 4 tricks. After each round, there is a chance for adding a bonus part to the robot or the changing of favorite colors.

The player with the most points at the end of the game wins.

Dice in the Dirt is a two-player tactical skirmish game set in a grounded wartime environment, where small squads fight for control of a dense, modular battlefield. The game focuses on pressure, positioning, and moment-to-moment decision-making rather than large-scale combat or abstract scoring.

Each player commands a squad of six soldiers, with each soldier filling a distinct role. Soldiers activate through a shared blind-bag system rather than a fixed turn order, creating uncertainty and shifting momentum throughout the game. On activation, players spend limited action points to move, fire, use equipment, or manage suppression. Combat emphasizes controlling space and disrupting the opponent rather than simply eliminating units.

Suppression plays a central role in gameplay, limiting enemy movement and actions and forcing difficult choices under pressure. Because activations are unpredictable, players must constantly adapt their plans, balancing aggression with caution while managing the tempo of the fight.

The game is won by completing scenario objectives and denying the opponent control of key areas of the battlefield. Victory depends on effective use of each soldier’s role, careful positioning, and the ability to respond to changing conditions as the battle unfolds.

-description from designer

A Cold Case. An Unsolved Crime. Can You Crack the Cold Case? Follow the trail of evidence as it guides you through unexpected twists and turns, unraveling the truth, layer by layer, until you crack the case wide open!

Sandie, a sneakerhead and shoe collector, is in a whole heap of trouble. She has been charged with the theft of a priceless trainer from the Northern Sole exhibition in Manchester. Facing substantial jail time, the loss of her invaluable collection and a barrage of abuse online, she needs your help! To save Sandie, you must piece together the evidence and solve a series of puzzles to identify the suspects, the motives, and to catch the true criminal.

All pieces are designed to be fully immersive, with authentic, real-world objects. Delve into the depths of investigation as you sift through witness statements, incident reports, and a myriad of clues to narrow down the list of suspects.

Contents: Introduction Letter; 23 pieces of evidence

Monster Taking is a must follow trick-taking game for 2 players, with a player drafted trump suit. Each card has a certain number of pips on it, in addition to the rank.

If the 2 players play the same suit, the person who loses the trick must give their opponent a number of cards from their hand equal to the difference of the pips on the cards in the trick. If the cards are of different suits, then the loser of the tricks discards such cards rather than giving them to their opponent.

The first person to run out of cards in their hand loses.

Pickleball Smash is a simple, easy to learn game that has players collecting dinks, blocks, smashes, or the all coveted ATP (around the Post) to create tricks equaling 11 points.

During setup, each player is dealt 5 cards, 1 card is placed face up in a discard pile, and the remaining cards form a draw pile.  Each player's turn starts by drawing a card from either the draw pile or discard pile.  Then the player Does one of four actions:

     Play a special card
     Play a trick
     Discard a card
     Pass


A trick consists of three of the same cards.  The value of the trick is based on the value of the cards in the trick.  The first player to play enough tricks to equal exactly 11 points wins.

In Solar Supremacy, players command rival Earth-based factions vying for control of the solar system, building engines of industry, colonizing hostile worlds, and waging tactical space and ground warfare to earn Supremacy Points.
​
Players expand across a multi-planet map, develop settlements into Outposts, Major Cities, Advanced Cities, and Army Bases, unlock Logistics Cubes to scale their economy, and deploy armies, turrets, and dreadnoughts to secure territory and key objectives.
​
Supremacy Points come from planetary hegemony links, advanced infrastructure, military achievements (destroying dreadnoughts and defeating heroes), and exploration objectives, with victory going to the first player to reach 15 SP and hold it until the start of their next turn.

—description from the designer

Storm Front is a tabletop battle game rulebook set in a frozen world where survival depends on movement, timing, and risk.

Theme
Warbands fight in the Survivable Zone, a narrow belt of land where a living storm roams the battlefield. The Storm leaves behind storm crystals—dangerous shards of power that can heal wounds, fuel decisive strikes, or drive desperate bursts of speed. But greed has consequences: the more crystals that gather, the more likely ice wyrms will rise from beneath the frozen ground.

Combat is fast and decisive. Wounds reduce a figure’s strength, retreats matter, and positioning is critical. Victory comes not from holding territory, but from knowing when to press forward, when to fall back, and when to risk everything for one more crystal.

Key Features of Play

     A moving hazard that reshapes the battlefield: The Storm advances every round, forcing constant repositioning.
     Resource-driven tactics: Storm crystals create hard choices between offense, defense, healing, and mobility.
     Fast, decisive combat: Wounds reduce a figure’s strength, retreats matter, and fights resolve quickly.
     Unpredictable threats: Ice wyrms punish overcommitment and reward smart risk management.
     Simple core rules, high tension: Easy to learn, but full of meaningful decisions every turn.

Each player has a number of bait tokens and a pawn of a color. Each places their pawn on the corner space of their color and a bait token on the start space of their fishing track. Place 5 fish tokens on any 5 of the 6 numbered oval spaces in the center of the board (leaving 1 space empty). On turn, spin the spinner (1-6) and move that many spaces. If you land on a purple circle (space), you have a nibble – advance your bait 1 space on the track. If you land on a yellow circle, spin again, and, if you spin even, advance your bait 1. If you land on a pink circle, your line is snagged – move your bait back one space. If you land on a brown circle, a turtle stole your bait – place your bait token in the middle of the board and put a new bait token on your start space. If you land on a corner circle, spin again. If it is the corner of your color, advance your bait 1 space. If your bait reaches your net, you have caught a fish. Place your bait in the middle of the board and put another one on your start space. Now spin again and take the fish of that number. If there is none, try again next turn. Player with the most fish when all fish are caught wins. If tied, player with the least bait in the pool wins.

Board shows mini-track on left with 7 spaces leading to the School of Pawsitivity and larger track on right with two holes in it to store Caring Cat tokens. Game includes 4 cat mover/standees, 1 Mean Mouse mover, 20 Caring Cat tokens (4 each for 5 different cats), 20 Heart/Charm tokens, and 30 cards, each showing an area around town and a situation (e.g. a person in the hospital).

Game 1: Race to Charrm. Each player takes 3 random Caring Cat tokens and a cat marker and places the marker any empty space on the large track. Place the remaining Caring Cat tokens in the hole in the street (board). The mouse marker and side track are not used. On turn, roll the die. If it shows a 1-5, move that many spaces. If it shows the mouse, lose your turn. If you land on a Sun space, take a Caring Cat token from the hole in the street. If you land on a dirt heap, return a Caring Cat token in the hole. If you land on a path space, do nothing. If you land on a card with a heart, reveal a card then play a Caring Cat token, descrbe somethign kind that cat would do in the pictured situation, and take a charm token. Each cat has a different characteristic (e.g. Chattychoo uses kind words while GoodyGum gives gifts). First player to colect 5 charms wins.

Game 2: Charm Together. Same as above, except this game uses the side track and Mouse and players play cooperatirvely. The mouse starts on the first space of the side track and move 1 step toward the school each time a Mouse is rolled. players win if they collectively collect 5 charm tokens before Mean Mouse reaches the school.

—user summary

Arcanists’ Array is a two-player strategy game about shaping probability and outthinking your opponent. Two rival arcanists face off in a quiet laboratory, activating powerful spell arrays through carefully prepared magical dice. Instead of rolling standard dice, players rebuild their dice faces every round, turning randomness into something that can be planned, bent, and exploited.

Each round begins with both players secretly choosing and ordering spell arrays that define how the round might be won. Before any dice are rolled, players simultaneously decide how many dice to use and how to configure them, all behind a screen. Dice are then rolled openly, and spell arrays resolve in a fixed order. Because most arrays care about all dice on the table, your opponent’s dice often matter as much as your own. Stronger arrays can override weaker ones, while failed or tied effects may push the round forward, creating tense layers of prediction and counterplay.

Players share a tug-of-war style life track, and the goal is to push it completely to the opponent’s side. Winning a round forces a meaningful choice: directly interfere with the opponent by taking and exchanging dice faces, or grow your own options by gaining access to more spell arrays. There is no guaranteed best move—playing too safely can be punished, and playing too boldly can backfire. Success depends on reading intentions, managing risk, and sometimes choosing to be unpredictable on purpose.

—description from the designer

A flip-and-write game in which we take on the role of a medieval King ruling his kingdom. The game is a direct sequel to Knight, shifting the focus from the deeds of a single warrior to the personal decisions of the monarch himself. Every royal choice - political, military, or economic - has a direct and far-reaching impact on the entire realm and shapes its future.

During the game, the King faces complex challenges affecting the whole kingdom, such as declaring and conducting wars, expanding borders, managing internal politics, appointing or removing lords, negotiating alliances, suppressing rebellions, and responding to crises like plague, famine, drought, or devastating floods. Each scenario emphasizes different aspects of rulership and requires the player to adapt their strategy to changing circumstances.

In each turn, the player draws 6 cards that determine the events of that turn. These cards define the available actions, provide a specific pool of resources, and indicate which actions are currently active. In solo mode, they also determine the consequences of decisions and the behavior of the automa. A major new element is the introduction of Advisor cards - influential figures at court whose unique abilities significantly affect strategy. Choosing and managing advisors is crucial, as they can strongly shape the direction of the kingdom and open powerful tactical and strategic options.

The game is played over 2 rounds, each consisting of 6 turns. After every 3 turns, a Goal card is revealed, introducing new conditions that define rewards and penalties based on the King’s achievements during that phase. This creates shifting priorities and forces the player to make difficult decisions as the state of the kingdom evolves.

The player wins by fulfilling the objectives defined by the selected scenario. There are always multiple paths to victory - for example, achieving a certain number of victory points, securing full control of the map, maintaining high stability and prosperity, or winning a required number of key battles. The player can also lose before the game ends if any of the scenario’s loss conditions are met, such as the collapse of royal authority, a successful rebellion, the enemy conquering the entire realm, or the fall of the capital due to invasion or neglect.

—description from the designer

Become a courier delivering fish can orders in a lively, lighthearted setting where cats constantly interfere with your plans. Players collect fish cans to match order values, while Greedy Cats eat away at your progress and Thug Cats block depots and harass opponents. The theme supports a playful sense of competition and mild mischief, suitable for families and children.

On your turn, you choose between pushing your luck by revealing cards from a shared grid to build your total of fish cans, or securing progress by delivering completed orders from your depot. Revealing more cards increases your chances to claim higher-value orders, but overshooting the highest available order causes your turn to fail. Clever timing—knowing when to stop and claim or when to continue—is the core tension of the game.

Interaction is direct but approachable: players can steal unprotected orders from each other, and Thug Cats can be placed in opponents’ depots to limit their capacity. Certain low-value orders provide protection, adding a layer of tactical choice between safety and higher rewards. The balance between risk, interference, and opportunity creates varied outcomes each play.

The game ends immediately when a player successfully delivers three orders. The winner is the player with the most money from delivered orders. In case of a tie, the player who delivered more orders wins; if still tied, the victory is shared.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: 三分鐘三國 (Three Minutes Three Kingdoms)
description:
Each player has cards numbered 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, and 8. Everyone plays a card simultaneously to compare values. At the start, one card is placed face down to determine how many points you score for each win—and if you win three times in a row or more, there’s an extra reward!

The basic rules are simple and the game can be played in just three minutes. It’s great for playing with children or people who don’t often play board games, offering light strategic thinking mixed with a bit of mind games in card play.

You can also use advanced rules with card effects: only look at a selected set of cards and take turns hiring them, adding variety while staying easy to learn.

Once you’re familiar with the game, you can even build your own deck—create your own all-star team and duel with friends!

每個人有345678的牌，同時出牌比大小，而一開始蓋一張牌決定贏一次得幾分，如果連贏三次以上還有獎勵！ 
基本規則簡單三分鐘就可玩。可以跟兒童或不常玩桌遊的人玩，體驗簡單策略思考加出牌心機。
可以用包含效果的進階規則，只看挑出來的牌並輪流聘僱，增添變化又能快速上手。
熟悉以後還能自己組牌，建構自己的明星隊跟朋友決鬥！

Paris, 1920 – In the city of love, a young woman receives presents from an anonymous admirer. Are you able to understand their meaning? But most of all, can you uncover the identity of the person behind all this?
Level: ●●●○○

echoes is a co-operative audio mystery game series. Using the free app, players listen to mysterious noises and voices that are connected to the playing material. Together they look for hints in the sound bites to bring the 24 story parts in order and solve the case.

In more detail, your task is to assemble the 24 parts of the story in the right order; each part is represented by an object — either a game board or a card — and its associated echo. The entire story is divided into six chapters, with each chapter being represented by a game board. By scanning the objects with the app, you'll hear the echo connected with each object, and by using conversations and noises in the echo, you try to figure out which three object cards are associated with a chapter and in which order they should be placed. If you're not correct, the app will inform you which cards are incorrect or in the wrong place; if you are correct, you can listen to the entire chapter all at once — and you should since new, additional hints can then be heard.

The objects themselves also provide you with clues from time to time, so neglect no details.

This is a game with just 1 card!
A square card with the numbers 1,2,4,3 on the face.
The player with the most confidence in math becomes the starting player.
The starting player looks at the card's face, secretly chooses one number, pinching that corner with their finger, and flips the card face down.
The opponent looks at the back, chooses one of the remaining three corners to pinch.
Then, the card is flipped, revealing the numbers each player has pinched.
The player with the larger number wins, with the exception of 1 beating 4. The winner scores the "opponent's pinched number."
Players take turns being the starting player, repeating the process until one player reaches 6 points, winning the game.

boardgame: MAGI CHAIN -知恵と神秘の冒険譚-
description:
MAGI CHAIN is a competitive board game for 1 to 4 players. Players take on the role of apprentice magicians, advancing their pieces while utilizing special card plays called “Chains” and powerful supporters called “Spirits”, aiming to collect more victory points than other players by the end of the game.

Victory points are earned by fighting monsters, delivering contracts to cities, learning certain spells, or leveling up your apprentice magician.

Whether you prioritize movement for efficient paper delivery, relentlessly defeat monsters for bold victory point gains, or skillfully manage spirits and your deck for technical point-scoring—enjoy a richly varied adventure simulation each playthrough, shaped by the board's state, your strategy, and synergies with your spirits.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
As one of the Diadochi, heir of Alexander the Great, you must control territories, but above all, gain legitimacy by controlling the royal family and defeating your opponents in battle.
Recruit armies with pezhetairoi, hetairoi, and elephants. And also fleets of penteras. Move them across the Macedonian empire to complete missions or engage other Diadochi. Place allied garrisons in the most useful key territories. And make short-lived pacts to gain the legitimacy necessary to seize a significant portion of the empire.
The game uses a card engine that is improved during gameplay through a deckbuilding system. Dice are used in combat to add excitement, and random events are triggered, providing objectives for the diadochi.

—description from the designer
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

description:
Tsukimi is a beautiful two-player strategy game inspired by the Japanese moon-viewing festival, where rival rabbit clans gather on the Moon to celebrate, compete, and share sweet mochis. Beneath the calm, poetic atmosphere lies a sharp tactical duel, as players fight for the most coveted spots to enjoy the festival under the glowing autumn full moon.

Each turn, you choose and place mochi tiles, positioning and maneuvering your rabbits to surround them and claim their value. Every placement matters: manage your limited rabbits, disrupt your opponent’s plans, and create clever tile combinations to gain the upper hand. When the Moon is complete, the clan that has secured the most valuable mochis wins, proving that even the most peaceful celebrations reward the smartest strategy.

—description from the publisher

You are part of the crew of the space ship USCN KRONOS. The ship is on fire, and there is a massive predatory alien hunting you through the corridors. The captain is panicking, the pilot is injured, and the ship's cat is missing. You must help them all, unlock the lifeboat, and get to it before the alien becomes enraged and kills you.

Escape the Kronos is a single-player card game that you play in your hand. Print out the 20 cards provided, pick one of 4 game modes, and follow the actions to move them and turn them to escape the alien and move through ship modules. Escape the Kronos takes seconds to set up and minutes to play. All the instructions come on one sheet you can print and fold yourself. Inspired by the graphic design of Ron Cobb on the original Aline film, the gameplay of Escape the Kronos is described on symbols along the edges of each card.

—description from the publisher

Crime Places: Das Sanatorium is a narrative-driven mystery game set in a long-abandoned psychiatric hospital. Decades after a school friend named Solveig vanished, the investigation returns to this forgotten place, where crumbling rooms and fragmented records reveal that her disappearance is tied to other, darker crimes. The theme centers on lost places, suppressed memories, and the unsettling realization that the building itself holds clues to a broader truth.

The gameplay combines crime investigation with escape-room-style puzzles using a deck of cards. Players explore the sanatorium room by room, reading short narrative texts, studying illustrations, and uncovering clues that must be compared and connected. Progress depends on observation and deduction rather than complex rules or randomness. The cards remain intact, making the experience replayable.

To win the game, players must correctly interpret the collected evidence and reconstruct what truly happened. Victory comes from identifying meaningful links between clues, understanding the sequence of events, and drawing the right conclusions about the crimes connected to the sanatorium. The case is solved only when the final explanation fits all the evidence discovered throughout the investigation.

Straight out of that mint tin in your pocket, let’s expand the Cosmos. In Existence, every game you play is the unique story of a new Universe, from its birth to its death, and of your forms of Life inside of it. Your goal: have these survive until the end of time. 
So you players will run the Laws of Nature. You will condensate matter and energy out of the Big Bang, into a variety of dynamically evolving stellar systems. You will create life. Yes, you will struggle to expand across interstellar distances, while being constantly threatened by the cataclysmic lifecycle of the stars. Yes, your life forms will be challenged by cosmic events, nearby aliens and the cooling of the Universe. Yes, every time one of you will play, a billion of years will flow. And now you understand that in this ever-going quest for energy, the competition with the other forms of life and the risks of planetary collapse will soon leave you and your lifeforms restless. To be honest, there is a real doubt whether even a single one of you will be adaptive enough to have his or her species survive. Will you want to join forces to make it through ? Will you betray your interstellar neighbors so as to guarantee your victory? Maybe both. In any case, this will be no picnic!
Developed within the framework of a Master in Philosophy of Science, EXISTENCE, Life & the Universe is initially a mind experiment to explore our possible futures at astronomical scales. This interestingly led by accident to a unique mechanism for the gameplay, in which tactics and long run strategy meet negotiation, while allowing the emergence of an incredible variety of interesting situations for endless replays.

—description from the designer

Crime Places: Bar der Dämonen is a dark, psychological mystery game set in an abandoned bar deep in the woods. Years after a brutal series of murders, players return to the scene alongside a notorious criminal to uncover what truly happened that night. The theme blends crime investigation with elements of occult horror, exploring demons both literal and psychological, unreliable memories, and the creeping doubt of whether the horrors uncovered are real events or twisted hallucinations tied to the place itself.

Gameplay focuses on investigation and deduction through a deck of cards. Players explore the bar and its surroundings by revealing cards, reading narrative fragments, examining unsettling illustrations, and collecting clues that must be analyzed and connected. Escape-room-style puzzles challenge players to recognize patterns and contradictions while gradually revealing the story. The rules are simple and unobtrusive, keeping attention on atmosphere, reasoning, and storytelling. The cards remain intact, allowing the game to be replayed.

To win the game, players must piece together the evidence and determine what actually occurred during the murders at the bar. Success depends on correctly interpreting clues, separating illusion from reality, and forming a coherent explanation that accounts for all discoveries. The game is won when the final conclusions align with the full body of evidence uncovered throughout the investigation.

Flipchain is a two-player game played on a hexagonal board of any size, initially empty.
Discs are colored red on one side and blue on the other side. Mark Steere designed Flipchain in December 2025.

The two players, Red and Blue, take turns placing discs onto unoccupied cells on the board, with their own color face up, one disc per turn, starting with Red. Immediately following your placement, you must flip zero or more enemy discs to your color, concluding your turn. Voluntary passing is not allowed, but if you don't have any legal placements available, you skip your turn. Flipchain uses the pie rule.

Your placed disc must form at least as many adjacencies with enemy discs as with friendly discs.

Player creates path, a path is a single width sequence of one or more discs (of either or both colors) with corners of at most 120 degrees

A loop is a closed path.

To win the game players must form a loop of their color.

Alianeados is a one-card game by Pulga Escapista.

Each player secretly chooses one alien and name it out loud. Players take turns saying different alien names in order to discard options. Until they are ready to guess with one is the original.

Primera y Última is a one-card game by Pulga Escapista.

A player grabs the card choosing a starting letter and then offers it to a second player who chooses the finishing letter and must quickly say a word that correctly uses both!

Operación D-2 is a one-card game by Pulga Escapista.

This complex spaceship needs to be quickly managed by two pilots at the same time, quickly prepare your fingers, when one player presses a button the other must press on the same spot to maintain the card balanced, if it falls you lose!

boardgame: Team of The Dead 〜オバケのチームでおいはらえ！〜
description: Balancing management costs is important in team building, but many managers continue to follow bad patterns without considering costs. In this game, managers and team members are like ghosts, and the goal is to build a team. Win by hiring and training players to effectively reduce management burden while accelerating organizational progress. Experience the challenge of team building for yourself.

Kids' version of P for Pizza, a quick-thinking category game, where the first player to collect 6 of the triangular 'pizza slice' cards and completes their pizza wins.

The game box contains 100 triangular cards which have 3 letters printed against each edge on one side and a category (e.g. 'something green', 'Musical instrument', or 'Rhymes with frog') on the other. The exception to this is some special 'Pizza Time' cards, which don't have a category - the category is pizza toppings.

A stack of around 30 cards are placed in the middle of the table, with the category side up. Three cards are then turned over and placed around the stack, letter-side up. Players then have to shout out a word matching the category that starts with any of the three letters facing it. Whoever says a correct answer quickest wins the card containing the letter they just used. This card becomes a 'slice' of their own pizza. The category card is then flipped into the vacant position, letter-side up, and the game continues.
family: Versions & Editions: Junior Versions of Grown-Up Games

boardgame: バッティン xトリテ (Battin x Torite)
description:
In Battin x Torite, the deck of cards used consists of 6 suits, each with a different range of ranks, and 2 copies of each cards.

The game is "must follow" trick-taking with a trump suit. If a person plays a card of the same rank as it already present in the trick, the previous player with that rank takes any card from their hand and gives it to that player, face down in front of them (not in the trick).

At the end of the game, a players earns points equal to the numbers of cards they took in a suit multiplied by the number of face down cards they have.
publishers: Jumbo Cat ( ジャンボキャット)

description:
This upgrade pack for Ice and Idols expands the base game with another asymmetric character, plus new pathways to traverse the temple!

NEW CHARACTER – The Collectors
This dynamic duo’s skill allows them to carry up to 3 unique idols at once.
 NEW PATHWAY TILES – Teleportation
These new pathway tiles allow adventurers to teleport from one space to another, adding a new layer of strategy when collecting idols, reaching altars, and avoiding obstacles.

Shake up your game of Santorini with new modes of play that use the minis from Riddle of the Sphinx!

Modes include The Sphinx's Game, Draconian Law, and The Wanderer.

The Santorini Promo Pack includes 4 Synth Santorini cards.

boardgame: När då då?: Konst, hantverk och design
description:
In När då då?, players take turns taking cards and forming a timeline with ten different events from the beginning of the era until today. Put events, people, places or gadgets in the right order of time.

This edition of the game focusses on art, craft and design.

Soylent Ghoul is a horror skirmish game. 
It needs lots of terrain, urban terrain is particularly good, some d8s, a whole lot of tokens, some specially marked and some minis representing twisted ghouls.

Soylent Ghoul is inspired by the stories of Thomas Ligotti, the artwork of Zdzislaw Beksinski and the worlds of Tokyo Ghoul, Soylent Green, and Winwood and Cord.

Play revolves around representing the hidden movements of ghouls in the shadows, and building panic and horror in other characters. Each ghoul has strange powers, but the greatest power comes from getting players to react at the wrong time, in the wrong place, and build their panic and pressure.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Ain el-Gazala 1942: Lo scontro di Knightsbridge
description:
The Battle of Knightsbridge was an engagement of the Battle of Ain el-Gazala that took place on 10 June 1942. In this episode, British forces advancing south to intercept the Deutsches Afrikakorps were halted and driven back by the Italian Ariete Armoured Division south of the junction between the track to Bir Hacheim and the Trigh Capuzzo.

"Ain el-Gazala 1942. Lo scontro di Knightsbridge" is a easy and accessible WW2 hex-and-counter wargame for two players, focused on the fight between British and Italian tanks. Maneuver your tank squad, encircle, and destroy your opponent within 6 turns.

---
Lo scontro di Knightsbridge fu un episodio della battaglia di Ain el-Gazala avvenuto il 10 giugno del 1942. In tale episodio le forze britanniche, che muovevano verso sud per intercettare il Deutsches Afrikakorps, furono bloccate e respinte dalla Divisione corazzata italiana Ariete a sud dell’incrocio fra la pista per Bir Hacheim e il Trigh Capuzzo.

"Ain el-Gazala 1942. Lo scontro di Knightsbridge” è un wargame sulla Seconda Guerra Mondiale semplice e veloce con esagoni e pedine per due giocatori, incentrato sui combattimenti tra carri armati britannici e italiani. Manovra la tua squadra di carri, accerchia e distruggi il tuo avversario entro sei turni.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

boardgame: Crumbs!: The Sandwich Filler Game – Full Stomachs Promo Pack
Crumbs!: The Sandwich Filler Game – Secret Ingredient Promo Card
description:
"Your cafe has that special something that customers keep coming back for. It's so good they don't even notice if something is wrong with their order!"

The Secret Ingredient card is a 'wild card', and can be used to replace a missing filling or to replace a mistakenly added one, still satisfying the customer's order. Use it to snatch victory from the jaws of defeat or ease yourself into higher difficulties.

All the Best "Worst" Jokes.  The goal of the game is to match jokes to their punch lines by the end of the game.  Along the way, you will either laugh or groan your way through the very best of the worst dad jokes!

Fantasy adventure patrons compete with one another to see who can secure the most gold from the nearby dungeon.  They will send parties of foolhardy adventures into a dungeon to face different challenges.  There are Quests, Traps, and Monsters to confront.  But the patrons themselves will not risk their lives and souls.  After all, that dungeon is a Deathtrap!

Featuring variable systems for resolving different challenges, Deathtrap allows players to take on variable levels of randomness from Combat dice rolling to Traps where you push your luck and branching Quests which require timing and precision.  Heroes are hired at the inn and equipped for their expeditions.  When they return, they gain levels and look to join other expeditions, now able to command greater shares of the loot.

—description from the designer

In Five, 2-6 players race to roll their dice in order to match patterns found on different cards. Over 10 rounds, the last player will gain 0 points and at the end, the player with the most points will win.
Players face a series of questions and mini-challenges on different topics, from chemistry to technology, to earn tokens and reach the finish line first.

PLACE - FLIP - CONTROL

Every battle has two sides… literally!

Place your tiles, flip the battlefield, and outthink your opponent in this fast, tactical 1-on-1 showdown!

With multiple game modes - from all-out warfare to capture the flag and tight close-quarters skirmishes - no two battles play the same.

Easy to learn, quick to play, and dangerously addictive.

Flip fast. Think faster.

The Maltese Falcon Card Game is part of PUG’s Tomes & Tables line of varied games derived from classic books, this asymmetric role, hidden deduction, treasure hunter is a game for 3-5 players designed by Joel Sparks (Cats of Catthulhu, Harvest Hoppers).

Up to 5 gritty characters move among the noir locations of 1920s San Francisco, searching for the real version of the precious Maltese Falcon statue. A unique fold-out playing board of poker-sized cards encompasses the Office, the Hotel Room, the Alley, the Apartment, and the Docks.

Each movement card has both a location and a character on it. Each turn, the player uses one movement card to go either to that location or to where that character is—including playing yourself to stay where you are!

Then the character takes a single action: searching, stealing, revealing a Gun or a Falcon. Actions can only affect another character if they are in the same location.

Meanwhile, the police are closing in, and someone is going to jail for murder. When the Fuzz card turns up, whoever is caught with the murder charge gets hauled away and can’t win. A remaining character with the real Falcon statue would win; if no one has it, it’s a matter of money and freedom.

—description from the designer

Space Nexus is a strategic board game set in a modular galaxy where players pilot ships through a constantly shifting network of space tiles. The galaxy is built from hex-based quadrants, creating a unique map every game filled with hazards, shortcuts, and powerful cosmic anomalies. Players must navigate asteroid fields, space lanes, wormholes, and other environmental effects while adapting to the evolving layout of the board.

On each turn, players roll for movement, maneuver their ships through connected quadrants, and use Explore cards to modify movement, overcome obstacles, or gain tactical advantages. As ships traverse the galaxy, players interact with Nexus locations that grant special abilities or rewards, encouraging both careful planning and opportunistic play. The game supports multiple modes, including competitive and cooperative variants, offering different strategic experiences and levels of player interaction.

Victory conditions depend on the selected game mode. In competitive play, players race to complete objectives by reaching key locations while strategically blocking or outpacing their opponents. In cooperative play, players must work together to achieve shared goals before escalating threats overwhelm the galaxy. Winning Space Nexus requires efficient movement, smart use of cards and abilities, and the flexibility to adapt to both the map and the actions of other players.

—description from the publisher

In Whiskey Times: Still Famous, you're hosting the most unusual whiskey tasting in history—your guests are dead, opinionated, and expecting their favourite pours. Historical figures from Churchill to Chaplin have arrived with specific drink preferences, and your reputation depends on whether you can serve them the perfect whiskey in the right glass with exactly the ingredients they demand. But gathering premium bottles is difficult, and sometimes a good substitute from the right region has to do.
The core tension comes from managing limited resources across multiple competing goals. Acquiring whiskey bottles consumes your entire turn's drawing power, while glassware and mixers come cheaper but are equally essential. You'll navigate dynamic markets that shift whenever players swap guests or gift bottles, creating a constantly evolving puzzle of availability and timing. Event cards shuffled into the resource deck add welcome windfalls and frustrating setbacks, keeping everyone on their toes as they race to complete their drink recipes.
Strategic depth emerges from the perfect-versus-substitute scoring system. Rushing to finish with regional substitutes might get you across the finish line first, but a player who carefully times their perfect pours and stacks flavour profile bonuses can score significantly higher. Victory tokens from gifting whiskeys to dismiss difficult guests add another strategic layer—sometimes it's worth abandoning a demanding historical figure for someone more accommodating. The game rewards players who can read the market flow, time their whiskey acquisitions, and balance speed against quality scoring.
Victory goes to the highest score once any player completes all their guests with at least one perfect drink served. This "highest score wins" condition means the player who triggers the endgame isn't guaranteed victory—careful optimization and bonus maximization throughout the game matter more than simply finishing first. With historical flavour text, educational whiskey region distinctions, and a tone that's irreverent without being disrespectful to the dead, Whiskey Times offers strategic hand management wrapped in an entertaining historical premise.

—description from the designer

Godmaker is a rogue-lite fantasy campaign game with similarities to action RPGs like Path of Exile or Diablo. An app tracks numerous stats including players level, skills, equipment, injuries, etc., crunches the numbers and at the beginning of each mission gives the players simple settings to adjust on their player boards.

A unique dice system provides points to players who can spend them on several dice of the size (d6, d8, and d12) and type (body, spirit, and mind) as best fits their style of play.

Unlike many games of this nature, encounters are short - about 30 minutes, and players level after each one. A single campaign lasts for a game year, during which time the heroes accomplish as much as they can. Travel, resting, and injuries (which occur aplenty!) all chip at the limited time. Players race against the clock, accomplishing what they can before the lich and his undead minions sweep the land clean at year's end and the players must begin anew.

But not from scratch! Heroes eliminated by the lich are added to the Pantheon and, depending on their level, skills and fame points gained during the past year, will benefit new heroes during the next year's campaign. Campaigns repeat until heroes become godlike and are able to defeat the lich!

—description from the publisher

boardgame: ダムカードバトル FNAWIPSR (Dam Card Battle FNAWIPSR)
description:
In Dam Card Battle FNAWIPSR, the players are playing a trick-taking game that uses souvenir dam card style dam cards.

In each trick, the players will play 2 cards - a rule card and a dam card. The rule cards (e.g. "longest length of dam crest") will determine which card will win the trick, and the values of the dams will determine the winner.

The player who wins the most tricks wons.

Bloink is a tower-building game that uses unconventional wooden pieces. Test your skills and decision-making abilities in this exciting game of balance and strategy! Start playing in less than five minutes. Choose pieces for your opponents while they choose for you, use steady hands and be the LAST TOWER STANDING!

In Toritemon, the players are playing a trick-taking game, aiming to win 0 or 2 tricks. The game is a "must follow" game, with a randomly determined trump suit.

Players that do not win a trick, increase a special gauge. Before or after a player plays a card, they can use the value of this gauge to play an odd card from their hand as a "special tool". These tools have special effects, such s changing the value of cards, etc.

The player with the most points after a few rounds wins.

boardgame: Kemet: Blood and Sand – The Great Old Ones
description:
With The Great Old Ones Game Up, eldritch entities are invading the dark lands of Kemet! This expansion allows you to swap the Thoth and Apophis miniatures from the Book of the Dead expansion with none other than Cthulhu and Nyarlathotep. This is a cosmetic game up, as the two Mythos entities have the same effects as their Egyptian counterparts from The Book of the Dead.

In the fantasy world of Kemet: Blood and Sand, the Ancient Egypt classic gods are not the only ones to be worshiped. Darker and scarier gods are the focus of unholy cults: the great Cthulhu is worshiped on the Nile shores, whereas the name of Nyarlathotep, the Black Pharaoh, is whispered in the far desert.

boardgame: Primal: The Awakening – Heart of the Wild
description:
"It is said the First Men could not comprehend the Voice of the Primordials.
All they felt was the rhythm of a deep breath, or a pulse, as if the world itself were alive. Their chants, carried by drums, brought them closer to the heart of the wild."

Two new Hunters join the hunt: Zaraya and Drusk.
Each brings unique mechanics and playstyles, offering a wide range of new tactics and strategies to experiment with.

The Spear Swift in movement, lethal in execution. A single piercing strike is enough to drive a monster’s heart into its fate.
Zaraya focuses on maneuvers, mobility, and precision strikes. Her Pierce mechanic lets her unleash devastating blows, but the spear also offers tactical control and defensive options to protect her fellow Hunters.

The Wardrum As long as the Wardrum thunders, the blood of hunters burns with fury. Will your chant rise louder than the Voice of the Dragon?
Drusk is a unique hunter who drives his companions forward with his powerful rhythm, amplifying their attacks while providing healing and control. The greater the rhythm he builds through his distinctive discard mechanic, the stronger the boosts he grants to his fellow hunters.

On a mysterious night in the enchanted forest, the witches are brewing their magical potions. But suddenly, a cockroach appears among the cauldrons! If it falls into a brew, the potion is spoiled.

Use your magic wand to find the right magical ingredients. The player who collects the most points by successfully brewing potions wins the game. Keep track of the location of the powerful crystal or the spider's web and be quick to correct the chaos.

Make sure you keep an eye on the cockroach: if it falls into a cauldron, the potion is spoiled. Quickly remove the cockroach from the cauldron with your magic wand and collect the corresponding cockroach chip. If you manage to combine the right ingredients and collect the most points, you will be the best sorcerer's apprentice in the forest!
family: Components: Game Box Used In Play

boardgame: Good Face Bad Face: Greedy Face Promo
description: This promo card is shuffled in the deck before the game (instead of the one of the Good Faces). A player can hide Greedy Face in his/her stack to steal the card from another player's party. If a player turns over a Greedy Face, they must give the stack owner one of the cards from their party.

CliQ is a fast-paced, push-your-luck card game where every turn you’ll add colourful cards to your board and chase the highest score you can. But beware - draw a card you can’t place and you’ll bust, dropping to zero for the round. Still, a bad round isn’t always the end: clever strategy, special cards, and a dash of luck (or sheer recklessness…) might just swing the game back in your favour.

The sound-and-light roulette spins—on which letter will it stop?
Draw category cards: dessert, city, wild animal…
How many words containing the chosen letter can you come up with in 30 seconds?
Beep, beep, beep—TIME!

This fast-paced and thrilling word game draws in players of all ages. Playing together brings joy and helps expand vocabulary.

The game includes wooden candies in different colors, and the objective is to collect a certain number of candies according to the agreed scoring system.
The game has five different level options that you can choose from to achieve the right level of difficulty. The game teaches you to recognise and name colours and form secondary colours, as well as practice counting in the form of different point counting methods. This game also develops the child’s perceptual ability as well as the ability to concentrate.
Players roll colour dice in all level options and then collect candies with the matching colours.

Temática: La Gruta de la Condena presenta una fantasía oscura y brutal. El jugador encarna al Portador de la Aniquilación, un guerrero condenado a abrirse paso por una gruta maldita infestada de demonios hematófagos, cuyo señor final es Haima. No hay espacio para la piedad ni la huida: el avance solo es posible mediante la destrucción absoluta de todo lo que habita el lugar. La ambientación enfatiza la violencia, la fatalidad y la sensación de estar atrapado en un ciclo de sangre y condena eterna.

Jugabilidad: El juego es una experiencia en solitario basada en cartas y tiradas de 1d6. En cada turno se revela una carta de Gruta que puede otorgar objetos, no tener efecto o forzar la revelación de cartas de Condena, donde aparecen monstruos u otras situaciones. El jugador gestiona un pequeño inventario de objetos que modifican las tiradas y ofrecen efectos tácticos, como evitar combates, curarse o manipular los mazos. El combate es el núcleo del juego y se resuelve acción por acción, eligiendo entre atacar, usar objetos o no hacer nada, siempre con el riesgo de perder Corazones si se falla o se duda.

Cómo se gana: Para ganar la partida es necesario agotar completamente el mazo de Gruta y resolver todas las cartas de Condena reveladas, lo que implica sobrevivir a todos los combates y eventos sin que los Corazones del jugador lleguen a cero. La derrota es inmediata si el jugador pierde todos sus Corazones en cualquier momento. La clave del éxito está en gestionar bien los objetos, asumir riesgos calculados y resistir hasta que la Gruta, finalmente purgada de toda vida, permita la salida.

The Grotto of Doom presents a dark and brutal fantasy. The player takes on the role of the Bearer of Annihilation, a warrior condemned to fight their way through a cursed grotto infested with blood-drinking demons, whose ultimate master is Haima. There is no room for mercy or escape: progress is only possible through the absolute destruction of everything that inhabits the place. The setting emphasizes violence, fatalism, and the feeling of being trapped in a cycle of bloodshed and eternal damnation.

Gameplay:
The game is a solo experience based on cards and 1d6 rolls. Each turn, a Grotto card is revealed, which may grant items, have no effect, or force the revelation of Doom cards, where monsters or other situations appear. The player manages a small inventory of items that modify dice rolls and provide tactical effects, such as avoiding combat, healing, or manipulating the decks. Combat is the core of the game and is resolved action by action, with the player choosing between attacking, using items, or doing nothing—always at the risk of losing Hearts if they fail or hesitate.

How to Win:
To win the game, the Grotto deck must be completely exhausted and all revealed Doom cards resolved, which means surviving every combat and event without the player’s Hearts ever reaching zero. Defeat is immediate if the player loses all their Hearts at any point. The key to success lies in careful item management, taking calculated risks, and enduring until the Grotto—finally purged of all life—allows an escape

—description from the designer (English)

boardgame: Merde! War Game set in the Napoleonic era: From the Peninsular War to Waterloo
description:
Merde! From the Peninsular War to Waterloo, 
Merde! completes the Napoleonic epic, allowing you to relive the battles fought by the Grande Armée between 1809 and 1815.
Like the first set, the new 3D playing pieces are designed to represent various formations and contain all necessary information regarding combat characteristics, movement, and discipline. 
Joining the previous armies (France, Great Britain, Austria, Russia, and Prussia) are the allies of the French (Kingdom of Italy, Confederation of the Rhine, Grand Duchy of Warsaw) and the British (Spain and the Netherlands).

Key Features:

     The Headquarters Board: this innovative player board allows players to plan army organization, activation sequences, specific orders, and victory points. Thanks to this mechanic, players can move their corps simultaneously.
     Command & Strategy: armies are divided into formations under the command of leaders tasked with capturing specific sectors of the battlefield. Players must strategically distribute their forces between attack, support, and defensive lines.
     Tactical Movement: movement and changes of front are managed via octagonal points on maps divided into attack and defense areas.
     Card-Driven Mechanics: Action Cards determine weather conditions, initial movements, and unit endurance; Battle Cards resolve armed engagements through direct challenges between players; Objective and Fire Cards regulate artillery volleys; BOT Action Cards enable a fully immersive solo play mode.


Victory Condition: The winner is the player who manages to break the enemy front by routing opposing units across the various sectors.

Included Scenarios
The box includes maps and Orders of Battle (in English and Italian) for some of the most famous engagements of the 1809–1815 period:

     Peninsular War: 1809 Talavera, 1810 Bussaco, 1811 Albuera
     Fifth Coalition: 1809 Aspern-Essling, Teugn-Hausen, Wagram
     Russian Campaign: 1812 Shevardino, Borodino, Maloyaroslavets
     Sixth Coalition: 1813 Hagelberg, Dennewitz, Hanau, Kulm
     Seventh Coalition: 1815 Ligny, Quatre Bras, Waterloo


—description from the publisher

boardgame: NUTS! War Tales! Hubal! the First Partisan!
description:
I will not lay down my arms. I will not take off my uniform. So help me God.
—Major Henryk Dobrzański, upon hearing the news of the Fall of Warsaw, September 28, 1939

Hubal - The First Partisan is a campaign book for the NUTS WW2 skirmish tabletop wargame system, and is intended for squad to platoon level games that put you in command of a unit of Polish partisans in 1939. 
The campaign book focuses on the heroism of Polish Major Henryk Dobrzański, also known by his nom-de-guerre "Hubal", and his unit of Partisan fighters, The Detached Unit of the Polish Army (Polish: Oddział Wydzielony Wojska Polskiego). 
Refusing to surrender after the Fall of Warsaw, Major Dobrzański led his unit in a guerrilla campaign of hit-and-run raids that ultimately compelled the Germans to allocate a full division to stop him. This marked the formation of the first organized Polish partisan unit in World War II—and the first partisan unit of the entire war.

Contents:

     Background of the Invasion of Poland
     Biography of Major Henryk Dobrzański
     Polish Cavalry and Partisan Unit Lists
     Special rules for Cavalry Combat, Privation, Stealth, and Ambushing
     Partisan Campaign linked to new Civilian Morale Rules
     Campaign-Specific Reinforcement Lists
     Campaign Maps
     Sample Scenario


What is NUTS?

     What: Wargame/RPG rules for World War II.
     Scale: Man to man combat. 1 figure equals 1 man; 1 model equals 1 vehicle.
     Your Role: Players can command from one squad to a platoon of infantry or from one platoon to a company of tanks.
     Playability: Designed for solo, same side and head to head play.
     Campaign: Procedurally generated Campaign and Mission system.


—description from the publisher
family: History: Resistance during WW2 in Europe

boardgame: NUTS! War Tales: The Black Devils, Soviet Naval Infantry
description:
Kommisars in Peacoats. Black Death.   
These evocative names were earned by the Soviet Naval Infantry of World War 2 due to their reputation as skilled, tenacious fighters and because of their black uniforms.

NUTS War Tales - Black Devils is sourcebook describing the Soviet Naval Infantry of WW2, also know as the "Black Devils." The Black Devils were known for their tenacity and became famous for their stand at The Grain Silo in Stalingrad.

The sourcebook includes: 

     A history of Soviet Naval infantry units 
     Unit Lists in NUTS 
     How to add them to your campaign, including rules for amphibious and riverine operations 
     A mini-campaign called Black Devils at Stalingrad.


—description from the publisher

In this light, family-friendly card game, you play as penguins competing to gain the most eggs and fish.

Each round, play a card, place it in 1 of 5 spots based on its numerical value, and place your penguin on it. When all players have taken their turn, gain eggs and/or fish, based on what's shown on the Ice Floe card.

After that, take your penguin back, wipe away the cards. Flip over the next Ice Floe card, and start the next round. After 7 rounds, the game ends. The player(s) with the least fish must discard 2 eggs. The player with the most eggs is the winner!

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Warhammer: The Horus Heresy – Liber Imperium
description:
Liber Imperium: The Forces of the Emperor Army Book

Within this book the host of the Emperor are revealed, those warriors who answered only to the throne had stood apart from Space Marine Legions that had conquered the galaxy. The Legio Custodes that guarded the Emperor himself, the Silent Sisters that stood watch over the Black Ships, and the Solar Auxilia that were the spearhead of the Imperial Army. Last but not least, were the shadowy clades of the Divisio Assassinorum, the Emperor's final rebuke to those who would seek to imperil his great dream of Unity

Herein are contained details of the warriors and war machines of the hosts of the Emperor as they were during the Dark Age of the Horus Heresy. This book includes Army List Profiles for the Legio Custodes, the Sisters of Silence, the Solar Auxilia and the Divisio Assassinorum, allowing players to build and field armies of these Factions using all of the core units available.
