—description from the publisher

Naruto Shippuden: The Ramen Game is a fast real-time card game set at Ichiraku Ramen. Players take turns flipping cards into a central pile. When a “Double Order” (two matching numbers) or a “Combo Order” (a sandwich pattern like x–y–x) appears, everyone races to slap the pile. The quickest slurper claims the cards and adds them to the bottom of their deck. Use character and special cards for extra chaos, or play a simpler family variant by removing them. Be the first to collect all the cards, or have the most when time runs out.

boardgame: Play For Blood: 2 player skirmish game in the Old West
description:
Play for Blood is a small unit, two player skirmish game set in the American Old West.  
This initial rule offering is meant to be miniature agnostic, meaning use whatever minis you want.  
Later this year, I'll be doing my best to offer printable western stl files for not only characters but terrain as well.

Play as your favorite historical cowboy, outlaw or lawman, or make up your own in this fast paced skirmish game designed to be played on a 24" x24" gaming area.  
Easy to learn rules get you up and playing in no time and most games last no more than 45 minutes.

Play for Blood utilizes a D6 dice pool mechanic and incorporates a simple playing card aspect for things like initiative and turn interrupts.

This rulebook also includes 2 starter scenarios to ease you into the gameplay, offers some optional rules to spice up your games, and printable character cards for your Posses.

What you'll need to start playing:

     5 Western minis per Posse
     a handful of six-sided dice (D6)
     a tape measure
     a deck of playing cards
     a 2'x2' playing area with some things to act as terrain 


If you have a love for westerns and small unit size skirmish games, give Play for Blood a try.

—description from the publisher

"A good tea time is made with good ingredients. The giraffes host a tea party every day. Everyone smiles when they enjoy the delicious black tea.

But oh dear... There's another tea party tomorrow. Are you sure it fine to be so generous?" - story from the rules

"The goal of this game is to reduce the number of cards you have. You play small games using the cards in your hand, and you may discard some cards you played depending on the result of the small game. You keep the cards that you didn't discard and use them as your new hand when you run out of your hand.

You may easily win a small game using a lo of cards in your hand at once, but don't forget that it's sometimes important to save your cards." - abstract from the rules

boardgame: Warhammer 40,000 (Ninth Edition): Arks of Omen – The Lion
description:
Arks of Omen: The Lion is the fifth and final book in the Arks of Omen campaign event series for the 9th Edition of Warhammer 40,000.

The Arks of Omen have sown terror and death from one side of the galaxy to the other. Despite the efforts of those who opposed them, their masters have gathered enough arcane artefacts for Vashtorr the Arkifane to fashion his monstrous Key. The hour draws nigh when the daemon and the Despoiler will between them to awaken this engine of terrible power.

There are still those who can oppose their dark plan. Chief amongst them are Supreme Grand Master Azrael and the many Unforgiven Chapters who fight beneath his banner. Gathering their might, the Dark Angels and their successors answer the Arkifane’s challenge, hoping to quell whatever malevolence he and Abaddon seek to unleash. Fortunately they are not alone in their quest, for Commander Dante races to lead his Blood Angels – and countless other Imperial forces – to the coming battle.

For all these grand events, none can compare to the return of a demigod long thought lost to the annals of legend. Lion El’Jonson, Primarch of the Dark Angels, has awoken, and by strange paths has returned to aid the Imperium in its darkest hour. Will the Lion triumph, or will the champions of Chaos unleash a new and nightmarish force upon the galaxy?

Contents
 * The ongoing narrative of Warhammer 40,000, with galaxy-shaking implications for the Imperium as the Lion and his sons battle to foil Vashtorr's designs
 * An inspiring showcase of photography and battle scenes, featuring the armies fighting on the planet of Wyrmwood[1]
 * Additional rules for using four factions in Boarding Actions – Leagues of Votann, Thousand Sons, Adeptus Custodes, and Agents of the Imperium
 * Rules for playing dramatic Dread Encounters that reshape the very battlefield and recreate climatic moments from the Arks of Omen saga
 * Rules for explosive Breaching Operation missions that use Killzone: Soulshackle terrain components, as your forces blast through walls and jam hatchways
 * Datasheets for Lion El’Jonson, Commander Dante, Navis Imperialis units, and Adeptus Arbites squads

The UNO game players love features graphics inspired by favorite characters from the Beetlejuice Beetlejuice movie. The special “Beetlejuice Beetlejuice Beetlejuice” rule lets a player draw 3 cards and then give them to any opposing player they want. But be careful – whoever plays the card must remember to say “Beetlejuice” three times or they must take all the cards. When down to one card, don’t forget to yell, “UNO!”

Roll dice, move tokens, buy properties (players/stands), collect rent, build up sets for more rent, and aim to bankrupt opponents by landing on their developed spots, but with player tokens representing Villa legends/stars and properties being club areas like Villa Park stands. Key actions involve buying unowned properties or putting them to auction if you pass, getting money for passing "GO," and using Chance/Community Chest (or similar) cards for special effects.

You’ve made new blankets for your cats, but they want their old ones back! Remake the original patterns quickly before the yowling begins!

Each round, a goal card is revealed for everyone to see. All players try to recreate that pattern as quickly as possible by weaving threads on their personal player boards. Players grab a cat token as soon as they finish. The player who fails to complete their blanket before the tokens run out receives a penalty, unless another player wrongfully claimed a token with an incorrect pattern.

It’s a unique, pattern-matching puzzle game for friends and families alike.

—description from the publisher

Countdown: 3, 2, 1!
All players point to the place where they want to lay an egg.
However, if more than one player chooses the same place, no one can lay an egg there.
Once a player has laid all of their eggs, they enter the hatching phase.
Shuffle the dinosaur eggs in front of you and draw them one by one. Each position awards different points.
In addition, there are gold egg and black egg markers, which affect the scoring of the dinosaur cards they are placed on!

Get rid of all your Chameleon Cards as quickly as possible!
Use Fruit Cards cleverly to change the colors of Chameleons and reach your goal faster!

Each round, you have two action options: play connected chameleon cards, or add fruit cards to change the colors of adjacent chameleons!
You may play chameleon cards that match the current “party” color to join them, or play additional cards to change the party’s color.
If the cards you play make the party reach exactly 8 cards, you can clear the party and immediately take another turn!

The players take turns to roll and move. The first player to place all 4 pawns inside the HOME BASE is the winner.
A player gets to roll again each time doubles are rolled on the dice. Dice must not go off the table to avoid penalties.
A total of 6 on the dice will place one pawn in the starting block or an individual 6 on either die will place one pawn in the STARTING BLOCK and move the number of blocks shown on the other die. With a roll of double sixes two pawns can be placed in the STARTING BLOCK or you may put one pawn in the starting block and move six more blocks. One pawn can be moved based on the total of the two dice or the number on each die can be used individually for two different pawns.

A pawn landing on an occupied block knocks the occupying pawn or pawns out of play. BONUS:
a) First take out is worth 5 blocks. *Move any pawn 5 blocks.
b) Second take out with the same play but not necessarily the same pawn is worth 10 blocks. *Move any pawn 10 blocks.
c) Third take out in the same play is worth 15 blocks. *Move any pawn 15 blocks

BONUS moves cannot be broken up to move more than one pawn. If no pawn can be moved the total number of bonus blocks the player loses the bonus.

The dome is the most strategic turning point. A pawn cannot be knocked out while in the dome. While in the dome, a pawn can move forward or backward to attain the best strategic position to knock out an opponent pawn in the exclusive area. A player may decide to by-pass HOME BLOCK in order to knock out an opponent pawn. This move could be a come back opportunity but it forces that pawn to make another lap before approaching the HOME BLOCK.

With a roll of 3 consecutive double dice, a player can move a pawn that is within 10 blocks of HOME BASE directly to HOME BASE, or a player may decide to move any pawn according to the numbers on the dice. A roll of 4 or more consecutive double dice will automatically place 1 pawn from anywhere in the exclusive area directly to HOME BASE or a player may decide to move any pawn according to the numbers on the dice.

boardgame: Science-fusion: the Elements of the Scienauts – Rare earths lanthanides faction-pack
description: Science-fusion: the elements of the scienauts battle card game characterizes the elements into interesting personas of the element that they portray. These detailed characters then interact in the card game in may ways using real information from known scientific facts. This faction pack includes the rare earth lanthanides which are a group of 15 metallic elements (atomic numbers 57-71) on the periodic table

MEGAPEDE catapults the player (alone or with friends) into the final days before universal collapse. Over the course of aeons, the stars have more than once come close to being extinguished by artificial means, yet providence has always lent a hand to its ungrateful children. This time is different. Somewhere deep within the cosmic drape, a colossal threat unclassifiable by science has emerged. To millenarian sects, it is a god in disguise, whose purpose is to punish and reset a reality that's failed utterly. Aiding these alien world-eaters and twisted zealots are cadres of corrupt politicians, who see tangible benefits of their own in doomsday. And thus, this one-sided war against innocent bystanders — be it ideological, economic or purely predatory in nature — is fought silently across galaxies: with media manipulation, suppressed truths, and the countless dead stripped of their voice. Time is slipping away and word doesn't travel fast from one star to the next, but a few are finally seeing through the shadows of this eschatological conspiracy.

In this space odyssey, players engage with an optional, evolving narrative spread across fifteen missions, playable either as a linear campaign or as standalone scenarios. While users can create additional missions of their own, the official adventure already offers substantial variety and replayability, thanks in part to enemy behavior, dynamic spawn points, separate game modes, secret challenges, and multiple approaches to tackling each objective. These objectives range from classic dungeon crawler fare — such as eliminating all enemies or reaching a specific location on the map — to genre-shifting operations that introduce unconventional mechanics, including stealth, tower defense, and survival-horror-style puzzles. Strategy and tactics remain all-encompassing: from selecting skills and purchasing weapons down to the actual encounters. Fighting isn't as simple as rolling dice; instead, it relies on a system that makes extensive use of logic. That said, if the player prefers to avoid direct confrontation, they can devise alternative plans, hide, or set traps for the monsters roaming the labyrinth. The dungeons in question are represented on a grid covering an entire book page, illustrated in an old-school style inspired by 1970s and 1980s wargames. The counters also come from the book: they're cut and glued onto the cardboard packaging it came with, turning even that into a game component. Otherwise, simple objects like miniatures, coins and chess pieces can be used in their place, with a few adjustments.

A core element of the gameplay is the light deckbuilding mechanic featuring a standard poker deck, whose general purpose is to let players accumulate Action Points for their turn. That way they can Move, Attack, and more. However, the more cards are played each turn, the more the discards pile up, enraging the enemies in the process. They will use the discards against the players, and it is therefore a game of balance: will you strike hard and hopefully kill, so nobody can retaliate? Or will you use your resources sparingly and wisely, making enemies slightly more subdued all while you save your cards for different actions?

—description from the publisher

In this game, each player takes turns secretly answering statements about themselves—things they have, things they can do, and things they want to do. The other players then try to guess what the player answered.

Every year, the boldest surfers face off at Tiburón Beach. 
Compete to ride the most waves and claim glory. You decide
whether to go all in or back off in time. Here, pushing your luck is part of the game. If you want to conquer Tiburón Beach,surf the waves wisely—but beware of the surfer shark. Will you be the one to master the waves and grab the best boards?

Every turn, push your luck to try and surf 5 waves to win the winning board. If a shark shows up, you've lost your turn! If you decide not to play more cards, you can preserve your waves and do all the special action. The first to have 3 surfboards, wins!


Tiburón Beach es un juego de cartas en el que, como cada año, los surfistas más atrevidos se enfrentan en Tiburón Beach. Compiten por ser el que surfea más olas y consigue la gloria. Tú decides si vas a por todas o te retiras a tiempo. Porque aquí, tentar a la suerte es parte del juego.

Rewild is a tile-laying game for one or two players in which you take on the role of villagers in the world of Arborea, carving out and protecting ecosystems so that young animals can flourish and thrive.

Each turn, you place square, quartered tiles into your growing ecosystem tableau. Each quarter of the tile represents a habitat, and the tiles are placed off-set so that smaller empty squares are created between them. Once one of these empty squares is created, players may fill it with a scoring token and place a creature meeple on top.

At the end of the game, score points for each creature equal to the value on the point token multiplied by the adjacent habitats that match the creature’s colour. Players also score for unused villagers (which can be used to alter which scoring token is taken), and for having large connected habitats.

-description from developer

Designed by the original studio, A missions are supplementary missions released online for print and play.

A23 is a medium difficulty exploration mission for Rue Morgue.

MISSION ALÉATOIRE
We had a beginning of a clue, and everything went mad. We must find the-the thingy. The thingummy. The thingamajig. Whatever. It's probably somewhere here, in a building or a tent. Or in the hospital!

Designed by the original studio, A missions are supplementary missions released online for print and play.

A24 is a hard difficulty exploration mission for Rue Morgue.

ON FERME
As usual, this hospital is a zombie paradise. The corpses are completely desiccated, and you can hear the moaning from a mile away. It’s like old leather creaking. The noise is slowly driving us mad, so we’re going to stop it. The hospital will burn!

boardgame: Zombicide: Mission A25 – The Tunnel: Part 1
description:
Designed by the original studio, A missions are supplementary missions released online for print and play.

A25 is a medium difficulty exploration mission for Rue Morgue & Angry Neighbours

We don't know who dug this tunnel, and that's quite intriguing. We're going down to get answers. Who knows? There might be survivors on the other side A shelter, friends perhaps... maybe even food!

At a space station, we send our astronauts to their rocket, which is ready for launch at the quantum gate, to fly to the next star system via hyperspace.

The first to get their astronauts from their quarters to the launch pad wins—because the quantum gate only has enough energy to launch the first rocket!

Components:


      5 interlocking game boards (cardboard)
      5 rockets (cardboard)
     30 chips (5x values 1 to 6)
     20 stackable playing pieces


This looks like a roll-and-move game,  but here the dice have been replaced by chips. Each player has their own set of face-down chips, which they reveal to show values 1 to 6 for moving their pawns.

Each player may also access the chips of other players to reveal an optimal move distance for themselves - simultaneously granting a special bonus to their opponent.

The option to stack pawns allows for tactical – although risky - „chain“ moves.

Since revealing chips is always associated with expectations, every move is exciting and fun, simultaneously ensuring a low down-time.

—description from the designer

Get the farm with the most animals!
The farm is in a mess! Tom needs your help to gather the animals into the pens before the wolves scare them away. Watch the cards, find trios or pairs—but don’t make a mistake or you’ll end up in the watering trough!

There's no turns in this game. Every one shows a card at the same time. If there is a trio of animals, you should do the sound and get to keep the cards in your fence. If there's a pair or a cow and a sheep, you grab the milk token! If there's a chicken and a duck, you grab the eggs! At the end of the cards, the one who has the most cards, wins!

boardgame: Zombicide: Mission A26 – The Tunnel: Part 2
description:
Designed by the original studio, A missions are supplementary missions released online for print and play.

A26 is a hard difficulty exploration mission for Rue Morgue & Angry Neighbours

The Tunnel - Part 2
Okay, I admit that exploring the tunnel was a bad idea. Turns out this isn't one tunnel but a whole bunch of underground passages. And... we got lost on our way home. To make matters worse, zombies are close on our heels. I can see some light! Let's get out, wherever we are, and seal this maze forever! I prefer finding my way home in broad daylight.

Conversex is an educational game aimed at promoting communication processes among adolescents and young people to support the transformation of knowledge, attitudes, and decision-making related to sexuality and sexual and reproductive rights (SRR).

In Conversex, players take on the role of sexual and reproductive rights promoters who seek to help resolve cases in a city. Conversex was designed as an Information, Education, and Communication (IEC) strategy, intended to foster communication processes around knowledge, attitudes, and practices related to sexuality as experienced daily by adolescents and young people in different social contexts, including households, schools, healthcare institutions, neighborhoods, and the internet.

To win in this game you need to cooperate with other players, rolling dice and moving your piece across different parts of the board. Each road leads to a distinct part of the city, each one will present questions about day-to-day situations regarding reproductive rights. By answering correctly this questions players will get points, reach a certain amount of points and you will win the game.

Speedy Market is a competitive dexterity-based card shedding game.

At the Speedy Market, you’re hoping to strike it lucky with your own flea market stall! Or maybe you just want to get rid of all the junk piling up in front of you. But the competition is fierce:
In this game, you race to shed all your cards as fast as possible, by either laying them on one of the piles in the middle of the table or on the personal pile of one of your neighbors.
You’ll have to be fast to come out on top – very fast! Will you shift all your tat and go home with pockets full of change? Or will you let the other players palm off so much stuff on you that you leave with a bigger pile of bric-a-brac than before?

Three centuries have passed since humanity planted its first flag on the Moon, and space is no longer a dream, it’s a frontier. Lunar bases have spread, orbital stations have multiplied, and Earth-Moon travel has become routine. But the Moon was only the beginning. The next step is farther, harsher, more expensive… and far more symbolic: Mars.

In Race to Mars, each player leads a private space company in a global race to be the first to reach the Red Planet. The game is a fast roll & write with a twist: instead of everyone simply using the same dice results, the dice represent limited actions and resources that all players must share. Each round, a pool of dice is rolled, and players secretly and simultaneously choose how to claim and combine them, trying to predict what others will do before revealing choices and writing results on their personal board.

To win, you must build the smartest route to Mars by balancing three key priorities: Travel to push your mission forward, Upgrade your spaceship to improve efficiency and unlock better options, and Research to gain powerful advantages and long-term momentum. With asymmetric characters adding unique strengths, every decision matters and reading your opponents can be the difference between a clean landing… and a mission that takes just a little too long.

—description from the designer

A quiz game for 2-4 (heterosexual) couples to play in pairs, competing to demonstrate how well you know your partner. Questions come in 'for her' and 'for him' varieties, with a final quick fire round.

—user summary

Coupleology is the hilarious new couples game where everyone is trying hard to prove how good a partner they really are whilst trying to impress their other half... but, it's never quite that easy! Coupleology comes packed with bizarre and funny questions designed to make everyone laugh and poke a little fun at 'relationships' and with the final quick fire round you never know, you might even win the game and some much needed 'brownie' points.

—description from the publisher

LEGO: NINJAGO – Destiny’s Bounty Adventures is a co-operative family board game in which you and your fellow ninja will go on thrilling adventures, facing serpents, pirates...and even a bewildered postman.

In the game, villains are attacking the great ship Destiny's Bounty, and the ninja must defend their ship! To defeat the villains, you must unlock your ninja potential. Upgrade your skills and work together with your fellow ninja using your unique abilities to thwart the villains' plans to conquer the ship.

In more detail, at the beginning of the game, you choose an adventure. Each adventure has a different difficulty and set-up and brings plot twists, making players adapt and approach each adventure in different ways.

The villains try to reach the important areas of the ship, and if they do, you add the villains to the villain track. If this track is ever full, the ninja lose the game.

To win, the ninja do things like drink tea, read comic books, and defeat villains. As they do that, their victory tracks fill and unlock upgrades in the form of better spinjitzu and potential cards that give a character specific abilities. Which cards you choose and how you co-operate will be essential to winning.

boardgame: Keep'n Swap: Is Winning Worth It?
description:
Card Values (Low → High)
Ace → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5 → 6 → 7 → 8 → 9 → 10 → Jack → Queen → King
Jokers have no value

Setup
Shuffle the deck (without the Jokers) and deal 27 cards to each player. Remember to add only 1 Joker to each draw pile after each player gets their 7 cards. 
From those 27 cards, each player:

     Draws 7 cards from the top of their "about to be draw pile" to form their starting hand
     Places the remaining cards face-down as their draw pile


Gameplay
Each round, both players simultaneously choose one card from their hand and reveal it.

     The player who plays the higher-value card wins the round.
     The winner discards the card they played, but takes the losing player’s played card into their hand.
     The losing player discards their played card.


If both players play cards of equal value, both cards are discarded and no special abilities activate.
After resolving a round, players must always maintain 7 cards in hand, if possible, by immediately drawing from their draw pile.
If a player’s draw pile is empty, they continue playing with fewer cards.

Special Cards

Ace (Lowest) — Reset
If an Ace loses a round:

     The player who played the Ace takes the opponent’s entire hand.
     The opponent first draws 7 new cards from their draw pile.
     The Ace player then shuffles the opponent’s old hand together with their draw pile and places it face-down as the opponent’s new draw pile.
     Then both the Ace and the other card played will be discarded
     If therr are no more draw piles, the Ace and the other card played just get discarded 


King (Highest) — Claim
If a King wins a round, the King player may choose one:

     Take the opponent’s played card, or
     Take the top card of the discard pile


Exception: If the opponent recently played a King, the King player automatically takes the opponent’s played card instead.

Queen (2nd Highest) — Purge
If a Queen wins a round, the opponent must discard at least 1 and at most 2 special cards from their hand, if they have any.

Jack (3rd Highest) — Burn
If a Jack wins a round:

     Both played cards are discarded.
     The top 2 cards of the opponent’s draw pile are discarded.


7 — Lucky Seven
If a 7 wins a round, the 7 player takes 2 random cards (the 1st card you choose will automatically be put into your hand) from the opponent’s draw pile and adds 1 to their hand and 1 to their draw pile.
Joker — Equalizer
Anytime a Joker is played:

     The draw pile equalizes to the lower value 
     If the opponent has no more draw pile then their hand gets to be equalized 


Special Interactions

     If both players play the same card, both cards are discarded and no abilities activate.
     If both players play special cards (except Jack and 7), both cards are discarded and no abilities activate.
     If Jack and 7 are played in the same round, both abilities activate:
     7 resolves first
     Jack resolves second
     8, 9, and 10 can cancel out 7's abilities


Draw Piles
Once both draw piles are empty:

     No further drawing is possible
     Lost cards are gone permanently
     Ace and 7 abilities no longer apply
     Players must rely entirely on their remaining hands 


Winning the Game
The game ends when:

     Both draw piles are empty, and
     All remaining rounds resolve naturally


The last player holding any cards wins.
If both players lose their final cards in the same round, the game ends in a draw.

Trunfos e Tesouros (Trumps and Treasures) is a trick-taking game where players represent pirate leaders trying to reach Treasure Island. Besides battling each other, they must watch out for a rather unstable Kraken that can change plans in an instant.

In the first part of the game, players simultaneously bid for cards (in a draft) and for the number of tricks they intend to do. During the games, certain cards move the Kraken and can change trump suddenly! Trunfos & Tesouros demands good hand planning, but also the skill and resilience to deal with sudden changes caused by their opponents.

boardgame: 커피 러시 프로모: 전통음료 (Coffee Rush Promo: Traditional Drink)
description: This is a promotional card pack that is provided to celebrate Lunar New Year in Korea. You can add Korean traditional drinks and refreshments to the cafe menu. This promotion pack includes four cards each for  and .

The Lark Game Box is a portable tabletop game system designed to play multiple classic and original games using interchangeable boards and shared components.

The Lark includes a compact wooden box, double-sided boards, and universal pieces that support a growing library of games ranging from traditional abstracts to modern microgames.

Some games are designed specifically for the Lark system, while others are compatible with it as an optional play surface or component set.

The original games revolve around a fixed set of components that are small enough to fit in the Lark. Crypticolor is a puzzle game where players use colored cubes to encode and decrypt 5-letter word puzzles. Flip & Skip is a game that includes actions like jumping and swapping chips, set collection, and a “rock, paper, scissors”-type of mechanic.

Flip and Skip:
Flip and Skip is a two-player abstract strategy game for ages 14 and up. It is played on a chessboard using double-sided chips that players may flip, skip, swap, and stack. A typical match lasts 20–30 minutes. The game combines positional play with hand management, creating a tactical duel of timing, risk, and board control.

On a player’s turn, they flip one or more chips in their hand and resolve one of the revealed actions. The used chip is then added to their collection. Next, they move one or more of their pieces on the board, advancing, defending, or capturing an opponent’s piece. Each turn requires players to balance chip management with tactical planning, as every action shapes both the board state and future options.

The first player to collect three sets of matching chips wins the game.

Crypticolor:
Crypticolor is a competitive spy-themed word-deduction game for 2–4 players. It blends Battleship-style hidden targeting with Hangman-style letter deduction in a tense contest of logic and pattern recognition.

On a player’s turn, they make three guesses to reveal information about a hidden letter shape within their opponent’s secret code word, then place colored cubes on a 5x5 grid to mark hits and misses. Each guess narrows the possibilities and brings the hidden word closer to being solved.

Players score points for correct guesses and lose points for incorrect ones. After all code words have been revealed, the player with the highest score wins.

An advanced mode introduces rotated, reflected, and partially obscured letter shapes.

The system emphasizes portability, tactile play, and long-term reusability.

-description from publisher

Created by Brown Hotel chef Fred Schmidt using leftover ingredients that he had on-hand, it is said that the iconic Hot Brown sandwich was made for hungry dancers seeking respite from the dance floor on frigid winter nights. While the modern Hot Brown may look different from its 1926 origins, it’s a decadent, open-faced sandwich with a balanced blend of savory richness and acidity. This is achieved by its chief ingredients: bread, Mornay sauce, turkey, bacon, tomatoes, and very rarely...peaches.

In Hot Brown Surprise, players take turns playing ingredient cards (e.g., tomatoes, bacon, turkey, etc.) from their hand face-down to build a single pile (known as the "Sandwich"). The Sandwich is complete when each player has one card remaining in hand. Then, the pile of cards is flipped over, and the effects on the ingredient cards are resolved in the order they were played. Once all effects are resolved, the player with the highest fullness score remaining in their hand wins!

The realm faces many perils, and if your fellow knights are up to the task, you can defeat them all. But what if they aren't?

Virtue is a semi-cooperative with no explicit traitor cards. Some souls are better equipped to face evil than others, but all knights could win together, or any knights could choose to serve themselves and put everyone else at risk.

During the game a series of quest cards are drawn onto the table, and players take turns either attempting to complete quests using the virtue cards in their hands or draw additional virtue cards at the expense of more quest cards piling up. If enough quests are completed and Camelot's honor grows high enough, all players win as a cooperative game. If too many quests are failed or left on the table Camelot falls, and only one player, the one who selfishly collected the most treasure during the game, wins alone.

Virtue is based, loosely, on Camelot myth, and all art is taken from public domain artwork reaching back to the 14th century. This isn't a world of hitpoints and fireballs; this is a story of courage and honor triumphing over the evil in the unknown forest, and in your God-fearing soul.

—description from the designer
family: Theme: King Arthur / The Knights of the Round Table / Camelot

description:
Thần Chủ is a strategy game set in a fantasy mythological world, based on Vietnamese historical sources. This idea stems from the Vietnamese tradition of venerating national heroes and those who have made significant contributions, even bestowing divine status upon them. All characters in the game, whether originating from real historical records or mythological tales, are deified. Therefore, the costumes, weapons, and settings of each character contain divine, fictional elements.

Basic mode includes:

     21 Faction cards
     8 Soul Lantern cards
     38 Hero cards
     81 Basic cards
     50 Strategy cards
     23 Equipment cards
     7 Event cards

Estrategia Militar is a classic Cuban military strategy game designed to train strategic thinking, planning, and terrain control.

The game board simulates a grid-based military map, showing different terrain areas such as forests, rivers, hills, and routes. Players control military units, moving in turns and capturing strategically important positions.

Gameplay emphasizes:

Movement planning

Controlling routes and key areas

Assessing position and terrain advantages

The game has an educational and tactical training style, popular among Cuban military board games in the 20th century, and is now primarily known as a vintage board game.

Watch the Skies is a strategic, political, and diplomatic megagame where dozens of players manage nations, international organizations, and secret forces in a world facing a global crisis.

Each nation is governed by a group of players, each assuming roles such as:

Politics

Military

Science

Diplomacy

During gameplay, players must:

Negotiate and sign treaties

Address economic, military, and social crises

Make long-term strategic decisions

React to mysterious events involving extraterrestrial beings

The game operates in real time, with an umpire coordinating the action, and the outcome depends entirely on the players' actions, negotiations, and choices.

28-32mm (Miniature-agnostic) and completely free
Satirical in theme; serious on the table
Small squads (~6 models) in fast, violent matches on dense, vertical 3’x 3’ boards
Rolling is based on 2d8 that can degrade to 2d6 as Talent wear down or stress out on the pitch
Extensive “Training Camp” for new and experienced players to learn in controlled environments with subsets of the complete ruleset, designed to get Talent on the pitch fast without needing to know all the rules
Tracks Activations with tokens
Playable as one-off “Unfriendlies” or brutal league campaigns
AI-free: made by real people

To win each battle, you gotta throw hands! Get the coolest cards, throw out your strongest hand, and watch them try to do something about it.  Will you sap their stamina with a bunch of small hands or go for the throat with a big hand? The first player to take too much damage loses the round. Win enough rounds to win the war!

Players play hands of either runs or sets, targeting the opponent to their left. That player must play also play a run or set of a higher value, so a 3-3-3 must be beaten by a 4-4-4 or higher. And a 2-3-4-5 must be beaten by at least a 3-4-5-6 or higher.  If not, that player takes those cards as three damage, and whatever they did play becomes an attack to the next player.

But beware, many cards have extra effects when attacking or blocking.  When a player receives 10 cards as damage, they are knocked out and the round is over. The player with the least damage, and the player who dealt the killing blow each get a winner token. The most winner tokes after a set number of rounds wins the game!

In Muffin's Kitchen only the best muffins win!  Sort through ingredients to meet each recipe's condition with the highest possible value! With only 6 rounds, you won't have any time to lose, so make every ingredient count!

Players build their hands of ingredients to best match the various recipes available each round.  The better the hand, the more valuable their cooking will be.  Will you work hard to get big points on one or two recipes, or spread out across several?

You'll need to find food fast!  Too bad the other bears have the same thing in mind.  Bid for food and try to hard the best of everything!  Watch out, because your bids will be next rounds market! Bid smarter than the average bear and you will bring in the best haul!

Bid for food in the market and hoard them in your tableau. Try to get the highest points on each type of food, or if you can't, get as much of it as you can.  Watch what the other bears are hoarding to bid correctly and bring in the best haul.

The cards you use to bid become part of the market next turn, so pay attention to what you are potentially providing your opponents!

It's almost noon, and it's only a matter of time before something goes down at the old showdown saloon. Use the lineup of saloon ne'er-do-wells to form connections, post bounties, and win the showdown at noon. The player with the most points after the dust settles wins!

Play cards into the lineup of the saloon representing various gunfighters, townsfolk, and drifters.  Each has different abilities and effects on the lineup.  Post bounties to alter the final outcome, and once a certain number of cards are out, draw!  The player who as arranged the saloon in their favor will win.

As a wisher, draw your wish and present it to the mighty Anito. As an Anito, interpret the wisher's drawing, then present a drawing back as a solution. Both wisher and Anito can only communicate with their drawings, so each side must hope the other gets their intent.  The wisher will then pick the best solution to their problem and give themselves and the chosen Anito points. The player with the most points at the end of three rounds wins!

Each player will be a wisher or an Anito. The wisher will draw a wish and try to illustrate it.  The Anito players will have a certain domain they have power over to use in their solution, such as "bees" or "toothpaste". Will you be able to grant their wishes?

Four new heroines bring animal ferocity to Bullet!  Each has new abilities and patterns to challenge even the most experienced Bullet players. Battle against each other or cooperate against a heroine's powerful boss form!

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Venusian Hammer: Victorian Science Fiction Warfare
description:
Welcome to Venus! I hope you kept your Martini Henry clean and ready for action. Whether it is the Prussians or the local wildlife, you are going to need it. 

Venusian Hammer gives you the rules needed to play Victorian Scifi battles on Venus. The game uses the popular Squad Hammer engine, featuring unit-based combat that can be understood in minutes, but which offers a great deal of flexibility and unit cooperation.

The game provides mechanics for regular infantry (including several alien mercenary types), heavy weapons, dinosaurs, giant bugs, walking "fighting machines" and even balloons, letting you experience the full range of 19th century Venusian combined arms warfare!

Points values are provided for pick up games.

The game is really aimed at people who want to try and create their own armies: Pick miniatures you like, maybe kitbash a cool fighting balloon and create some fun terrain. A deeply personal army that is unique to your gaming table.

A typical army has 5-8 units, so you can build armies on the cheap. 
Any figure scale you enjoy will work. 
In smaller scales units can be built as single-base units with multiple figures per base, while in 28mm, we suggest 5 figures per unit.

—description from the publisher
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
Four new heroines bring designed by the fans of Bullet!  The first Bullet: Fan was a popular expansion created by fans, and now Bullet: Fan+ brings four more heroines to the game.  Chosen from submissions from Bullet: Cubed backers, each has new abilities and patterns to challenge even the most experienced Bullet players. Battle against each other or cooperate against a heroine's powerful boss form!

—description from the publisher

Dash through the forest! The first player to figure out the route and bring their game piece to the finish line wins the race. Special tiles provide surprise boosts along the way! A simple, straightforward game suitable for younger players. Includes 8 cute wooden characters and a wooden die. No plastic components at all!

This is a hybrid card/board game inspired by William Dalrymple’s book The Anarchy on the relentless rise of the East India Company.

The board is a political area map of India circa 1740. Each area on the map has a corresponding card suit identified by the background color and name on top for clarity.

The core mechanics are taken from the The Great Khan Game which used a Rummy-like draw-and-discard system whereby players take initial control of an area by placing a "meld" from their hand which has to have a leader.

There are 126 cards, each of which represents a place, leader, army, or commodity within a given area which when melded together represent an area’s political strength (crown symbols), defense strength (castles), attack or defense strength (swords), and economic strength (treasure chests).

The first player to reach 20 treasure chests in their combined melds in their income phase at the end of a turn wins the game.

Once placed, a meld/area can be captured by rival players either through political coups or military attacks.

Coups are done by placing an "alternative government" meld with more crowns than the incumbent. The defender can thwart these by adding cards from their hand to strengthen their government. The winner gets to steal the loser's meld.

Military attacks from neighboring areas are made uncertain using simultaneous bidding. Money played a bigger part in battles than soldiers in this history. For instance, the English East India Company’s Robert Clive won his celebrated Battle of Plassey by bribing his opposing general not to pitch.

Evenly matched battles tend to be disastrous with both sides suffering three cards "killed" as in placed at the bottom of the draw pile, possibly causing both areas to revert to neutral.

—description from the designer

boardgame: Re:ゼロから始める異世界生活　カードゲーム (Re:Zero − Starting Life in Another World: Card Game)
description:
Re:Zero − Starting Life in Another World has been turned into a card game. The simple rules, based on the game of Memory, mean anyone can enjoy it.
Not only do you flip over all the face-down cards and find matching cards to win, but you can also win up to four cards at a time by flipping them in the "color order" set for the character you chose before the game!
The special card, Subaru, is a "RE:SHUFFLE card," which shuffles all the cards on the table, adding to the thrill!
The user with the highest score for the cards they get at the end wins.
Characters from Re:Zero are featured on cards, and mini character cards add additional points, so you can enjoy the game until the very end.

boardgame: 盾の勇者の成り上がり カードゲーム Episode -Wave of Catastrophe- (The Rising of the Shield Hero: Card Game – Episode: Wave of Catastrophe-)
description:
"The Rising of the Shield Hero" is now available as a card game!
This is a single-player card game with random gameplay that can be enjoyed over and over again.
Guide Naofumi and Raphtalia to repel the threat of the waves! During your adventure, you may even recruit Filo!
Enjoy an adventure just like in the anime, with Naofumi protecting and Raphtalia and Filo attacking together.
Through battles, you can level up Raphtalia and Filo and increase Naofumi's shield skill, allowing you to progress through the game more strategically.
It also comes with acrylic blocks of Naofumi, Raphtalia, and Filo to use as pieces in the game.
In addition to being a great game, the acrylic blocks are also valuable fan items!

In this cooperative strategy game, players take on the roles of four food industry companies that have joined forces under the name Café Global to create the world’s largest coffee house network. However, they face a serious challenge — the aggressive rival Espresso Mundo, which is expanding relentlessly across the globe.  In a six-year competition, Café Global players win if the company gains greater worldwide market presence and popularity than the competition.

Each year in the game consists of two phases: a base round, in which every Café Global player takes a turn, followed by a response round simulating the actions of the rival Espresso Mundo, involving a degree of randomness.
On their turns, players create their own card tableau representing their current team of workforce, and use the team’s capabilities to produce and transport goods or expand by investing in new plantations and workforce – a vital part of successful operation. Players must also carry out coordinated market analysis and promotion, directly influencing Café Global’s market presence and popularity. From time to time, engaging in high-risk dealings might be worthwhile, but such actions undermine the joint venture's risk rating and could even lead to its downfall.

The success of the consortium ultimately depends on the jointly built coffee house network. The chances of winning are never fully predictable, but the game’s difficulty level can be adjusted from ultra-easy to ultra-hard by varying the composition of the card decks.

—description from the publisher (translated)

Ebben a kooperatív stratégiai játékban a játékosok négy élelmiszeripari vállalatot képviselnek, melyek Café Global néven egyesítik erejüket, hogy létrehozzák a világ legnagyobb kávézófranchise-hálózatát. Azonban komoly kihívást jelent számukra egy agresszív rivális, az Espresso Mundo könyörtelen terjeszkedése. A hat éven át tartó versenyből a Café Global akkor kerül ki győztesen, ha piaci jelenlétével, népszerűségével megelőzi a konkurenciát.

Minden egyes év két szakaszból áll: egy alapfordulóból, amikor a Café Global részéről minden játékos egyszer sorra kerül, majd egy válaszfordulóból, amely a versenytárs Espresso Mundo – részben véletlenszerűségre épülő – lépéseit modellezi. 
A játékosok a körüket egy kártyatabló kirakásával kezdik, amely az adott évben rendelkezésükre álló munkaerőt jelenti. E csapat képességeire támaszkodva állítják elő és szállítják le terményeiket, illetve terjeszkednek, vásárolnak újabb ültetvényeket, erősítik tovább csapatukat. Összehangolt marketinggel és promócióval igyekeznek erősíteni a Café Global jelenlétét és népszerűségét.  Időnként érdemes rizikósabb ügyleteket is vállalniuk, de ügyelniük kell rá, hogy ezek rontják a közös cég kockázati megítélését, és akár a bukását is okozhatják.

A konzorcium sikerét a közösen épített kávézóhálózat alapozza meg. A győzelem esélye változó és előre nem felmérhető, de a kártyapaklik összeállításával szabályozható a nehézségi fok, az ultrakönnyűtől egészen az ultranehéz játékmódig.

—description from the publisher

Shuffle the deck and deal  8 cards. The dealer goes first. The remaining cards are placed down. This is called the Draw pile. Every player plays a number sequentially. (1-12) If you don’t have that number, skip your turn and draw 1 card. You get minus 1 point. Players get points by playing cards. It restarts to 1 when the number hits 12.
If you lose 5 points, you lose. That is where the Free point card is used to save yourself. But one player can play a Minus 1 point card and you lose again. Whoever gains 35 points or is the last one in the game wins.
Note: If you have 2 number cards of the same number (ex: 2 of 8 number cards),  you can play it and get 2 points. And if you have too many cards of the same number you can play it and get points on how much cards you have of that number.

—description from the designer

The most ancient and powerful planet nations of the Lantean Empire have been thrown into a galactic civil war. After a millenia of stable, but uneasy, peace, a sudden an unexplained interdimensional rift known as The Split has caused a decade long galactic civil war. As the war rages on, and relationships deteriorate, the leader nation of the Empire, Atlantis, struggles to maintain its thousand year long control over the fractured planet nations of the Empire.

Ash is an immersive 4x empire building space opera played over multiple rounds of expansion, empire building, and war intermixed with political jousting and senate diplomacy. Take control of 1 of 10 legendary galactic factions of the Lantean Empire to defend your claim to power and control of the Empire.

Players will build fleets, economic engines, defend their planets in layered defense strategies with meaningful and fun combat mechanics, out manneuvre their opponents, and strategize diplomatically in the galactic senate by creating and abolishing laws and alliances that affect the entire galaxy. Claim victory through strategic long term planning of your faction's unique win conditions or through imperial domination!

—description from the publisher

boardgame: "D" Is for D Day A Front Line Game
description:
The date: June 6th 1944.  World War II is in its’ fifth year. The Axis Powers of Germany, Italy and Japan tried to rule the world and nearly succeed.  Many countries fell.  In response the USA, England, Russia and others Allied. Struggling, the Allies fought on until a decision to was made to risk an amphibious invasion on the fortified French cost of Normandy held by Germany, Designated, “Operation Overlord” and also known as “D-Day”. The invasion was meant to establish a foothold on mainland Europe. Those first hours would tell, if WWII would end soon or continue indefinitely. D Day Front Line, lets us imagine the desperate hours when valiant men bulldogged their way through a rain of fire, to valiantly face, Sentinels of Axis might.

D Day is for 2 -3 players, ages 13 and up, lasting 1-2 hours, shorter games are possible. One or two players represent the Allies [Yanks (USA)/ Brits (England)] vs a lone Axis (German) player. Most games last 5 rounds, made of 4 turns per round. 
When a 4th turn ends, the round completes and another round begins, except for turn four of round five, ending the game.

Lastly players review the Victory Points, on VP Track, to determine the winning side. The game can also end as an Allied Win at once: When any turn ends with the VP Marker on the 9-12 Victory Points space.

The game can also end as an Allied Win at once: When any turn is done and the VP Marker on the 9-12 Victory Points space.

—description from the designer
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

boardgame: Trying Men's Souls: The Campaign for New York, August-October 1776
description:
Following the British defeat at Bunker Hill and their evacuation of Boston Harbor, the American colonies essentially held all the land of the former colonies. However, the American invasion of Canada ended in humiliating defeat and King George III won enough support from hardliners in the Parliament to raise a massive army and fleet that the British monarch hoped would bring the rebellion to an end and the colonists to heel. A month after the colonies declared their independence, in August 1776, a British fleet of over 30 warships and 400 transports carrying over 32,000 men arrived in New York harbor. Opposing them was the largest American army ever assembled of 23,000 men under the command of General George Washington. What ensued was the largest campaign of the Revolutionary War, one that could have either restored the monarchy’s rule to the thirteen British colonies or allowed the “Great Experiment” of democracy in America to grow.

Trying Men's Souls is a moderate complexity level simulation wargame on the campaign for New York. Players are cast in the roles of Generals Washington and Howe as they command their forces in the battles to take control of the American colonies’ largest city and port. Can you do as well or better?

—description from the publisher

Players take turns acting as the “Alien,” expressing an alien message to the other players. The “Humans” must guess the answer as accurately as possible to earn points!

If the number of correct guesses is greater than or equal to the number shown on the left side of the Record Card, the Alien may reveal the answer and scoring begins.
However, if the number of correct guesses is below the required amount, the Alien must perform an additional clue!

The Alien can use various methods to express the alien message, such as:

Making a sound

Tapping out a rhythm

Using facial expressions

Standing up and acting it out with body movements

Let your creativity shine and help the Humans understand your alien language!

—description from the publisher

It is late 1942 and the seas are dangerous and frightening. Allied convoys surge across the Atlantic, bringing vital supplies to Britain and Russia. But beneath the waves, Germany’s U-Boats are on the hunt, silent and deadly.
You are the captain of a Flower Class Corvette. Your mission is to escort and protect merchant shipping, evade aggressive U-Boats and survive the threat of German air power.
Aircraft patrol the skies and U-Boats roam the convoy lanes. Your four inch gun may sink a lone surfaced U-Boat within its limited firing circle, but your depth charges are your main weapon, as you drop them into the deep seas in the hope of destroying an unsuspecting U-Boat. The decisions you make will be crucial to your success as a Corvette captain.
This is a game of cat and mouse, timing, and nerve. You’ll manage movement, detection and damage across a range of different maps, using sonar sweeps, depth charges and gunfire, both at U-Boats and at the enemy in the skies above.

Can you complete your mission or will your Corvette become yet another silent wreck beneath the Atlantic?

How the game works
This is a solitaire wargame. You play the game, and the enemy is controlled by the game. You will need about 6 standard six-sided dice. It is recommended that the counters provided on the final page of the game are used to play this game (especially for the Ships, Aircraft and U-Boats). Simply stick them to card and cut them out.

Quick to set up, easy to play, very replayable but certainly challenging, this game should provide you with hours of fun.

—description from the designer

This expansion for Uh Oh, Dino! adds egg-eating raptors and falling space rocks into the game. As players rescue eggs, they trigger "lookout" events which may introduce these additional dangers. 3 new dino powers are also included in this expansion along with a triple-super-fruit and a mechanism for dinos to "poop" out super-fruit to replenish the draw bag.

—description from the designer

Expansion pack for "Go Fuck Yourself".

It's a simple game of go fish, except it's really go fuck yourself!

Do you have repressed sexual shame?
What about old testament views on women? 
Any thoughts and prayers for mass shootings?

Go fishing for the embarrassing secrets your friends hold in hand, and go fuck yourself if you guess wrong!

A pink-themed reimplementation of Battleship, where the objective is to find a set of accessories (mobile phone, handbag, shoe, sunglasses, skirt and vest) before your opponent by guessing location by calling out co-ordinates on a grid. The boards are hinged plastic designed to look like shopping bags.

—user summary

Girls just want to have fun! Is your skirt on Aisle B?

The battle is on for this season's coolest accessories! What can you bag before your shopping rival? Hide accessories around your Battleshop to keep your rival guessing, and use your shopping strategy to figure out which aisles the accessories are in. The first girl to bag all six accessories is the star shopper!

—description from the publisher

You are members of a direct action collective defending your neighborhood from fascist street violence. Your ability—and willingness—to throw a punch has earned the admiration of your community…along with scrutiny from law enforcement.

However, it’ll take more than punching the teeth out of a few nazi faces to counteract the rising threat of fascism. Leverage your brief notoriety from “bashing the fash” to overcome leftist infighting and organize mutual aid networks into a stalwart community resistance movement.

Work together to punch the teeth out of fascists. Then, use the notoriety from your street fighting victories to avoid incarceration and build mutual aid networks.

Don’t let too many Fash go unpunched or they’ll take over, and be careful not to catch too many Charges or you’ll be Incarcerated. Spend the Teeth collected from knocking down Fash to draw Mutual Aid cards so you can hurl the Victory Brick forward.

Players compete to claim the throne of a fallen empire by crowning their city as the sole Imperial Capital. Capitals of Tactoria is a strategic, card-based kingdom builder where each player develops a personal city tableau while closely observing the growth and intentions of their rivals.

On each turn, players play cards to expand their kingdom with buildings, troops, and special characters. Some cards are played face up, revealing city structures and long-term strategies, while others remain hidden, concealing military strength and unique abilities. This mix of open and hidden information allows players to anticipate opponents’ plans without ever having full certainty.

The game is quick to set up and easy to learn, yet grows increasingly deep as more cards enter play and kingdoms take shape. Smaller, well-constructed cities can outperform larger ones through efficient combinations and timing. Each decision pushes a city further in a chosen direction, gradually forming a distinct strategic identity based on the cards played.

Alongside city development, players upgrade their capital through a shared progression, racing toward the highest tier. Each upgrade unlocks new bonuses and abilities, with multiple viable paths to the top. Combat is always a looming threat, but entirely player-driven—attacking at the wrong moment can weaken an army as much as it harms an opponent. Victory comes from carefully balancing kingdom building, capital upgrades, and the tactical use of military force.

—description from the publisher

8 districts is free and played with a deck of cards and an app. A Print&Play where you have nothing to print.

8 districts combines the strategy of planning with the interactivity of an app. Your mission as Urbanist Head is to fulfill dynamic public contracts, manipulating your Project cards in the 8 districts of the city. To do this, you will use your cards and the effects of the figures (Specialists).

—description from the designer

8 distritos es gratuito y se juega con una baraja de cartas y una app. Un Print&Play donde no tienes nada que imprimir.

8 distritos combina la estrategia de la planificación con la interactividad de una aplicación. Tu misión como jefe urbanista es cumplir con contratos públicos dinámicos, manipulando tus cartas de proyecto en los 8 distritos de la ciudad. Para ello, utilizarás tus cartas y los efectos de las figuras (especialistas).

—description from the designer (Spanish)

In this must not follow trick taking game, players will place tiles onto the board based on cards played and attempt to predict which suit will have the most board coverage at the end of the hand.

—description from the designer

Players test their knowledge and luck to earn bragging rights among their friends as either the trivia whiz or the ultimate rule-bending gambling strategist. In the game, roll the die to determine a trivia category, wager chips on the difficulty of the question, and answer correctly to win more chips! Strategic "Hedge Cards" can be purchased to help you out along the way. The first player to reach the agreed-upon winning total, or the last player standing with money, wins the game!

—description from the designer

It is the late 1800’s and scientists have unlocked the power of electricity! Take the role of an innovator and visionary working to supply an industrializing world with this new energy. Build up your industry across five different sectors, creating a revolving engine to produce, upgrade, and transmit energy cubes.

Each round, you will activate one sector of your business, represented by a tile, and take all the actions of that sector. Sector tiles can be upgraded with industry cards, adding more action icons to that sector. Cycling through your sector tiles is important because they become more powerful the longer you wait to use them. Cards with special arrows allow you to chain activations across multiple sectors, creating a dynamic engine that changes and improves with every turn. A combination of strategic engine building and tactical decision-making in response to your own shifting player area will bring you the most success.

Energy cubes transmitted to the grid are consumed to satisfy demand goal cards, earning you Energy Points. By meeting demand goals that require more and cleaner energy, you will earn greater rewards. Test your skills playing solo, or play competitively and claim your place at the forefront of the energy revolution!

—description from the publisher

What a lovely sunny day. Relax and enjoy the view from your hot air balloon!

In this leisurely tile placement game for 2-4 players, each player will try to create the most beautiful Panorama before the sun sets.

Put your panorama together one tile at a time, and choose them wisely: At the end of the game, you’ll score points based on the number of animals and flowers in each type of terrain (yes, even animals love flowers)! But watch out...
The further you move to select a tile, the more you leave behind for the hot air balloons of your opponents to pick up!

Games stay fresh and replayable through the common objectives, such as the longest river or the sky with the fewest clouds.

—description from the publisher

The dicey waters of the Six-Sided Seas have tempted many pirates with the promise of riches and glory. Legends tell of several powerful treasures lurking amongst the isles...powerful enough to control everyone and everything on these waters when combined together. There are many paths to becoming the most ruthless pirate in the sea, and the choice is in your hands. But be warned, you’ll face rivals at sea who also wish to claim the title, so prepare for a cutthroat battle or go down with the ship! It’s win or die on the Six-Sided Seas!

Six Sided Seas is a push-your-luck, worker placement game. On your turn, you will roll dice from your supply to generate crew members for your ship that can be assigned to stations. After each roll, you can choose to roll again or stop. Be careful though, because if you go over your limit, you will bust! If you choose to stop rolling and didn’t bust,  you will then assign crew members to stations on the ship. The stations on the ship allow you to deal damage to the opposing pirate ship, explore islands, or search for gold. The first player to sink their opponent’s ship, reach the maximum gold coin limit, or control the majority of the explorable islands wins!

Work with other players to create a map of the realm, but only the most accurate player will take the glory.

Latitude takes place on a blank map which slowly develops as the game goes. Each turn a player places a land type on a location (sea, plains or mountain), and places a marker showing how big they believe that location will be when the map is completed.

Each location is a contiguous group of the same land type, and each location cannot have the same marker repeated. This leads to high level strategy, as locations get completed, blocked off and more, all in a simple to play game with clean efficient rules and beautiful original non-AI artwork.

—description from the publisher

Heirloom is a hex-tile laying strategy game where players cultivate beautiful heirloom tomato gardens by carefully expanding and optimizing a community garden of tomatoes, and harvesting them with their baskets.

Over the course of the game, players draft and place hexagonal farming tiles to grow contiguous fields and create high-value tomato varieties. The twist is that you can overlap ("overgrow") tiles, which segments the boards in unique ways.

It's also an "order fulfilment" game where we are trying to bring fresh tomatoes to the get-together to make a specific dish. Getting your tomatoes there first will earn you more VP.

Featuring vibrant botanical artwork and a clean, approachable ruleset, Heirloom offers a relaxing yet thinky tile-laying experience focused on growth, planning, and the quiet satisfaction of a perfectly cultivated garden.

-description from designer

boardgame: Flames of War: Blitzkrieg – Invasion of France, 1940
description:
In the spring of 1940, the quiet fields of France awoke to thunder. Across the Ardennes rolled the steel tide of the Wehrmacht — swift, relentless, unstoppable. In a campaign of daring speed, columns of tanks and waves of aircraft were the spearhead of the Blitzkrieg, or Lightning War, a new style of warfare that would change the world forever.

INSIDE YOU WILL FIND:

     History and Background of the Invasion of France in 1940
     German Force, Formations and Units for: Light Tank Company, Medium Tank Company and more...
     French Force, Formations and Units for: Somua S35 Combat Squadron, Char B1 Tank Company, Chasseurs Company and more...
     British Force, Formations, and Units for: A13 Armoured Squadron, Matilda I Tank Company, Matilda II Tank Company, Rifle Company and more...
     Missions and Rules
     Inspirational Colour Photos
     Painting and Basing Guides

boardgame: Chippewa: Miniatures Rules for the War in North America 1790-1815
description:
Tactical rules for the North American Wars from 1790-1815. 
These rules utilize a variable scale allowing players to game in a 1 to 5 or a 1 to 10, or a 1 to 20 scale but using the same basing. 
The rules are simple to learn and play but give accurate results. 
Organizations are given for the era and a section of nine scenarios with historical orders of battle and maps are included.

Sequence of Play:

     Issue orders
     Draw cards for movement
     Check for Command compliance
     Movement and/or Fire
     Morale
     Melee

boardgame: People's War: Wargame Rules for the Age of the Guerilla
description:
Miniature wargame rules to simulate guerilla warfare, more detailed for the Vietnam war.

Sequence of Play:

     Plot Air and Artillery Phase
     Air Movement Phase
     Land Movement Phase
     Booby-trap Phase
     Air Support Phase
     Artillery Phase
     Small Arms and Anti-aircraft Phase
     Melee Phase
     Morale Phase

Navigating the ups and downs of travel experiences is worth the effort when you get to your destination. In this family card game, players will make choices to collect the cards necessary to book their destinations.

The Travel Bug Card Game was created out of a passion for families to explore the world together. Kids can see that handling challenges with flexibility and a sense of adventure pays off once they're able to experience the destinations. Each destination has different components required for booking in a combination of flights, passport, visa, ground transportation, and attraction entry passes. This is more than a game, it's edutainment.

RULES of the GAME

Object of the Game:

Book a trip of a lifetime. Be the first to reach 12 destination points.

Set Up:

Flip four Destination Cards face up from the Destination Deck.

Deal each player seven cards from the Card Deck.

How to Play:

Begin play by drawing a card. Then do ONE of the following options:

A) All set. Let’s jet! - Activate your status to book a destination by placing this card face up in front of you.

B) Book a destination. - Play the cards that match the requirements listed on the Destination Card. Place the required cards into the Discard Pile. (Passport and Immunity Cards remain in your Wallet.) Add the Destination Card to your Wallet. Draw enough cards from the Draw Pile to have seven in your hand.

C) Delay!- Place this card in your opponent’s play area to inactivate them from booking destinations.

D) Add a Passport to your Wallet.

E) Add an Immunity Card to your Wallet.
Influencer - All Entry Passes are Free.
Frequent Flyer- All Flights are Free.
Diplomat- All Visas are Free.

F) Discard a card from your hand to the Discard Pile.

Play continues clockwise until a player accumulates 12 Destination Points in their Wallet. (Each Destination Card has 2,4, or 6 Destination Points.) At this point, the other players have one more turn.

The player with the most Destination Points wins. In case of a tie, the tied player with the fewest Destination Cards wins.

—description from the publisher

Terminator 2: The Board Game is an officially licensed board game from STUDIOCANAL that puts players in the middle of the struggle to prevent Judgment Day.

Players take on the roles of Sarah Connor, John Connor, and the reprogrammed T-800, working together to survive and stop Skynet’s rise. The game can be played cooperatively (1–3 players) against an automated T-1000 opponent, or in a 1-vs-many mode where one player controls the relentless T-1000 hunting the heroes.

The game takes place across modular locations representing Los Angeles. Players move their miniatures between locations to attempt missions, resolve encounters, gather intelligence, and avoid the T-1000. Missions and encounters are resolved through a dice-based system using Combat, Skill, and Persuasion dice. Players roll dice to generate successes while managing the noise their actions create, as too much noise may attract the T-1000 or trigger dangerous consequences.

Throughout the game, heroes upgrade and expand their dice pools, acquire weapons, equipment, allies, and special abilities, and adapt their strategy as the story unfolds.

To win, the heroes must complete objectives that unlock a Final Mission to prevent Skynet’s rise. The T-1000 wins if the heroes falter along the way or if John Connor, humanity’s future leader, is eliminated.

—description from the publisher

Each player chooses a figure and places it on the center spot. Then roll one die and begin with number 1, occupying the colored numbered spots.

Dark yellow means that you can roll again as many times as you land on that color, sometimes three or four times in a row. Light yellow and light blue are considered numbers only. The dark blue spots, on the other hand, are lucky spots, because you can jump from the second circle to the spot of the same color on the outer circle. If you land on a red spot, you fall off the moving carousel and have to start again at number 1.

You must continue around each circle until you reach the highest number; only then can you jump. The largest circle is only circled once. At number 88, you turn around and return in the opposite direction. The dark blue spots are considered numbers only on the return journey, while the red spots are danger points, even on the way back. If you land on one of these, you can no longer start and must forfeit the game.

The winner is the one who overcomes all dangers on the way back and circles the smallest loop until he comes to sit on number 1.

Rainbow Race is a chaotic family racing game designed by a father-daughter duo, Cyrille HEJL and Maëna.

In this colorful adventure, 2 to 6 players use 2 color dices to race their unicorns across a treacherous, ever-changing landscape to reach the summit of the Rainbow. Special tiles and cards give various effect to enhance the gameplay and fun!

Once at the top, roll a double on 3 dice to jump and win the race!

Don't be fooled by the cute artwork; the race is ruthless, filled with traps, twists, and shifting alliances (suitable for players aged 7 and up).

—description from the designer

Guten Duck is a fast-paced, real-time party card game for 3–6 players that celebrates greetings from around the world. In this lively social game, each player holds a hand of greeting cards featuring “hello” phrases in various languages. Players simultaneously play cards by calling out the greeting shown; when two players match the same language, both respond with the corresponding thank-you phrase and score points. A large "Duck King" token adds a fun twist: if you hold a card matching the one placed under the King Duck at the start of the game when play ends, you get bonus points. Cards also include fun language facts on the back, making this both an entertaining and educational experience. Typical play takes about 5–10 minutes and is suitable for ages 6 and up.

boardgame: Let's Become a Virtual Virtual Tuber!
description:
Let's become a Virtual Virtual Tuber! casts players as fictional VTubers launching their own virtual livestream careers in a whimsical online world. Each player builds a unique VTuber by drawing head, body, name, and item cards, allowing for a huge variety of character combinations. The theme revolves around streaming-style interaction, where you engage in simulated broadcasts, generate viewer reactions, and jockey for popularity with other players.

Gameplay unfolds over a series of “streams.” On your turn as the active streamer, you choose one of the available broadcast cards to present, draw an event card to shape the content of the stream, and narrate your virtual performance. Other players act as the audience: they play comment cards to either support or playfully disrupt the broadcast. Comment cards influence the outcome and are kept for scoring later, creating a lively back-and-forth between streamer and viewers.

After selecting the stream and comments, the active player makes a judgment check by rolling two dice and adding their character’s attributes based on “impulse” or “logic” icons on the chosen broadcast card. Matching attributes yield bonuses while mismatches might incur penalties. The result determines how many “likes” the streamer earns for that turn, reflecting the success of the broadcast.

Play continues with each player taking turns as the streamer until everyone has completed the required number of broadcasts for the session. At the end, players compare the number of like tokens they’ve accumulated. The VTuber with the most likes is crowned the top streamer and wins the game, though there’s also a cooperative mode where all players work together to reach a collective success goal.

Henge! is a light, family-friendly card game where players race to spot animals that a mischievous tanuki has “disguised” as other creatures; everyone flips their hand cards and searches the shared collection of animal chips to find matching illustrations as quickly and accurately as possible. Players continue until they believe they have gathered all the correct matches, then call out to stop the round. Players then compare their collections to see who assembled the correct set first, before proceeding to the next round. The rules are simple and easy for all ages, making it a quick multiplayer visual recognition and speed game suitable for casual play.

Rubricatus is a source of life. On its shores, all unique species have been born and grown. But one of them has set out to prosper beyond what all the others could dream of. They are human beings. They have the potential to thrive on the banks of the river and make magnificent works thanks to their intelligence. But will they have enough with that?

The Etheries, the small spirits of nature and caregivers of the great Rubricatus, look with interest and some concern at these beings. They are inclined to help them, but fear that they may damage the river and turn the land into an unrecoverable moorland. That's why they called you.

Take the reins and guide humanity, with the help of the Etheries, in the great adventure of civilization in the Llobregat river, from the time of the Iberians to the 21st century. Will you be able to develop the territory without harming the river?

Rubricatus is a game of 3 to 4 players, where you will control the fate of the people and towns installed on the banks of the Llobregat. Players will have to collaborate to prevent the river from being damaged without remedy, while competing to be the main developers of the territory throughout history.

Rubricatus uses a mixed system of placement of workers with card engine and with many different possibilities of scoring.

—description from the designer

Rubricatus és font de vida. A la seva riba han nascut i crescut tot d'espècies singulars. Però una d'elles s'ha proposat prosperar més enllà del que totes les altres podrien somiar. Són els éssers humans. Tenen el potencial per prosperar a la vora del riu i fer obres magnífiques gràcies a la seva intel·ligència. Però en tindran prou amb això?

Les Etèries, els petits esperits de la natura i cuidadores del gran Rubricatus, miren amb interès i certa preocupació aquests éssers. Se senten inclinades a ajudar-los, però temen que puguin danyar el riu i convertir la terra en un erm irrecuperable. Per aquest motiu us han convocat.

Agafeu les regnes i guieu a la humanitat, amb l'ajuda de les Etèries, en la gran aventura de la civilització a la llera del Llobregat, des del temps dels ibers fins al segle XXI. Sereu capaços de desenvolupar el territori sense perjudicar el riu?

Rubricatus és un joc de 3 a 4 jugadors, on controlareu el destí de les gents i pobles instal·lats a la vora del Llobregat. Els jugadors haureu de col·laborar per evitar que el riu quedi malmès sense remei, mentre competiu per ser els principals desenvolupadors del territori al llarg de la història.

Rubricatus fa servir un sistema mixt de col·locació de treballadors amb motor de cartes i amb moltes possibilitats diferents de puntuar.

—description from the designer (Catalan)

boardgame: Holiday Hills: Decoration Objective PAXU Promo Card
description: A special PAXU promo for decoration objectives.  Score -4/14 if completed or not completed.  Involves using String lights, 2 candy canes, and 2 ornaments (2x3 grid).

Nova Duel is a high-speed, 1v1 tabletop card game of tactical starship combat. Designed for print-and-play accessibility but built with wargame depth, Nova Duel puts you in the cockpit of a customizable starfighter where every decision drains your limited energy reserves.

This isn't just about drawing the best cards—it's about managing a volatile reactor. You generate 3 Energy (EN) per turn, but you draw 4 cards. You cannot play everything. Do you dump energy into a devastating Alpha Strike? Or do you reserve power for an Evasive Maneuver to survive the enemy’s retort?
