15 unique and oversized Champion cards and 10 Legendary Upgrade cards.

Just add them to your existing Champion deck. Compatible with base game and other expansions.

This Expansion features 15 unique characters from the Mindbug Universe, reimagined as Champions. Designed in collaboration with the original creators, these cards offer fresh tactical options and a unique twist for fans of both games.

15 unique and oversized Champion cards and 10 Legendary Upgrade cards.

Just add them to your existing Champion deck. Compatible with base game and other expansions.

New soldier type and objects to add to the game.

GENERAL YELLAGREEN-"BUBBLE GUM"

During HQ setup, you can choose General Yellagreen and take the Chest card and Order tokens of the color of your choice.

Once your opponents have chosen, you can then pick the green or yellow Little Soldiers of your choice according to the mission rules.

ADVANCED TACTICS" MODULE

General Yellagreen has no Tactics cards at the start of the game.

During the game, you can gain Tactics cards by activating and crates with Little Soldiers of the corresponding color.

OBJECTS MODULE:

Magnet: When you activate a Magnet, pull another Little Soldier (which can be an ally or an adversary) in the direction of the Little Soldier who activated the Magnet using a move. They must use the shortest possible route.

Heavy Mortar: When you activate a Heavy Mortar, fire a Mortar Shot from your Little Soldier's position with the token.
All figurines within the orange arc-both allies and adversaries-who are not protected by scenery must take as many tokens as the number shown on the visible side of the Heavy Mortar token.

Promo was available in Tabletop Gaming magazine issue 111.

boardgame: Warhammer 40,000 Roleplay: Day Of Ascension Card Game
description:
Step into the shadows of the Imperium with the Warhammer 40,000 Roleplay: Day of Ascension Card Game. You’ll find 56 beautifully illustrated cards and a comprehensive rulebook that plunges you straight into the brutal action - everything you need to start playing right out of the box.

Designed for 5–12 players aged 14 and up, this fast-paced social deduction game challenges you to uncover the Traitors hidden among the ranks before they can spread the Genestealer’s Kiss and doom the Loyalists. Use Fate Cards and Special Roles to twist the game's flow, gather intelligence and outwit your opponents. Every session offers new intrigue - add unique Roles like the Astropath, who manipulates the Fate deck or the Magos Biologis, who can peer into another player’s secrets, to keep each playthrough fresh and unpredictable.


     Official Warhammer 40,000 Social Deduction Experience – Immerse yourself in the grim darkness of the 41st Millennium with an original infiltration and betrayal game set in the Imperium’s shadowy underbelly.
     Fast-Paced, High-Player Gameplay - Designed for 5-12 players, perfect for game nights, conventions or large group sessions where tension and paranoia thrive.
     Deception and Deduction at Its Core - Root out the Genestealer cultists hiding among your allies or deceive your way to victory as one of them.
     Fully Illustrated Deck - Features 56 detailed cards that bring the universe’s paranoia, faith, and corruption vividly to life.
     Dynamic Role System for Endless Replayability - Mix and match unique Roles like the Astropath or Magos Biologis to alter how each session plays out - no two games are ever the same.
     Fate Cards that Twist the Game - Manipulate destiny, reveal secrets and sow chaos with Fate mechanics that make every decision count.
     Quick to Learn, Deeply Strategic - Jump straight into play with an easy-to-follow rulebook - but mastering the mind games takes true cunning.


Every conversation is a weapon: every ally, a potential heretic.

Can you survive the whispers of the cult… or will you join the Day of Ascension?

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Las Ligas de la Ñ: La Gran Guerra
description:
Las Ligas de la Ñ: La Gran Guerra ("The Ñ Leagues: The Great War") is an asymmetrical strategy game for 2 to 8 players, in which each player controls one or more leagues from the Las Ligas de la Ñ universe (Fuego, Cuervo, León, Luna, Cáliz, etc.) on a shared map. The game is played in turns and combines positional control on the board with the management of a common general deck.

In this new Ñ game (the thirteenth in the series and the most ambitious to date), players compete to achieve the highest final score by combining points earned through in-game achievements with points awarded according to final position, based on the order in which leagues are eliminated or survive. Each league has its own play style, defeat condition, and unique objectives, allowing it to score in different ways.
The game takes place on a map-board where each element of the map represents a space (leagues, monuments, creatures, ships, etc.). Characters enter play from their league’s home space (coat of arms). Movement is carried out by drawing a straight line between spaces, provided no other space interrupts the path. Duels can only occur when two characters occupy the same space.

On their turn, a player chooses one main action: play a card, move a character, draw a card from the general deck, or activate a special power. All leagues draw from the same general deck, designed to cover the different approaches to the game (development, conquest, elimination, persuasion, exploration, survival, and events). At certain moments, the Great War event may be triggered, acting as a decisive phase that uses the combined total of the characters’ values to resolve a high-impact confrontation.

Engirdle is a drawless loop-annihilation game for two players: Black and White. It is played on the hexes (cells) of an initially empty hexagonal board. The recommended board size is 7 cells per side, but boards from 5 to 9 are also valid. Each player has access to a sufficient supply of stones of their own color.

				
				
					Definitions:
     An internal cell is a cell from which, in each of the hexagonal directions, every infinite straight line that starts from it leaves the board after crossing at least one stone before reaching the edge; otherwise, it is an external cell.
     A region is a maximal set of connected empty cells.
     An internal region is a region in which all its cells are internal cells; otherwise, it is an external region.
     A wall of an internal region is a maximal set of stones connected to each other where each stone in the set is adjacent to that internal region; a stone may belong to more than one wall if it is adjacent to more than one internal region.
     A loop is a single-color wall that surrounds an internal region that is not adjacent to any external region.
     To flip is to replace stones of one color with stones of the opposite color.


				
				
					Turns:Black plays first, and then turns alternate. On your turn, perform the following actions in order:


     Place a stone of your color on an empty external cell.


Remove from the board, simultaneously, all stones that are on internal cells.

     Flip the stones of a wall when an internal region is adjacent only to that wall: each stone takes the dominant color of the longest wall to which it belongs; in case of a numerical tie, it takes the resulting color of the last stone placed or flipped in that wall.


				
				
					End of the game:A player wins if, at the start of their turn, there is at least one loop of their color with no enemy stones on the board.

To balance the game, before starting, the first player places a black stone on an empty cell and the second player chooses a side. This balancing method is called the pie rule.

—description from the designer

Jokenpô: Arena is a competitive card game inspired by rock-paper-scissors. Players build decks with different card types and effects, trying to outplay opponents through prediction, timing, and card combinations. Each round, players reveal cards simultaneously, resolving effects based on their interactions. The goal is to score points by winning rounds and creating strategic advantages.

Sumendi (from Basque: sumendi, meaning “volcano”) is a territorial game for two players: Black and White. It is played on the hexes (cells) of an initially empty hexagonal board. The recommended size is 6 cells per side, although boards of size 5 or 7 are also valid. Each player has access to a sufficient supply of pieces of their own color. During the game, these pieces may form volcanoes (stacks) or lava (individual pieces).

				
				
					Definitions:
     A volcano is one or more pieces located on the same cell.
     The height of a volcano is the number of pieces it contains.
     A lava piece is an individual piece placed on a cell without forming stacks.
     An island is a maximal set of connected lava pieces of the same color. An isolated lava piece is also an island.
     The size of an island is the number of lava pieces it contains.


				
				
					Turns:Black plays first, and then players alternate. On your turn, place a volcano of your color on an empty cell whose height is equal to the number of adjacent cells not occupied by other volcanoes.

If the new volcano is placed adjacent to one or more existing volcanoes, each of those volcanoes loses one unit of height, which is spilled as a lava piece of its same color onto an empty cell. The lava must be placed in a straight line from the volcano, at a distance no greater than the height it had before losing that unit, and without jumping over intervening pieces of any kind. If any of the affected volcanoes cannot spill lava according to this rule, the placement is illegal and may not be made.

				
				
					End of the game:The game ends when no player can make a placement. At the end of the game, the minimal common height between both players is first compared (the lowest height at which both players have at least one stack). At that height, each player scores the number of stacks they have at that height plus the total size of the islands adjacent to those stacks. The player with the higher score wins. In case of a tie, the comparison is repeated at the next minimal common height, and so on, ascending through the heights up to a maximum of 6. If all levels are tied, the game ends in a draw.

To balance the game, before starting, the first player places a black volcano on an empty cell and the second player chooses a side. This balancing method is called the pie rule.

—description from the designer

Twilit or Twilit Poker is a poker variant for two to six players played in No-Limit mode. The rules for blinds, betting, and hand rankings are the same as in classic poker.

				
				
					Initial deal:Five cards are dealt to each player. Each player chooses three of their cards to place face up in front of them; the remaining two stay face down (though each player may look at them). Then, a first betting round takes place.

				
				
					Draw-and-discard cycles (three repetitions):One card is dealt to each player. Starting with the player to the left of the big blind, each player discards one card face up in front of them.


     If the discarded card was a face-down card, it is simply placed as a face-up discard.
     If the discarded card was a face-up card, it is placed as a face-up discard and the player must turn one of their face-down cards face up in order to always keep exactly three face-up cards and two face-down cards in their hand. Then a betting round takes place.


				
				
					Showdown:After completing the three cycles, the players reveal their final hands. Only the five cards that remain in hand count to form combinations; the face-up discards are not part of the final hand.

				
				
					End of the game:Each hand ends with the showdown. The player with the best combination according to classic poker rules wins the pot. The game continues for the agreed number of hands or until the usual winning condition in No-Limit mode is met.

—description from the designer

If you thought you’d already laughed as much as possible with Segue o Fluxo, get ready: Segue o Fluxo 2 is here!
With 55 brand-new questions, even funnier, more unexpected, and spot-on, it challenges everyone to think alike… or not!

"Round up broken heroes, pavement prophets, and alleyway weirdos to climb the rusty rungs of street power in LONERS! Recruit street toughs to build your hand, slam down powerful sets and runs to show your muscle, and call security if things get too hot. Wow never pegged you for a snitch?? Be the first to shed all your cards and win!"

LONERS is a thematic climbing game of high times and low-lifes. Play ever-escalating combos from your hand to take down the active crew on the block. You can’t rearrange your hand, so you’ll need to recruit public Loner cards to juice your combos—but that’s risky. Any combo using Loners can be stolen if another player can add a card, claiming ownership.

You can also flip the script by playing Security Guards cards to de-escalate the fight, forcing future combos lower. Get rid of your cards to win the round, but no one can go out until all public Loners are claimed. Time your plays right—or get jumped.

—description from the publisher

A community of very charismatic little animals has arrived in an unexplored forest to build the home they've always dreamed of. They are now known as the "Forest Fellowship".

The game is divided into short turns where each player can activate only one of their main character cards. Each player starts the game with three cards (their crew): two main characters (a Lumberjack and a Picker) plus a Carpenter.

On each turn, the player activates one of their main characters, performing these 3 phases: 
Move > Action (Take, Talk or Explore) > Relocation

The winner is the player who manages to be the first to find their  Perfect Place and collect 15 units of Food, 20 of Wood, and 40 units of Strength (Help).

NOTE: With one deck can play 2 Players; With 2 decks of Forest Fellowship can play up to 4 Players; With 3 decks can Play up to 6 Players.

Your adventure might have ended once, but your legend lives on. Become one of six famous knights and change the whole kingdom once again. Take on the role of such legendary figures as Sir Lancelot, Sir Galahad, El Cid, Lagherta or Caterina Sforza

Character pack is an expansion for Dice Tales: Knights Adventure that brings six famous knights from history and fiction to your table, each with special ability.

Rumours & Ribbons is a competitive board game where you play as young individuals of leisure and means in a fictional version of early 19th-century England. You’ll spend your days seeking a place of prestige in society, searching for a beau worthy of the name, avoiding the gossip of your so-called “friends,” and enduring the judgment of the neighborhood’s most cynical aristocrats.

On your turn, you’ll visit different locations in the neighborhood and spend your dowry to purchase elegant outfits, gloves, ribbons, and fans—accessories that will enhance your appearance when the grand ball is held in search of beaus. You can also pursue academic disciplines to raise your cultural standing and make a better impression at social events. This resource-gathering mechanic forms the backbone of the game and allows you to build a long-term strategy toward victory.

After visiting a location and using all your available actions for the turn, it’s time for a bit of gossip! Exchange a card with another player, and if your card wins the comparison, your opponent must apply the effects of the card you gave them... which are unlikely to be beneficial, of course. This key mechanic drives interaction and raises the level of competition.

Two or three times per game, a high-society ball is held, and all players are invited to attend. At the ball, you dance, mingle, spread rumors, and may even leave with a potential partner—though men in this game are not guaranteed to stick around! They may be lured away by others or simply disappear as victims of a new gossip in town.

At the end of the game, the player who has earned the highest level of prestige and fulfilled the most conditions of their personal objective wins, while the others are left to stew in envy and disdain... at least until the next ball season begins!

—description from the publisher

The excitement of baseball no longer has a season. Step up to the plate from the comfort of your table!

Base On Board is a strategic tabletop baseball game for fans and newcomers alike. We designed it to capture the mental chess match between a pitcher and a batter, a manager and an opponent. No bat, ball, or field required, just your wits and a desire to win. Outsmart your opponents, make clutch plays, and lead your team to glory in an immersive baseball showdown!

—description from the publisher
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
Sly3 card game is a fast-paced card game for 2–6 players where you race to
  empty three card zones before anyone else. Games take about 15 minutes. Ages 7+.         
                                                                                           
Players compete to clear their Hand, Open Cards (face-up), and Sly Cards (face-down, played blind). On your turn, play a card equal to or higher than the top of the pile.

Can't play? Pick up the whole pile.                                                  
                                                                                           
The Chaos Rule is the heart of the game: any player holding a matching card can play it out of turn — interrupting anyone, at any time. This is the base game, not a variant.    
                                                                                       
Special cards shake things up: 7 forces low play, 9 skips the next player, Wild Paw plays on anything, Swipe clears the pile and lets you play again.

Validated with 39 first-time players at PrezCon 2026 — ages 7 to 78, 4.2/5 fun, 4.5/5 ease.                                                                                    
       
"Easy to learn. Light-hearted. Short. All ages could play easily." — Roger W.       
                                                                                           
  —description from the designer

Players, in the roles of farmers, winegrowers, breeders, agronomists and shepherds, will try to cross Agrylandia avoiding droughts, floods and fires and taking advantage of the favourable climate, water purification and investments in organic farming to finish first and win the title of best farmer.
It won't be enough for players to go fast: they will have to answer questions correctly that will test them, but, at the same time, provide them with valuable information about European agriculture. So take up the challenge, roll up your sleeves, grab your tools and come and play with us: Agrylandia awaits you!

—description from the publisher
I giocatori, nei panni di contadini, vignaioli, allevatori, agronomi e pastori cercheranno di attraversare Agrylandia evitando siccità, alluvioni ed incendi e sfruttando il clima favorevole, la depurazione delle acque e gli investimenti sul biologico per arrivare primi ed ottenere il titolo di miglior agricoltore.
Ai giocatori non sarà sufficiente andare veloce: dovranno rispondere esattamente a domande che li metteranno alla prova, ma, nello stesso tempo, forniranno loro delle preziose informazioni sull'agricoltura europea. Quindi accetta la sfida, rimboccati le maniche, prendi gli attrezzi e vieni a giocare con noi: Agrylandia ti aspetta!

—description from the publisher (Italian)

This game is composed of 32 cards, numbered from 1 to 32, in which just as many basketball players, from all the NBA Teams, appear.

To win a player must get sets of 3 cards with consecutive numbers, putting them on the table in their player area (right in front of them) facedown.

During a game, each player bill be getting cards to their own facedown groupings as long as they have consecutive numbers, either increasing or decreasing.

Game Mechanics: all cards will be distributed equally among all players. The active player will start play by asking any of the other players for a card, speaking the number aloud. If said player has that card in his hand, he must give it to the other player. The active player will keep asking for cards until  he requests a card the solicited player doesn't have. I such case the player to the right becomes the active player and will ask for cards in the manner described above.

If a player gets rid of all his cards, either by putting them facedown on the table or by giving them to other players, on their turn they will still be able to request cards from the other players even if that person can no longer be asked for cards. The winner will be the player that, once all cards have been played, has the larger number of cards in the pile on his player area.

In the event of a draw the game will be won by the player with the card of the highest number value in his face down pile.

The game consists of a special tuckbox with cards. Each card shows a countdown from 5 to one, each number with a trivia clue, all five of which lead to the same answer.

One player pulls out a card so that just clue number 5 (the hardest one) is visible and reads it to everyone. Players may now note down the answer and mark it with a 5, or pass and wait for the next clue (number 4) to be read. When everyone has noted an answer, players will score the number next to their answer if correct. So the longer you wait and the more (and more obvious) clues you need, the fewer points you will get. Whoever has most points after 5 cards, is the winner.

NUTS Platoon Leader is a series of optional rules for managing your Platoon – the infantry and attached units your Star commands. 
So while your Star may still be in the thick of the fight, as Platoon Leader you also have to worry about things like resource management, your soldier’s well being and morale, who to send on the next Mission and who to keep off the Line.

Here's what you’ll find in this guidebook

     Platoon Organization & Assets
     Issuing Orders for the next Mission
     Basic Platoon Formations
     Platoon Management
     Privation & Fatigue
     Soldier Recovery & Replacements
     Platoon HQ and Squad tracking sheets
     You can even suck up to your CO to get better Support for your Platoon - hey, check your ego at the door, your Platoon needs you!


—description from the publisher

The Elemental Mages are summoning dragons for their Dragon Riders.
Channel the magic of Earth, Air, Fire and Water to attract the dragons aligned to these forces by rolling specially created dice.
The first to complete their set wins.

Each round, each player takes a turn selecting 4 dice at random from the bag. They then roll these dice up to 3 times aiming to get the same elemental symbol on 3 of the dice. If they do, they have summoned the appropriate dragon to their cause.

There are 4 sets of 4 dice. Earth, Air, Water and Fire. Each die has 3 sides with their symbol and then 1 of each of the other 3 symbols on the remining sides. It is much easier to roll an earth symbol on an earth dice. Each player must select their target dragon carefully.

A player wins when they have summoned one of each of the 4 Dragons.

—description from the publisher

Factions control the flow of information, and store the most sensitive secrets in siloed, offline servers around the city. You are a street runner; hacking into locations of rival factions for intel. The only way to stay ahead is to keep moving; so hack in and get out.

run/hack is a competitive cyberpunk strategy game for 1-4 players. Defend your servers, upgrade your gear, hack into rival servers; all without being noticed.

Mechanics include dice collection & manipulation, cards, missions, a randomized hex board, area control, and a distinctive action selection grid.

—description from the publisher

Age of Fantasy: Quest is set in the dark, magical world of Tyria, a continent fractured by the collapse of the Human Empire and the opening of the Great Rift. In an era of war and calamity, players navigate a land haunted by daemons and ancient powers. Heroes from many peoples must form unlikely alliances to face otherworldly monsters and forge their path through the ashes of old empires.

The game is a solo or cooperative miniature skirmish adventure where a party of 2 to 4 player-controlled heroes faces off against an automated AI system. Each hero is defined by unique stats like Quality and Defense, as well as attributes for Strength, Dexterity, and Willpower. Gameplay is structured into rounds where players and the AI alternate activations, using actions such as Advance, Rush, Charge, and specialized Skill actions.
Combat and interactions are resolved using a streamlined D6 system. Heroes can use their Power to overcome failed tests or activate unique class abilities, while casters manage spell tokens to unleash magical effects. A dynamic Alertness system tracks the passage of time and the noise made by the party, triggering waves of reinforcements that keep the pressure high as the mission progresses.

To win, the party must complete a Primary Objective, which ranges from clearing specific enemy waves in "Clean Sweep" to retrieving vital items in "Retrieval". Victory is achieved when these specific conditions are met and the heroes successfully reach an extraction point, earning experience points and gold for long-term progression. However, if all heroes are rendered unconscious by wounds, the mission is failed.

—description from the publisher

Play as an animal balancing their relationship to the elements and attempting to reach their spiritual potential allowing them to become Otsuki (guide) to help others realize their highest self.
Players play tricks with element suited cards. Trick winners must balance (or unbalance) elements within tricks and choose cards from hand to pay as offerings in hopes of moving up the spirit board at the end of the round. Each player's chosen animal has specific abilities related to their animal nature that can be used during the play to alter the course of each trick.
The first player to balance all four elements AND reach the top of the spirit board to reveal your spiritual nature wins the game.

—description from the designer

Valpiedra 1.5 is an expansion for the card game Valpiedra: Descensus ad Tenebras, allowing you and your companions to explore the dark catacombs even further in search of a sacred relic and seal the portal to hell before corruption consumes the world.

During the game, the group explores levels full of dangers, makes critical decisions, and carefully manages their health, equipment, and fear. Combat is resolved using dice and each hero's unique abilities, facing enemies that evolve, summon reinforcements, or even break the rules of the game.

Get ready to go further and delve even deeper into the darkness.

—description from the publisher (translated)

boardgame: Tiny Epic Galaxies: Deluxe Edition (2nd Edition)
description: The 2026 Deluxe Edition of Tiny Epic Galaxies comes packed with all of the Kickstarter-exclusive content packs and its expansions all in one box.

boardgame: Tiny Epic Dungeons: Deluxe Edition (2nd Edition)
description: The 2026 Deluxe Edition of Tiny Epic Dungeons comes packed with all of the Kickstarter-exclusive content packs and its expansions all in one box.

description: The 2026 Deluxe Edition of Tiny Epic Dinosaurs comes packed with all of the Kickstarter-exclusive content packs and its expansions all in one box.

description: The 2026 Deluxe Edition of Tiny Epic Pirates comes packed with all of the Kickstarter-exclusive content packs and its expansions all in one box.

boardgame: Tiny Epic Vikings: Deluxe Edition (2nd Edition)
description: The 2026 Deluxe Edition of Tiny Epic Vikings comes packed with all of the Kickstarter-exclusive content packs and its expansions all in one box.

boardgame: Tiny Epic Zombies: Deluxe Edition (2nd Edition)
description: The 2026 Deluxe Edition of Tiny Epic Zombies comes packed with all of the Kickstarter-exclusive content packs and its expansions all in one box.

Tapalavras is a fun and challenging experience for anyone who loves word games.

Players choose a card and a category, start the timer, and then have 10 seconds to say a word using the letters available on the board, stimulating creativity and quick thinking.

Ideal for playing with friends or family, it’s a great choice for improving vocabulary in a fun and competitive way. Perfect for all ages!

To your cauldrons, witches and wizards!
Potion Rush is about to begin — the great annual competition of the master alchemists of Witch Valley!

Race to gather the ingredients needed to create the most powerful or the most diverse set of potions before anyone else. But be sure to bring an arsenal of artifacts and spells with you, because your rivals will do everything they can to make your life difficult.

In Potion Rush, you and your friends take turns performing one action per turn. On your turn, you may draw cards from the deck in search of Ingredients; brew Potions using the Ingredients you already have; or use Artifacts to speed up your search or steal cards from your opponents. You may also cast Spells at any time to disrupt other players and escape the tricks they set for you. To win the game, you must be the first player to brew 3 or 4 potions, either all of the same type or all different from one another.

With the game QI, your child’s playtime becomes even more exciting. It’s a question-and-answer game that tests knowledge, supports learning, and lets kids challenge their friends and the whole family.

QI is a trivia challenge featuring 3,000 questions on a wide range of topics, ideal for 2 to 6 players aged 12 and up. The objective is to move across the board by answering questions from categories such as Science, History, Arts, and General Knowledge.

—description from the publisher

In Breachers: Rifts of Everdark, players control a group of heroes who are part of a mysterious organization known as the Breachers. Their mission is to seek out and hunt down the fearsome rifts — ruptures and portals that connect the world of Everdark to the Abyssal Plane.

To accomplish this, the heroes must explore treacherous dungeons in search of whoever is responsible for opening these portals.

The heroes advance through the dungeon room by room, with the primary goal of sealing all open rifts. In each room, abyssal creatures pour into the world of Everdark through portals created by the dungeon’s boss, attempting to bring the heroes’ journey to an end.

Every dungeon and ruin explored holds secrets and traps designed to strike even the most experienced hero.

Geeks vs Trolls is a fast-paced party card game for 1 to 6 players (about 15 minutes) where players manage their hands, use unique character abilities, and attack opponents with chaotic take-that effects.

Each player starts with five LIFE CARDS placed face down and one ABILITY CARD in hand. The winner is the last player still alive.

An unpredictable game full of strategy and fun, a tribute to the classics — created just for you and your crew, where a single card can change everything!

boardgame: Carapace: A Game of Memory and Ruin
description:
Carapace is a solo tabletop skirmish game. Players control Combat Wings comprised of ancient war machines trying to recover their original purpose and memories. Piloted by a Cantor, a sacred steward who speaks for you, together you battle across ruined reliquaries, drift-wrecked strongholds, and memory-haunted warfronts. This is a game of reclamation and survival. You won’t just win missions, you’ll regain parts of yourself and your world doing it. Victory is temporary, but identity is everything. Tell the story of your carapaces, one battle at a time.

—description from the publisher

Grow your village, build your army, sacrifice
to the Gods, and destroy your friends in this strategic and slightly divine card game.

The Last Village is a medieval fantasy card game that supports 2-5 players and plays in 60-90 minutes.

The game is split into 2 phases: the building phase and the battle phase.

BUILDING PHASE:
During the Building phase, players collect resources to construct buildings and make sacrifices to the Gods.
Buildings offer upgrades and improvements to the player's village and army. A successful sacrifice to the Gods earns a powerful Act of God card to be used later during battle.
Every time a Village card appears, players gain Battle cards to
grow their army.
After the last (5th) Village card is played, the Battle phase begins.

BATTLE PHASE:
The Battle phase is split into 5 rounds, where players use their
Battle cards, buildings, and Acts of God to win Victory Cards.
The player with the most Victory Cards wins the game.

—description from the publisher

Curtain up for the street! In “Street Art,” you become street performers competing for the biggest audience. Play dazzling street acts—from the spark‑throwing fire breather to the motionless statue to the whirling breakdancer—and attract the onlookers flocking to your shows. But the mood can shift: a sudden rainstorm may drive the crowd away, and a spectacular firework display can lure fans away from your opponents’ performances. Who plays their cards most skillfully, who steals the show at just the right moment, and who becomes the undisputed star of the street?

A fast, easy‑to‑learn card game full of action, mischief, and street‑art flair.

—description from the publisher

Star Astalos Beyond, the first expansion for Star Astalos, contains 35 new cards (humanity cards, aliens, factions) and updates for 8 existing cards of the base game.

Cards from Star Astalos Beyond have been designed to bring more variety, create more danger/pressure during the early game, and provide the player with tools to better survive during the late game, when the threat level gets high.

You will have tools to defend your base a bit further and thus achieve better final scores.

—description from the designer

Lucky Kitty is a light, tactile game where playful kittens paw at a basket full of tangled yarn. Each player represents a kitten trying to carefully pull out the right colored threads to complete their personal collection. But yarn isn’t always cooperative: hidden knots can suddenly appear, forcing a kitten to stop and potentially letting an opponent benefit instead.

At the start of the game, players receive a face-down deck of yarn cards that secretly defines the colors they will need to collect. On a turn, a player first reveals the top card of their deck, adding it to their display to show which yarn colors they may pull from the basket. Then they choose one of those colors and gently pull on a matching yarn. If a knot appears, the player must immediately let go and their turn ends. If the entire yarn comes out without revealing a knot, the player claims it and places it with the corresponding revealed cards.

Players take turns clockwise, balancing memory, risk, and timing as they decide which yarns to attempt and when to press their luck. The game ends immediately when a player has successfully collected all the yarns matching the cards in their deck. That kitten wins the game by completing their collection first.

—description from the publisher

In Next Station: Berlin, you are competing to design the most efficient metro network in the German capital. Over four rounds, each player draws colored metro lines on their own map, starting from two designated departure stations on opposite sides of the city. As Station cards are revealed, everyone simultaneously extends their current line to matching symbols, carefully following the printed connections and strict building rules—no crossing tracks, no loops, and no retracing your path.

Each round introduces a new color, gradually layering your network across Berlin’s districts. Special cards allow you to create railway switches or mark key hubs as National Train Stations, opening up new tactical possibilities. Across the game, you will aim to link your two starting stations, spread through multiple districts, and create valuable interchange stations where different colored lines meet.

After four rounds, players total their points from completed lines and interchanges. The player with the highest score is declared Berlin’s best metro planner.

—description from the publisher

At the Fae royal court, is nothing as it seems. The fae folk of the four seasons, along with Sun and Moon, vie for dominance and influence. In this card game, you cleverly trade cards to expand your power and score up to ten points. But be careful: everyone follows their own plans and can ruin your victory at the last moment. Who will keep a clear view, outplay their opponents, and secure their power?

A tactical card game full of intrigue and scheming — for everyone who enjoys turning the tide with clever moves!

—description from the publisher

Kingdomino: The Lost Treasures is a mini-expansion for Kingdomino in which mysterious gemstone-filled chests lure rulers to enrich their growing realms. As you expand your kingdom with domino tiles, you may uncover hidden treasures—precious gems that bring additional crowns, but sometimes unexpected risks. This expansion adds a new layer of tactical opportunity to the familiar tile-drafting and kingdom-building experience.

After setting up the base game, Treasure tokens are placed nearby, with two revealed to show available gems. Whenever a player places a domino that forms a square of four differently typed, adjacent landscapes, they discover a treasure. They choose one of the two visible Gem tokens and place it at the intersection of those four landscapes, orienting it as desired. Each gem provides crowns that are added to territories during final scoring, but careful placement is essential—some gems include skull symbols that cause the pointed territory to score zero points, regardless of its size or crowns. Once placed, gems cannot be moved or rotated.

The game ends as usual after all dominoes have been placed. Players score territories by multiplying the number of squares in each contiguous landscape by the total number of crowns it contains, including those granted by gems. The player with the highest total wins. However, there is also an alternate path to victory: any player who collects five different gem types in their kingdom immediately wins the game. The Joker gem may represent any type, making this sudden triumph more attainable—though it provides only a single crown.

—description from the publisher

Wombat Tower is a cooperative dexterity game in which players work together to defend a central tower from waves of invading enemies. The Wombats are under attack, and there’s no time to prepare elaborate plans—players must use quick thinking, careful aim, and a bit of luck to knock the assailants away before they overwhelm the tower’s defenses. The game features a playful, tactile experience centered on a dice tower and advancing enemy forces along four paths.

On a turn, a player may rotate the dice tower to face a chosen path, then roll a special die inside the tower to physically try to knock enemy figures off the board. Any assailants that fall are removed, while those merely pushed are returned to their spaces. After the roll, the die’s symbol is resolved, typically bringing new enemies onto a path, advancing enemies toward the tower, or triggering a bonus action using special Machine cards that modify how the die is launched. Enemies consist of Soldiers and more dangerous Leaders, whose arrival can cause entire paths to surge forward together.

The players win immediately if all assailants are cleared from the board at any point. However, the tower is protected by a limited number of shield tiles, and every time an enemy cannot be placed or advanced because a path is full, a shield is broken. If the third shield breaks, the game ends in a collective loss. A heroic variant increases the difficulty by adding extra enemies during setup, making coordination and precision even more critical.

—description from the publisher

On the Trail is a head-to-head race game that puts players in the role of endurance runners tackling a demanding trail made up of colored race legs and tactical stops. The theme centers on pacing, stamina, and smart decision-making as runners push toward the final aid station while coping with physical setbacks and seizing moments of momentum.

Each turn, players must advance their runner by completing at least one leg of the trail, playing Race cards that match the color of the next leg, using wild cards, or spending pairs of cards as substitutes. Players choose how far to push each turn, deciding when to stop, which affects both hand management and the type of stop they land on. Aid Stations can provide reusable Boost cards with special abilities, while Setback stops impose ongoing penalties that restrict options and compound over time. After movement, players refresh their hand, but poor planning or bad luck can make it impossible to move, forcing a runner to drop out of the race.

The goal is to be the first to reach the final Aid Station and qualify as a finisher. Once a player finishes, the round is completed so both players have taken the same number of turns. If only one player finishes, they win immediately; if both finish, the runner with the most cards left in hand claims victory, rewarding careful timing and restraint. If a player cannot advance on their turn, they suffer a DNF, leaving their opponent with a clear path to victory if they can still complete the race.

—description from the publisher

In this marine biology themed deck-building game, each player creates their own ecosystem in the Pacific Ocean by placing animals onto compatible substrates and developing a balanced marine community.

Victory points can be earned in two main ways.
First, substrate cards reward specific categories of animals. By chaining compatible substrates together and successfully placing the right animals on them, players can significantly increase the number of points scored within each animal category—provided they meet the placement requirements.
Second, at the start of the game, each player draws a personal board objective that defines a specific spatial pattern. Filling the board according to this template grants additional bonus points at the end of the game.

Cards also provide actions that influence the development of ecosystems. There are two types of actions:

Immediate actions, which can be triggered when the card is played (unless the card was placed for free).

Bonus actions, which are unlocked by paying an additional cost. When the bonus is activated, both the base action and the bonus effect are resolved.

These actions may help players expand and optimize their own deck or slow down the progress of their opponents, creating a balance between engine-building and interaction.

Throughout the game, players must also adapt to player interactions, environmental disturbances, and the growing pressure of climate change, which can prematurely end the game if ecological thresholds are exceeded. This introduces a tension between competition and limited cooperation to maintain ecosystem stability long enough to score effectively.

The game ends in one of two ways:

A player completes their ecosystem by filling all substrate spaces with at least one animal (or marine litter).

Climate change thresholds are reached, immediately ending the game.

Players then total their victory points from substrates, animals, and completed objectives.
The player with the highest score wins.

Usual Crapules takes you into the frenetic atmosphere of a trial between former robbers. After a botched heist, the players find themselves before the judge: whoever gets the shortest sentence will be able to dig up the loot before the others.

The game combines bluffing, card combos, and delightfully treacherous interactions. Each turn, you play a card: evidence to incriminate another player, a juror to increase the sentence, a lawyer to plead innocence... or an objection to turn the tables. The mechanics allow for counterattacks, and numerous plot twists.

The final verdict depends on the total number of years in prison accumulated by the proofs collected and the jurors placed. Each game is fast-paced, tense, and funny, with high replayability thanks to the asymmetrical talents of the scoundrels and the numerous Balance tokens offering unique effects.

—description from the publisher

A cooperative crew actions combat simulator during the service of historically familiar missions of WWII by the men of the US Air force B-17 Bomber Squadron. You take flight, fend off enemy plane attacks, and drop your payload on the target.  This legacy light game allows you to upgrade your crew through successful shooting down enemy planes and for dropping bombs on the correct area of the map. Custom attack dice and damage allocation happens throughout the mission.  Allocating where to place your crew on the b-17 and which to remove to allow for preparation of the drop are crucial decisions made by players.  Maneuverability is counter intuitive as you seek to stay close together to give maximum fire power over the waves of enemy attacks. Play symmetrically as pilots or pic from one of five asymmetric pilots from real individuals who performed heroic acts during their service.

—description from the designer

In an alternate-history 1950s, astronomers make a discovery that changes everything: a cosmic catastrophe is approaching Earth on a fixed and unstoppable timeline. Governments around the world reach the same conclusion—there is no way to stop it. Survival will require escape. The race begins.

In Kiamat, players lead rival world powers as they launch from Earth and push outward into the Solar System, harvesting resources from Luna, Mars, and the distant outer worlds. Each expedition is costly, and every decision matters. Players must carefully plan their routes, timing, and investments as they work to advance their civilization toward a single goal: building enough infrastructure to leave Earth behind before time runs out.

The game unfolds over a series of phases. During each phase, players venture into space to gather resources and push their survival efforts forward, then return to Earth to regroup and prepare for what comes next. But as the end draws closer, public morale steadily declines. Desperation grows. Players may bend the rules to gain short-term advantages—but every shortcut comes at a cost, and the people are watching.

Kiamat is a tense race game where speed alone is not enough. Any civilization that fails to complete its escape is eliminated entirely. Among those that succeed, victory goes to the player who reaches the end with the strongest morale and the most stable society. The world may be ending—but how you leave it behind still matters.

—description from the designer

Creatoons: Caricatures of Great Creators is a card game for 2 to 4 players, played over three rounds, in which players compete to become the most inspired and brilliant creator. Each player represents a single artistic discipline —Writers, Composers, Painters, or Filmmakers— and scores points primarily through the characters of their own discipline. Character cards feature three values (Genius, Originality, and Fecundity), with Genius being the core value used for scoring, while the others come into play through specific card effects.

Players always hold five cards in hand. On their turn, they draw one card from a shared deck and discard one, gradually shaping their hand while denying opportunities to their opponents. The goal is to legally discard all cards in hand by forming specific combinations tied to the player’s discipline, special support cards, or rare universal combinations. When a player manages to discard their hand, the round immediately ends and all players score based on their current cards.

In addition to character cards, the deck includes support cards that enhance scoring, powerful one-time effects, disruptive cards that allow direct interaction between players, and high-risk cards that can dramatically alter the outcome of a round. After three rounds, the player with the highest total score is declared the winner.

Creatoons is directly related to the book series Caricatures of Great Creators by Pablo Morales de los Ríos, including volumes such as Caricatures of Great Creators: Classical Musicians. The card game expands the illustrated universe of these books into a playable format, transforming the caricatures of composers, writers, painters, and filmmakers into interactive characters with distinct creative traits. Both the book and the card game are conceived as complementary products within the same artistic family.

—description from the publisher

Look up! Your friend Lino the mammoth has found wood, stones, berries, and even rare crystals! It rattles and clatters as he rolls them down the mountain. Work together to build a little village before Lino gets tired!

With Paleolino, children can now dive into the exciting world of Paleo, the 2021 Kennerspiel des Jahres.It's a cooperative adventure for up to 4 kids age 5 and older.

Players cooperate to build ten inventions. To do so, they must gather the required resources. On a player’s turn, new resources enter the game by shaking the shaker and drawing a resource chip. The chip is dropped into a 3D mountain, where it tumbles down and ends up in one of four drawers, called caves. One cave must be opened - but will it contain the resources you need, or will you have to rely on help from your fellow players?

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Dice Raiders: In Temple of Anubis
description:
Unravel the secrets of the past with Dice Raiders: In Temple of Anubis.
Step into the shoes of a fearless adventurer, following in the footsteps of archeology heroes like Jones and Croft! In this game, you are one of the on a quest to solve the mysteries of the ancients hidden within the Temple of Anubis. You will need to use your strategic thinking to overcome obstacles and challenges as you explore the temple and uncover its secrets. With its engaging gameplay, unique mechanics, and immersive theme, Dice Riders is a game that will keep you coming back for more. So why wait?
Play the game and explore this amazing place.

Dice Raiders is a Roll and Write Print and Play game. You will roll the dice.
Game is prepared for 1 to 4 players in mind, and is great for each number of players. Solitaire mode had special “Independant Explorer” mechanics that will give the challenge for solo players. On average, players can expect to spend around 20-30 minutes playing the game.

This Promo for Aquaria consists of a new card and red Microflora Dial, only achievable by being the first player to achieve the goal listed on the card!

This Promo comes free with every pre-order of Aquaria.

New rule: 
Take this card when you are the first player to put your Aquarium cards (incl. Lionfish) in 1 column. You gain red microflora dial for this column, which gives you double income.

Begin the railroad revolution in the land of soccer and tango at the foot of the Andes. The new expansion for Dice of Steam welcomes you in Argentina! Prepare to embark on a captivating journey as you lay your tracks across South America.

Argentina's terrain is a stunning blend of contrasts. To the west, the mighty Andes mountains rise, home to towering peaks and glaciers. In the central region, vast grasslands known as the Pampas stretch endlessly, perfect for farming and grazing. The south reveals Patagonia's rugged wilderness, with dramatic glaciers and windswept plains. To the north, tropical jungles house the magnificent Iguazú Falls, while the northwest boasts colorful mountain valleys like the Quebrada de Humahuaca. The eastern coastline features a mix of beaches and cliffs along the Atlantic Ocean. Argentina's diverse landscapes offer adventure and beauty in every corner.

Claim your ticket, unleash your entrepreneurial spirit, master the art of steam, and leave your mark on the history of the 1900s Argentina.

-description from publisher's website

In Maestros restauradores ("Master Restorers"), 1 to 4 players take on the role of antique dealers, competing for prestige and recognition from their opponents.
As an antique dealer, you must search for antiques, bring them to your workshop, and hire master restorers who will work with materials and their own skills to transform old junk into authentic treasures.
Each antique dealer begins the game with an initial hand of 2 antique cards, 2 master cards, and 2 material cards.
In each round, the antique dealers can perform any actions they can on their turn. The goal of the game is to be the antique dealer with the most points at the end of the game, which occurs when all the antique dealers pass because they cannot perform any actions on their turn.

A game of Maestros restauradores consists of an indeterminate number of rounds. In each round, each antique dealer has one turn in which they can perform any action as many times as they can or want. The available actions are as follows:

1. PLAY ANTIQUE. Play an antique card face up in front of you on the table. This area is called the WORKSHOP. You can play up to 3 antiques from different guilds, but any quantity. That is, of the 6 guilds in the game, you can only have 3 different guilds in your workshop, and any quantity of each. For example, you can have 3 tables, 1 sofa, and 2 sculptures. You can have antiques in your workshop without a master to whom they are linked. But BEWARE, if another antique dealer plays a master linked to that antique, they can take it from you and add it to their workshop, provided they don't already have that antique in their own workshop.

2. PLAY MASTER. Once you have an antique, you can place a master associated with it in the workshop. For example, if you already have a sculpture in the workshop, you can place the sculptor. This master should be placed below the sculpture. Just like with antique cards, you can have multiple masters working on antiques.

3. PLAYING THE APPRENTICE. The Apprentice card is a special card. It must be linked to a master, placed under the master's card so that the card's name is visible. The Apprentice has several abilities. First, for each apprentice in the workshop, you will have one additional card at the end of the turn. Second, you can flip the Apprentice card to search the discard piles of rival antique dealers for cards related to their master's guild and steal them. For example, if linked to a master cabinetmaker, the apprentice can search wood cards and/or table cards from the discard piles. They can see all the piles but can only steal from one, to retrieve the card and add it to the hand of the antique dealer who owns the Apprentice.
Only one apprentice can be linked per master.

4. STORE MATERIAL AND ANTIQUE CARDS. In your workshop, you have an area called the WAREHOUSE where you can store material and/or antique cards from your hand. This action allows you to clear your hand so that at the end of your turn you can draw more cards and have more options on your next turn. You can store up to 4 different cards; that is, you can store 1 stone card and a double material card that contains stone, since they are different cards. Cards in the warehouse can be played at any time during the turn. For example, if you need a material card for restoration, you can use it from the warehouse instead of your hand, or if you have an antique card stored, you can take it from the warehouse to the workshop at any time during the turn.

5. RESTORING ANTIQUES. Once you have the antique and its associated master, it's time to start earning prestige points, or not, because you can continue playing with more masters and more antiques. If you decide to restore, you must spend materials from your hand, your storage, or by using the master's or masters' abilities simultaneously, working together, in the amount required for the antique. Remember that you can use coins as a wildcard, as if they were any other material. But be careful, because if you no longer have any antiques in your workshop, all your masters will be discarded.

It can also be a combination of materials and the master's skill.
It can be productive to accumulate more than one table to keep the woodworker busy. Once you've restored the antique, it will move from the workshop to the scoring area on the left side of your workshop. The antique will rotate 90 degrees so you can see the prestige points you've earned.

6. PAY FOR WORK. With this action, you can spend coins from your hand and/or warehouse to pay for exhausted workshop masters or apprentices, restoring their abilities. You can also pay 2 coins for each exhausted workshop master or apprentice belonging to your opponents. In that case, they will go into your hand for you to play later.

7. DISCARD. With this action, you can discard cards from your hand and/or warehouse to your discard pile. This discard pile is called the bin and is located in the upper left corner of your workshop, above the warehouse. These cards are rotated 90º clockwise to distinguish them from the warehouse.

END OF TURN
When you have finished performing your actions, draw enough cards to replenish your hand to 6 cards. Remember the additional card provided by the workshop apprentices. You can take cards one at a time from any deck in the market. It is very important to learn to choose wisely.
The turn passes to the next antique dealer clockwise.

END OF THE GAME AND VICTORY.
The game ends when all antique dealers have passed simultaneously. This usually happens when there is a shortage of a particular deck on the market.
Then, the points of the antiques restored during the game are tallied, the prestige points of the antiques in your workshop (not in your warehouse or hand) are subtracted, and that will be the final score. Whoever has the most prestige points will be the winner and will have the recognition of the other antique dealers.

—description from the designer
family: Organizations: Board Games Association of Creators in Spain (LUDO)

boardgame: Dice Tales: Castle For The King
description:
The king declared your mission is to help workers in construction of the castle. You will need to acquire resources and construct a stronghold one tower at a time. As a thanks for your help quartermaster will help you get stronger.

Castle for the king is an expansion for Dice Tales: Knights Adventure - print & play solo adventure game. This Expansion brings a new location to a game - Castle Grounds, as well as new mechanics.

Gather the knights of the Round Table for one more adventure. Defeat the legendary Black Knight and bring back the Holy Grail in this Expansion for Dice Tales: Knights Adventures. Become a hero of the Arthurian Legend.

This expansion will bring new challenges to your game as well as new ways to gain renown of the greatest knight in the kingdom. It’s time to receive your sword from a lady of the lake and set off on another adventure.

Once per millenia a mythical city, holding incredible artifacts outside of our understanding, appears in the sky above the kingdom. Venture into Cloud Empire to acquire the riches and gain power to become the greatest of knights.

Cloud Empire is an expansion for Dice Tales: Knights Adventure - print & play solo adventure game. This expansion brings a new location to the game with riches that will help you in your adventure.  Fasten your armor, there  are more riches to find.

—description from the publisher

This is an expansion for Beginning Expedition: An Island Exploring Game.
 If you want to explore more islands try it is an expansion for you. Visit Rainforest, Desert, Winter, Silent night maps for more variety between your plays.

—description from the publisher

Prologue is an expansion for Collateral which takes place before the main game.

Prologue takes place in a wild prairie. A place rich in resources and mystery. Its tribe's first steps in an unknown and dangerous place, but at the same time this is perfect for the settlement. Write how your tribe's story began and prepare for the future of these lands! - from publisher

Off to Treasure Island! In this exciting adventure game, little pirates travel to Treasure Island to find the treasures hidden there. Only those who guess the correct colours of the gemstones will get their hands on the treasures. They don't need to be told twice and set off on this adventure together with their parrot Pipo. This game requires memory skills, accurate perception and quantity perception.

—description from the designer

boardgame: The Island of the Screaming Statues
description:
A mysterious tropical island, shrouded in jungle, in which disquieting ruins await to be explored. The secrets of the dread Enotrian pirates, scourge of the seas of Norindaal. Ancient treasures. Supernatural menaces. And four adventurers that were not supposed to be here. Haq, Kil, Gress and Varda are back, and stranded on the Island of the Screaming Statues. It should have been an easy job.

—description from the publisher

Find your perfectly imperfect!

You’re down on luck with love, but don't fret, your Friends are here to help!

Before you entrust them with total control of your dating app, however, you want to make sure they know what you like. Take turns playing as the Dater, trying to find the dating diamond in the rough, or the Friends, trying to guess which of the three Profiles the Dater will find Perfect, Meh, or Nope!

Every time your choice lines up with the Dater's, you score points, and every time it doesn't, you learn something new about each other!

—description from the publisher

100 Pub Quiz Style Questions!

Includes 5 Rounds: Sport, General Knowledge, Music, Entertainment, Picture Round

20 Questions Per Round

—description from the back of the box

boardgame: Echoes of the Dead: A Three Part Adventure for Four Against Darkness
description:
Tasked with rescuing a missing cleric as your first mission, what awaits you in the catacombs is a haunting truth. Surrounded by restless dead, every step inching you closer towards eternal damnation, can you redeem your brave fellow and prove your worth to the clerical Order? Will you journey back from the darkness ... or be consumed by it? Echoes of the Dead is a linear mini-campaign in three sessions, perfect for beginning adventurers.

-description from publisher's website

boardgame: Deadly Competition: An Adventure for All Levels of Play for Four Against Darkness
description:
Occasionally, your group may not be the first to delve into the dungeon. A rival band of adventurers has set their sights on defeating the Final Boss before anyone else. Cooperation is not an option here; it's a race, a perilous one at that. This is competition. Deadly competition. Deadly Competition is a 12-page zine for Four Against Darkness, in PDF, with a separate file for the cover. It prints as a saddle-stitched booklet in a5 format. If you are printing at home, you need European A4-sized paper. Printing instructions are included. This adventure is played like a normal random dungeon, but the final bosses will be other adventurers, not monsters.

-description from publisher's website

Gin Up is a climbing-shedding game for two players inspired by the classic game of Gin Rummy. Players will take turns drawing and discarding until one of the players decides they have a strong enough hand to knock. Once a player has knocked, the game pivots to a climbing-shedding phase, where both players are trying to be the first to shed out.

The game can be played with a standard 52 card deck. Hands of 13 cards are dealt to each player, and one card is turned up from the deck to start a discard. Players alternate drawing a card to their hand, either from the discard or the draw deck, and then choosing to either discard a card or knock. Once a player knocks, there is no more card exchange, and the player who knocked will initiate a climbing-shedding phase.

During the climbing-shedding phase, players will play successively higher combinations of cards (similar to melds in Rummy games), trying to empty their hand of cards before the other player. The player who knocked gets the advantage of going first, but the player who didn't knock gets an advantage too: they may, instead of beating a previous meld, add on to it if they have the appropriate card.

The player who manages to shed all their cards first scores points based on the cards remaining in their opponent's hand.

Players take part in a contest set in a llama-themed world, where speed and sharp observation are the keys to success.

The goal is to be the first to correctly identify shared details between their cards.

Bounce It! is an easy, addictive game in which push your luck meets push your skill.
On your turn, you have a handful of balls that you try to bounce towards a hole in order to win the highest chip you can. Whenever a ball goes in, you may either end your turn and take the current chip, or keep playing and try to win a higher one. However, if your last ball misses, you get nothing, and the next player can try to win the chip you left behind.
The first to reach 42 points wins this game of fast-paced fun.

—description from the publisher

Speedy Dice is a fun and dynamic board game by IMC Toys that tests players' visual speed, coordination, and mental agility. With simple rules easy to learn, it's perfect for family and friends, as it offers quick and entertaining rounds where everyone plays at once.

The goal is to roll the dice and quickly form the figure shown on the card by correctly combining the elements. Speed and observation are key to winning, making each game an exciting and laughter-filled experience.

With 6 different game modes, it allows for varying difficulty levels to suit all ages, ensuring replayability.

—description from the publisher

The complex and rich history of the American West is often explored by writers and filmmakers. From the dime novel Westerns to the latest Spur Award winners, from the silent black-and-white one-reels to the recent mini-series sagas, there seems to be no end to stories set in the West. And that’s just how it’s depicted in fiction. The reason Western stories have always flourished is because the actual history provides such fertile ground from which fictional stories can grow. The West invited people from all over the world to come and reach for the stars, and it rewarded anyone who succeeded on that path.

How the West Was Won puts players into several businesses that were typically present in the American West during the years 1845-1890. You are all young entrepreneurs competing in an economy created to exploit seven different markets. Your goal is to become the richest person in town, and you accomplish this by competing with other players for the local resources and by hiring workers to perform actions that hinder competitors or contribute toward your own wealth. The game’s theme makes it easy to recruit new players, and the short set of rules makes it quick to learn and teach.

Gameplay involves a mix of worker placement and card-driven action. Each player starts with a boss and several workers. You place your workers into jobs of your choosing that are spread across seven separate “markets”: agriculture, freight, gold, wildlife, tribes, whiskey, and law. Each market has two job types, and those job types compete for the same market cards. For example, saloon keepers and temperance leaders compete for the whiskey cards, and farmers and ranchers compete for the agriculture cards. Within each of the 14 job types, workers line up in a queue, with the first worker in line getting activated before the next worker in line.

Every player also receives a hand of market cards each game turn. The market cards activate the workers, one card-play at a time. Everyone’s cards must be played, but there are fewer cards in hand than there are workers in jobs, so not every worker will be activated. The person who plays a card decides which side of the market to activate (for example, farming or ranching). Jockeying for a better position in a job queue increases a worker’s chance of activation when that side of the market is chosen. Furthermore, the cards in your hand may or may not include the markets where you have workers, so you must depend on cards other players play—and when—just as they may benefit from cards you must play.

The sequence of card plays combined with the arrangement of workers in each job line determines who gathers resources or earns money. There are five types of goods in the economy: food, horses, hides, tools, and wood. The more items you gather of a given commodity, the higher price-per-item you get when you sell that good. But diversifying your business across multiple job types improves your end game score. Furthermore, you might use some goods as capital investment when you hire new workers, else you must pay the maximum cost for that investment.

Components:
    One Game Board
    52-Card Market Deck
    7 Character Cards (5.5” x 8.5”)
    60 Worker Meeples
    One Resource Countersheet
    One Rulebook
    One 6-sided Die

-description from publisher's website

The Thunderverse, home of Thunder Alley, Grand Prix, and the wilder little brother Apocalypse Road. Each game has its own rhythm and sensibilities. But each needs specific tracks upon which to compete. Over time these tracks become the comfortable backgrounds in the stories. But eventually, players will seek out and desire new challenges and changing backgrounds. First there was the Thunder Alley track pack, and it introduced dirt racing and a few legendary multi-purpose tracks. Then new tracks were created focused on Grand Prix.

RICHEY RACEWAY - ATHENS, OHIO
A classic oval perfect for Thunder Alley, but with a few chicanes and an infield course designed to challenge Grand Prix drivers as well.

CARLA HORGER MEMORIAL SPEEDWAY - ORLANDO, FLORIDA
Not only is it a grueling road course setup to challenge Grand Prix and Thunder Alley fans, but we snuck in an Apocalypse Road course in the otherwise unused corners of the course. The Carla Horger Memorial Speedway invites everyone to compete.

AUTODROME SIMARD-LALUMIÉRE - ÎLE NOTRE-DAME, QUÉBEC, CANADA
Autodrome Simard-Lalumiére is a primarily Grand Prix course, but it does make a nice alternate for Thunder Alley. We tried to squeeze an Apocalypse Road track into the infield area, but it was not a fun course and not at all an attractive look.

TRIMPERIAL RACEWAY - NEW ORLEANS, LOUISIANA
Trimperial Raceway was designed as an Apocalypse Road track. But the tight footprint allowed us to easily add a Thunder Alley/Grand Prix course around the outside. Sometimes things just work out perfectly.

-description from publisher's website
family: Series: Thunder Alley/Grand Prix engine (GMT Games)

description:
In this competitive exploration game, players lead an expedition into a lost city hidden deep within the jungles of Honduras. The expedition splits into two groups: explorers navigating the jungle on foot and drones mapping the terrain from above.

Players move explorers, collecting jungle tokens as you land on them. But their point values are hidden until the end of the game. When the expedition concludes, token values are revealed based on the final positions of both explorers and drones.

Careful planning and smart positioning throughout the game determine the winner.

—description from the publisher

MEMO5 is a fast-paced, competitive memory card game focused on hidden information, timing, and player interaction.

Each player manages a 2×2 grid of face-down cards and aims to finish each round with the lowest total. Lower “cold” numbers contribute less to a player’s total, while higher “hot” numbers contribute more and may also trigger special effects when activated.

On a turn, players draw, swap, or discard cards and activate abilities that can reveal, replace, or otherwise affect cards in play. Players may also react outside their turn by identifying matching cards or calling out mistakes, further challenging players’ confidence in the information they’re tracking.

The game plays in short rounds, supports up to 10 players, and emphasizes memory, timing, bluffing, and exciting player interactions.

—description from the publisher

Artefacts expands Bretwalda with a mythic dimension. Suits of armour, helmets, swords, and talismans are not mere decorations – they can change the course of history. Each Artefact unlocks unique abilities, giving your Ruler an edge in the fierce contest for the title of Bretwalda. This expansion is not only about adding new strategies, but also about weaving epic storytelling into every decision you make on the battlefield.

Artefacts is a modular expansion for Bretwalda, designed for 1–4 players. It introduces 20 unique Artefact cards and their matching markers, inspired by Celtic, Anglo-Saxon, and Norse myths. Each Artefact grants the controlling Ruler special abilities or bonuses, dramatically shifting the balance of power on the board – from Beowulf’s Armour, which strengthens your leader, to Raedwald’s Helmet, a priceless treasure worth a precious Dalc.

Artefacts can be gained in multiple ways: discovered during play, purchased, or seized as rewards for defeating opponents. Once acquired, they remain with the Ruler until their death – when they may pass into the hands of another. New Season cards further enrich the game with additional events, ensuring that no two campaigns for the throne of Britain play out the same way.

A board game with a thrilling, mysterious atmosphere, set in an isolated medieval village.

Limited communication becomes a tool of manipulation, and survival depends on developing effective methods of information exchange and conscious observation. Every player holds a specific role within the village - but is everyone truly who they claim to be?

One or two psychopaths stalk the village, seeking to eliminate all its inhabitants.
But can you be certain that those who stand with the community today will not turn to madness tomorrow?

Trust is fragile - someone who fought side by side with others for survival may, the very next day, secretly cooperate with a psychopath.
To survive, the villagers must uncover and eliminate all psychopaths.

Observe. Question. Choose your words carefully, so they convey as much meaning as possible with as few words as possible.

When words are few, they matter most.

A peasant, the village head, a judge, a guard, a priest… or perhaps you are the psychopath?
Each role in the village comes with its own responsibilities and unique abilities that influence the course of the game. Even the seemingly “ordinary” peasant has a real impact on the fate of the community.

Thanks to the village board, movement across it, and defined character functions, players gradually immerse themselves in the world of the game, achieving a high level of immersion.

The game is fully designed and complete in concept; however, ongoing testing is still underway, and some elements may be adjusted as we refine the experience.

It is available as a Print & Play edition. The official downloadable version can be found on itch.io.

—description from the publisher

Stalemate. Since the opening of the Dresden Rift, new and unspeakable horrors have poured forth from the heart of the newly emergent Neu Republik. The Allied plan to end hostilities in Europe is in tatters. The world now pays the price of their failure.

Technological development, already in overdrive in the fever of war, has escalated further due to the power of the Rifts. Weapons of unprecedented destructive capacity are being deployed with increasing frequency by all the belligerent nations, leaving Europe in utter ruin and no-one able to gain an upper hand.

It is now beyond any doubt - control of the Rifts is the path to victory. Dresden is the heart of Axis power, the heart of Festung Europa.

Festung Europa is the first in a series of supplements for Konflikt ’47 detailing the background, history, and characters of the Rift War. This volume is centred on the European theatre, and the concerted effort of the British-US alliance, and the Soviet Bloc, to oust control of the Dresden Rift from the Axis.

Within its full-colour pages, rife with glorious photography, artwork, and illustrations, you’ll find timelines, short stories, game scenarios, and more. New miniatures will be released alongside each supplement, representing the heroes, horrors, monsters, and mechs, described in its pages.

Enter a world of dial-up internet, shadowy rooms, and government secrets.

Welcome to Six Eyes, an organization that doesn’t officially exist. It’s 1997 and you’ve been recruited to investigate the impossible: strange vanishings, ticking bombs, and classified conspiracies.

In this narrative-driven roll-and-write game, every choice matters. Will you spend your turn rushing to a crime scene, grilling a witness, or hacking into classified records? You’ll need to balance luck and deduction to seek out the essential clues, avoid red herrings, and crack the case before time runs out!

Each turn, you’ll roll three 6-sided dice, choosing two to keep and use in various ways on your game sheet – will you drive or fly to the next point of interest? Will you use your dice to get a ballistics report, or question a key witness?

As your progress, you’ll read short story passages that’ll reveal the case before you – or send you off on wild goose chases. Gather the right clues to help you fill in your report at the end by completing some multiple-choice sentences based on what you’ve learned about the case. You won’t always have definitive black-and-white answers, but if you’ve spent your time investigating smartly, you should have enough information to fill in the blanks.

Then you can get back to figuring out exactly what this shadowy agency you work for actually is…

– Balances luck, strategy and deduction
– Combines roll-and-write with choose-your-own-path
– Each case is different, introducing different gameplay mechanics and scenarios – from mysterious disappearances to wild conspiracies to ticking time bombs!

Season 1 comes with 5 cases for you to play. After you finish a case you can either play it again more efficiently with the knowledge you now have (and try and beat your high score), or move onto the next case.

-description from designer

You play as a heroic adventurer who has narrowly defeated a powerful and malevolent mage after a long and grueling battle. However, with his dying breath, the mage casts a final, sinister spell, warping reality around you. The world blurs and distorts, and you find yourself transported to a vast and desolate sandy arena, an ancient battleground soaked in the blood of countless warriors.

As you regain your senses, you realize that this arena is not just a barren wasteland, but a twisted creation of the dying mage's curse. The arena is alive with danger, constantly shifting and bringing forth increasingly powerful foes, each more formidable than the last. The mage, even in death, continues to torment you, conjuring these relentless enemies from the sands themselves.
